title: Chāndogya Upaniṣad
titleEnglish: Tat tvam asi — That thou art

verses.dev: ओमित्येतदक्षरमुद्गीथमुपासीत । ओमिति ह्युद्गायति तस्योपव्याख्यानम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: omityetadakṣaramudgīthamupāsīta | omiti hyudgāyati tasyopavyākhyānam || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the syllable 'OM' as the Udgītha, for one sings the loud chant beginning with OM. Of this syllable, the close explanation follows.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that 'OM' (*Omkāra*) is the most intimate and direct phonetic symbol of the Supreme Brahman (*Parama-Brahman*). In the context of the Sāmavedic rituals, it is called the *Udgītha* because it is chanted aloud by the Udgātṛ priest. Śaṅkara emphasizes that this text bridges ritual action (*Karma*) with spiritual meditation (*Upāsanā*). By meditating on OM not just as a sound, but as the supreme, unattached substrate of reality, the mind is purified and turned away from objective phenomena toward the non-dual Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja (following Rāmānuja's methodology) interprets 'OM' as the primary name of the Supreme Lord, Śrīman Nārāyaṇa. The syllable designates Him who is the container of all sentient (*Cit*) and insentient (*Acit*) beings. Meditating on OM as the Udgītha is an injunction for loving, structured meditation (*Bhakti-rūpāpannā Upāsanā*). The verse commands the seeker to realize that the Lord is the ultimate goal and the source of all sacrificial rewards.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya asserts that the word 'Akṣara' here does not merely mean an alphabet or syllable, but 'The Imperishable One'—Lord Viṣṇu. OM represents His infinite, independent, and flawless attributes. Madhva highlights a strict hierarchy: the sound OM is the dependent acoustic vehicle, while the independent reality it represents is Viṣṇu Himself. Meditating on Him as the Udgītha means recognizing Him as the prime mover of all vital energies and cosmic activities.

verses.dev: एषां भूतानां पृथिवी रसः पृथिव्या आपो रसोऽपामोषधयो रस ओषधीनां पुरुषो रसः पुरुषस्य वाग्रसो वाच ऋग्रस ऋचः साम रसः साम्न उद्गीथो रसः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: eṣāṃ bhūtānāṃ pṛthivī rasaḥ pṛthivyā āpo raso'pāmoṣadhayo rasa oṣadhīnāṃ puruṣo rasaḥ puruṣasya vāgraso vāca ṛgrasa ṛcaḥ sāma rasaḥ sāmna udgītho rasa || 2 ||
verses.trans: The earth is the essence of all these beings; water is the essence of the earth; plants are the essence of water; man is the essence of plants; speech is the essence of man; the Rigveda is the essence of speech; the Sāmaveda is the essence of the Rigveda; and the Udgītha (OM) is the essence of the Sāmaveda.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks this ascending scale of refinement to show the absolute prioritization of consciousness. Earth supports physical life; water vitalizes earth; plants sustain man. Within man, speech is the highest output; within speech, the wisdom of the Rigveda; within the Rigveda, the musical harmony of the Sāmaveda; and within the Sāmaveda, the syllable OM. Thus, OM is the *Rasa-tama*—the ultimate essence of all essences, the final distillation of the cosmos into a single point of pure conscious awareness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains this text as a structural map of the Lord's cosmic body (*Vibhūti*). The progressive refinement demonstrates how the Lord systematically structures creation, ensuring each layer serves as the essence and support of the lower one. The ultimate essence being OM proves that the entire material universe is designed to find its final meaning and resting place in the praise of the Supreme Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets 'Rasa' as 'the source of strength and controller.' The earth controls creatures, water controls earth, and so forth, in a strict cosmic hierarchy managed by specific presiding deities (*Abhimāni Devatās*). The ultimate controller of the entire sequence is the deity of the Udgītha, who is entirely dependent on and subordinate to Lord Viṣṇu, the absolute, independent Essence of all things.

verses.dev: स एष रसानां रसतमः परमः परार्ध्योऽष्टमो यदुद्गीथः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣa rasānāṃ rasatamaḥ paramaḥ parārdhyo'ṣṭamo yadudgīthaḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: That Udgītha (OM) is the ultimate essence of essences, the supreme, the highest, deserving the highest place, the eighth in the sequence.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The term *Parārdhya* means deserving the absolute highest place. Shankara emphasizes that because OM is the eighth step in the cosmic ladder of essence, it represents the transcendence of the seven worldly layers. It stands for the pure Self (*Atman*) which is unattached to the material elements, and meditating upon it allows the seeker to transcend relative nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The description highlights the supreme majesty of the name of God. Being 'the highest' signifies that meditation on OM transcends all ordinary ritual actions. It is the spiritual crown of all Sāmavedic worship, designed to connect the soul directly to the Lord's transcendent form.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes the word *Paramaḥ* (Supreme). It refers to the independent Lord who dwells within the eighth factor (Udgītha). While the previous seven items are material or cosmic elements, the eighth points directly to the transcendent nature of Viṣṇu, who surpasses all material categories.

verses.dev: कतमा कतमा ऋक् कतमत् कतमत् साम कतमः कतम उद्गीथ इति विमृष्टं भवति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: katamā katamā ṛk katamat katamat sāma katamaḥ katama udgītha iti vimṛṣṭaṃ bhavati || 4 ||
verses.trans: What is the Rigveda? What is the Sāmaveda? What is the Udgītha? This is now being deeply considered.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that this verse sets up an inner philosophical inquiry (*Vīmāṃsā*). The repetition ('Which? Which?') indicates that this is not a casual question, but an exhaustive analysis meant to shake the student out of mechanical ritualism and push them toward deep internal reflection.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The inquiry is meant to clarify the relationship between the ritual text, the musical chant, and the divine target. It shows that true worship cannot be mindless execution; it requires a clear, conceptual understanding of the sacred structures ordained by scripture.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this inquiry is aimed at identifying the specific presiding deities governing each layer of sacred sound, ensuring that the seeker understands the exact spiritual hierarchy and does not confuse a subordinate cosmic power with the Supreme Independent Lord.

verses.dev: वागेवर्क्प्राणः सामोमित्येतदक्षरमुद्गीथस्तद्वा एतन्मिथुनं यद्वाक्च प्राणश्चर्च सा म च ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: vāgevarkprāṇaḥ sāmōmityetadakṣaramudgīthastadvā etanmithunaṃ yadvākca prāṇaścarca sā ma ca || 5 ||
verses.trans: Speech is indeed the Rigveda; the vital breath (Prāṇa) is the Sāmaveda; the syllable OM is the Udgītha. This forms a divine couple—namely, Speech and Breath, the Rik and the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara unifies the macrocosm with human physiology. Speech (*Vāk*) is the source of the written verse (*Rik*), and the vital internal breath (*Prāṇa*) is the driver of the musical chant (*Sāman*). They form a sacred pair (*Mithuna*) inside every human being. When speech and breath join to utter OM, they merge their energies back into the single source of life—pure Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The union of Speech and Breath represents the cooperative design of the human vehicle by the Lord. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this biological pair is meant to be dedicated to the service of the Lord through scriptural recitation, showing that our physical functions are built specifically for divine devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva identifies the underlying deities: *Vāk* is governed by Agni and Sarasvatī, while *Prāṇa* is governed by Mukhya-Prāṇa (the chief life-force deity). Their union represents the cosmic cooperation of these deities under the sovereign command of Viṣṇu, who uses their unified action to manifest sacred Vedic knowledge.

verses.dev: देवासुरा ह वै यत्र संयेतिरे उभये प्राजापत्यास्तद्ध देवा उद्गीथमाजह्रुरनेनैनानभिभविष्याम इति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: devāsurā ha vai yatra saṃyetire ubhaye prājāpatyāstaddha devā udgīthamājahruranenainānabhibhaviṣyāma iti || 1 ||
verses.trans: When the Devas (gods) and Asuras (demons), both offspring of Prajāpati, fought against each other, the Devas took hold of the Udgītha (OM), thinking: 'With this chant, we shall overcome the Asuras.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara interprets this battle allegorically. Both Devas and Asuras are 'offspring of Prajāpati' because they represent the two opposing tendencies inside the human mind: the Devas are our virtuous impulses illuminated by scriptural wisdom, while the Asuras are our selfish, sensory desires driven by ignorance. The battle is internal. To defeat our darker impulses, we must take refuge in the meditation of the Udgītha (OM).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This is a historic and cosmic event used to illustrate spiritual truth. The Devas represent the forces of righteousness (*Dharma*) who seek shelter in the supreme name of God to defeat the forces of chaos and sin (*Adharma*), proving that victory over material temptation is only possible through divine refuge.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that the Devas and Asuras are real, distinct groups of souls with fixed, eternal natures (*Sāttvika* and *Tāmasa*). The Devas are naturally loyal to Viṣṇu and gain strength by taking refuge in His sacred acoustic symbol, while the Asuras are defined by their eternal hatred and opposition to the divine order.

verses.dev: ते ह नासिक्यं प्राणमुद्गीथमुपासांचक्रिरे तं हासुरा पाप्मना विविधुस्तस्मात्तेनोभयं जिघ्रति सुरभि च दुर्गन्धि च पाप्मना ह्येष विद्धः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: te ha nāsikyaṃ prāṇamudgīthamupāsāṃcakrire taṃ hāsurā pāpmanā vividhustasmāttenōbhayaṃ jighrati surabhi ca durgandhi ca pāpmanā hyeṣa viddhaḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: Then the Devas meditated on the Udgītha as the vital breath in the nose. But the Asuras pierced that breath with evil. Therefore, with the nose, one smells both what is pleasant and what is foul, because it has been pierced by evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that the Devas tried to limit their meditation to the physical sense of smell located in the nostrils (*Nāsikya Prāṇa*). Because it is a localized sensory organ, it is vulnerable to attachment and repulsion. The Asuras 'pierced' it with evil, meaning they introduced duality—causing us to love sweet smells and hate foul smells. This shows that any meditation restricted to a physical sensory organ remains bound by ego and worldly corruption.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The physical sense of smell is vulnerable to the impurities of material nature (*Prakriti*). The school notes that because the sensory faculties can be misdirected toward selfish gratification, they are easily tainted by sin, resulting in the mixed experiences of pleasure and disgust found in earthly life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that the minor deity presiding over the sense of smell was overwhelmed by the demonic forces of darkness because of its limited cosmic rank. This demonstrates that minor deities cannot provide absolute protection against spiritual corruption; they are dependent powers that can be destabilized.

verses.dev: अथ ह वाचमुद्गीथमुपासांचक्रिरे तां हासुरा पाप्मना विविधुस्तस्मात्तेनोभयं वदति सत्यं चानृतं च पाप्मना ह्येषा विद्धा ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha ha vācamudgīthamupāsāṃcakrire tāṃ hāsurā pāpmanā vividhustasmāttenobhayaṃ vadati satyaṃ cānṛtaṃ ca pāpmanā hyeṣā viddhā || 3 ||
verses.trans: Then the Devas meditated on the Udgītha as speech. But the Asuras pierced speech with evil. Therefore, with speech, one speaks both truth and falsehood, because it has been pierced by evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Speech can be used to chant holy mantras, but it can also be used to lie, insult, and deceive. Shankara points out that because the physical organ of speech is driven by individual desires, it was corrupted by the Asuras. It remains dualistic and cannot serve as the untainted foundation of absolute meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Speech is a material instrument that can be turned toward virtue or vice. When human intent is corrupted by material desires, speech becomes an instrument of falsehood (*Anṛtam*), losing its natural, divine orientation toward truth.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The deity governing speech (*Vāk*) was assaulted and partially corrupted by the Asuras, allowing lie and deception to enter human communication. This proves that no individual mental or physical faculty is completely immune to material degradation on its own.

verses.dev: अथ ह चक्षुरुद्गीथमुपासांचक्रिरे तद्धासुरा पाप्मना विविधुस्तस्मात्तेनोभयं पश्यति दर्शनीयं चादर्शनीयं च पाप्मना ह्येतद्विद्धम् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha ha cakṣurudgīthamupāsāṃcakrire taddhāsurā pāpmanā vividhustasmāttenōbhayaṃ paśyati darśanīyaṃ cādarśanīyaṃ ca pāpmanā hyetadviddham || 4 ||
verses.trans: Then the Devas meditated on the Udgītha as the eye (sight). But the Asuras pierced the eye with evil. Therefore, with the eye, one sees both what is beautiful and what is ugly, because it has been pierced by evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Sight is pulled toward attractive forms and repulsed by offensive ones. Shankara emphasizes that as long as our meditation is tied to objective seeing, our mind remains caught in the trap of likes and dislikes (*Rāga-Dveṣa*), which is the definition of the Asuric infection.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The eye registers physical modifications of matter. Because the mind interprets these changes through personal ego, it creates the binary categories of material beauty and ugliness, separating the soul from seeing the single divine presence pervading everything.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the solar deity (*Sūrya*) presiding over individual human eyes allows for flawed perceptions when the human soul falls under the influence of dark karma, showing that sensory observation is fundamentally unstable.

verses.dev: अथ ह श्रोत्रमुद्गीथमुपासांचक्रिरे तद्धासुरा पाप्मना विविधुस्तस्मात्तेनोभयं शृणोति श्रवणीयं चाश्रवणीयं च पाप्मना ह्येतद्विद्धम् ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha ha śrotramudgīthamupāsāṃcakrire taddhāsurā pāpmanā vividhustasmāttenōbhayaṃ śṛṇoti śravaṇīyaṃ cāśravaṇīyaṃ ca pāpmanā hyetadviddham || 5 ||
verses.trans: Then the Devas meditated on the Udgītha as the ear (hearing). But the Asuras pierced the ear with evil. Therefore, with the ear, one hears both what is pleasant to hear and what is unpleasant, because it has been pierced by evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The ear listens to praise with delight and to criticism with anger. Shankara states that this vulnerability shows the ear is an impure center for absolute meditation, as its function is continually disturbed by external conditions.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Hearing gets trapped by material sounds, gossip, and wordly attachments. This corruption prevents the soul from listening to the eternal spiritual sound of the scriptures, diverting its focus away from God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The deities ruling the quarters of space (*Digdevatās*), acting within the human ear, cannot block out negative acoustic influences due to the presence of cosmic evil, reinforcing the need for a higher, unassailable power.

verses.dev: अथ ह मन उद्गीथमुपासांचक्रिरे तद्धासुरा पाप्मना विविधुस्तस्मात्तेनोभयं सङ्कल्पयति सङ्कल्पनीयं चासङ्कल्पनीयं च पाप्मना ह्येतद्विद्धम् ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: atha ha mana udgīthamupāsāṃcakrire taddhāsurā pāpmanā vividhustasmāttenōbhayaṃ saṅkalpayati saṅkalpanīyaṃ cāsaṅkalpanīyaṃ ca pāpmanā hyetadviddham || 6 ||
verses.trans: Then the Devas meditated on the Udgītha as the mind. But the Asuras pierced the mind with evil. Therefore, with the mind, one imagines both proper thoughts and improper thoughts, because it has been pierced by evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The mind (*Manas*) is the clearinghouse of all desires and fantasies. Shankara points out that the mind is notoriously unstable, jumping from pure intentions to dark, destructive fantasies. Because it is highly volatile, it was easily breached by the Asuras, making it an unreliable foundation for steady realization unless it is completely anchored in the higher Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The mind is the source of attachments (*Saṅkalpa*). Raṅgarāmānuja notes that when the mind is left uncontrolled, it becomes flooded with inappropriate material desires, turning it into a tool of bondage instead of a pathway to liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The moon deity (*Candra*), who rules the human mind, is subject to fluctuations and dark periods. Madhva asserts that this mental instability proves that the individual mind cannot save itself; it must be completely subordinated to a force that cannot be touched by darkness.

verses.dev: अथ ह य एवायं मुख्यः प्राणस्तमुद्गीथमुपासांचक्रिरे तं हासुरा ऋत्वा व्यानशुर्यथा अश्मानमखणमृत्वा लोष्टो विध्वंसेतैवम् ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: atha ha ya evāyaṃ mukhyaḥ prāṇastamudgīthamupāsāṃcakrire taṃ hāsurā ṛtvā vyānaśuryathā aśmānamakhaṇamṛtvā loṣṭo vidhvaṃsetaivam || 7 ||
verses.trans: Then the Devas meditated on the Udgītha as the chief vital life-force inside the body (Mukhya Prāṇa). When the Asuras rushed at it to pierce it with evil, they were completely shattered and destroyed, just as a clod of earth is smashed to pieces when struck against a solid, unyielding rock.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A magnificent spiritual climax. Shankara explains that the 'chief vital breath' (*Mukhya Prāṇa*) does not refer to the physical act of breathing or any specific sensory organ. It represents the primal, unattached life-force itself, which animates the body without seeking any private sensory pleasure. It has no personal likes or dislikes; it functions with absolute equality toward all. Because it is completely free from individual desires, the Asuras (evil tendencies) could find no foothold in it. The moment their selfish energy struck this pure, objective life-force, they were instantly obliterated. Meditating on this pure, unattached substrate represents the step toward realizing the non-dual Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The *Mukhya Prāṇa* is the untainted instrument of the Lord inside the body. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that while the five senses are easily corrupted by worldly objects, the central life-force remains pure and dedicated exclusively to keeping the temple of the body alive. It represents the perfect servant of God, totally immune to demonic corruption, serving as an ideal model for the soul's surrender.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: This is a foundational verse for the Dvaita school. Madhvācārya declares that *Mukhya Prāṇa* is the highest of all dependent entities, the supreme intermediary soul (*Jīvottama*). He is absolutely flawless and immune to sin (*Pāpma-aspreṣṭa*). The Asuras, representing the dark forces of ignorance, are completely shattered when they challenge his authority. Madhva asserts that salvation cannot be reached directly; a soul must go through Mukhya Prāṇa, who acts as the ultimate spiritual master and path to Lord Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: तमु ह एतमुद्गीथमुपासीतोष्णं वा एतमुद्गीथमुपासीत। य एवासौ तपति तमुद्गीथमुपासीत यो वै सोऽसौ स एवोष्णस्तस्मादेतमुद्गीथमुपासीत ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tamu ha etamudgīthamupāsītoṣṇaṃ vā etamudgīthamupāsīta | ya evāsau tapati tamudgīthamupāsīta yo vai so'sau sa evoṣṇastasmādetamudgīthamupāsīta || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on this very Udgītha as that which gives out heat. One should meditate on the Udgītha as yonder Sun that shines. Truly, he is warm, and this chief vital breath is also warm. Therefore, one should meditate on both as the Udgītha.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this verse initiates an upāsanā (meditation) based on identity of attributes (*guṇa-sāmānya*). The individual life-force (*Mukhya Prāṇa*) inside the body is warm, and the cosmic Sun (*Aditya*) in the heavens is also warm. The attribute of heat (*uṣṇatva*) unifies the macrocosm and the microcosm. By meditating on this common denominator, the seeker expands their individual consciousness into cosmic consciousness, realizing that the light animating the body is identical to the light illuminating the universe.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja interprets the heat and light of both the Sun and the Prāṇa as manifestations of the divine energy (*Vibhūti*) of the Supreme Lord. The Sun is not merely a physical sphere, but a cosmic eye of Nārāyaṇa, while Prāṇa is His internal vehicle. The meditation acts as a way to view both the internal life-force and the external universe as functional limbs of the single, sovereign cosmic body of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the word 'sau' (yonder Sun) refers to the deity Sūrya, who is powered by an indwelling form of Lord Viṣṇu. Similarly, the heat in the body is driven by Viṣṇu acting through Mukhya Prāṇa. The meditation emphasizes the dependent nature of both the solar deity and the vital breath, directing the soul's focus past the physical elements to the Independent Lord who warms them both.

verses.dev: नमेवमनेन चामुना चोभौ समानौ। अयं च सो चोष्णः। अयं च सो च स्वर इतीममाहुः। स्वर इति प्रत्यास्वर इत्यमुं तस्माद्वा एतमुद्गीथमुपासीत ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: namevamanena cāmunā cobhau samānau | ayaṃ ca so coṣṇaḥ | ayaṃ ca so ca svara itīmamāhuḥ | svara iti pratyāsvara ityamuṃ tasmādvā etamudgīthamupāsīta || 2 ||
verses.trans: One should realize: 'This (Prāṇa) and That (Sun) are exactly the same.' This is warm, and That is warm. This is called sound (Svara), and That is called moving sound or reflection (Pratyāsvara). Therefore, one should meditate on this and that as the Udgītha.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the linguistic and functional parallels. Prāṇa is called *Svara* because it produces vocal sound and is self-manifested in the body. The Sun is called *Pratyāsvara* because it sets and returns day after day, acting like a recurring reflection of light. The underlying reality behind both the sound of life and the light of heaven is the non-dual Brahman. This meditation breaks down the artificial barrier between 'inside' and 'outside'.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The equality (*samānatva*) mentioned here does not mean absolute ontological identity, but a perfect functional harmony designed by the Lord. Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the synchronization of internal life rhythms (*Svara*) with external solar movements (*Pratyāsvara*) is proof of a singular, divine cosmic order controlled by the Supreme Ruler.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that *Svara* and *Pratyāsvara* are names of specific divine forms of Lord Viṣṇu residing within the Prāṇa and the Sun respectively. The meditation is intended to show that the identical Supreme Power operates seamlessly across different planes of creation, ensuring that the seeker respects the unified hierarchy of the universe.

verses.dev: अथ खलु व्यानमेवोद्गीथमुपासीत यद्वै प्राणिति स प्राणो यदपानिति सोऽपानः। अथ यः प्राणापानयोः सन्धिः स व्यानो यो व्यानः सा वाक्तस्मादप्राणन्ननपानन्वाचमभिव्याहरति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalu vyānamevodgīthamupāsīta yadvai prāṇiti sa prāṇo yadapāniti so'pānaḥ | atha yaḥ prāṇāpānayoḥ sandhiḥ sa vyāno yo vyānaḥ sā vāktasmādaprāṇannanapānandvācamabhivyāharati || 3 ||
verses.trans: Now, indeed, one should meditate on Vyāna as the Udgītha. That which one breathes out is Prāṇa; that which one breathes in is Apāna. The junction of Prāṇa and Apāna is Vyāna. This Vyāna is speech. Therefore, when a person speaks, they neither breathe out nor breathe in; they speak while holding both.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides a profound physiological and psychological analysis of the breath. *Prāṇa* is the outward breath; *Apāna* is the inward breath. The quiet interval or gap where these two currents meet is *Vyāna*, the pervasive vital force. Speech requires a person to hold their breath—meaning all speech happens exclusively within the quiet space of Vyāna. By focusing on Vyāna during the Udgītha, the practitioner is actually meditating on the still, unmoving pause between actions, which points directly to the silent witness, the Ātman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights the precision of the body's internal engineering. Vyāna is an specialized energy stream supplied by the Lord to sustain difficult, coordinated physical tasks like speech and sacred chanting. Meditating on Vyāna helps the devotee realize that even the silent, physical transitions of the body are powered by a higher grace meant for divine praise.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva views Prāṇa, Apāna, and Vyāna as distinct sub-deities working under the leadership of Mukhya Prāṇa. Vyāna holds a unique position because it acts as the bridge (*sandhi*). Meditating on Vyāna means worshiping the specific coordinating aspect of the life-force, acknowledging that all communicative power belongs ultimately to the Lord dwelling inside the breath.

verses.dev: या वाक्सर्क् तस्मादप्राणन्ननपानन्नृचमभिव्याहरति यर्क् तत्साम तस्मादप्राणन्ननपानन्साम गायति यत्साम स उद्गीथस्तस्मादप्राणन्ननपानन्नुद्गायति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yā vāksark tasmādaprāṇannanapānannṛcamabhivyāharati yark tatsāma tasmādaprāṇannanapānansāma gāyati yatsāma sa udgīthastasmādaprāṇannanapānannudgāyati || 4 ||
verses.trans: What is speech is the Rik (verse). Therefore, one recites the Rik without breathing out or breathing in. What is Rik is the Sāman (chant). Therefore, one sings the Sāman without breathing out or breathing in. What is Sāman is the Udgītha (OM). Therefore, one chants the Udgītha without breathing out or breathing in.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara demonstrates that the entire Vedic liturgical framework (Rik, Sāman, Udgītha) is entirely sustained by this internal, non-breathing state of *Vyāna*. Because sacred chanting requires the suspension of normal inhalation and exhalation, it mimics the process of *Prāṇāyāma* (breath control). Thus, every vocal ritual action is rooted in an effortless internal stillness, revealing that ritualism is secretly an inner meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This verse reinforces the idea that scriptural worship is an intense, single-pointed physical and mental offering. The suspension of normal breathing during the loud chant shows that the physical organs must be completely controlled and bound together in absolute concentration when addressing the Supreme Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes the hierarchical chain of dependencies: Speech supports the Rik, Rik supports the Sāman, and Sāman supports the Udgītha. Since all of these operations happen through Vyāna, it proves that the presiding deity of Vyāna is a major cosmic facilitator of Vedic wisdom, acting on behalf of Lord Nārāyaṇa.

verses.dev: अतो यान्यान्यन्यानि वीर्यवन्ति कर्माणि यथाग्नेर्मन्थनं आजेः सरणं दृढस्य धनुष आयमनमप्राणन्ननपानंस्तानि करोत्यतस्य हेतोरव्यानमेवोद्गीथमुपासीत ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: ato yānyānyanyāni vīryavanti karmāṇi yathāgnermanthanaṃ ājeḥ saraṇaṃ dṛḍhasya dhanuṣa āyamanamaprāṇannanapānaṃstāni karotyatasya hetoravyānamevodgīth0hupāsīta || 5 ||
verses.trans: Therefore, any other actions that require exceptional strength—such as rubbing two sticks together to strike fire, running a race, or bending a stiff, heavy bow—are all performed without breathing out or breathing in. For this reason, one should meditate on Vyāna as the Udgītha.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara uses everyday, physical examples to make the spiritual point accessible. When a person performs a feat of immense strength, they automatically hold their breath. This internal concentration of energy (*vīrya*) is the natural expression of *Vyāna*. By linking the cosmic Udgītha to this concentrated pocket of power, the text instructs the seeker to locate the core of spiritual strength not in superficial physical movements, but in the deep, concentrated stillness within.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that worldly acts of power confirm the sacred nature of Vyāna. If material success requires the focused holding of energy, spiritual liberation requires an even deeper concentration of the soul's faculties. The physical exertion of holding the breath is a natural signpost pointing toward the internal effort needed for *Bhakti*.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that all physical strength (*Bala*) in the universe belongs originally to Lord Viṣṇu, who manifests it in the living world through Mukhya Prāṇa (often called Prāṇa-Bala). The acts of striking fire, racing, or pulling a bow show how this divine energy is lent to human beings through the sub-deity Vyāna.

verses.dev: ओमित्येतदक्षरमुद्गीथमुपासीतोमिति ह्युद्गायति तस्योपव्याख्यानम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: omityetadakṣaramudgīthamupāsīta | omiti hyudgāyati tasyopavyākhyānam || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the syllable 'OM' as the Udgītha, for one sings the loud chant beginning with OM. Of this syllable, the close explanation continues.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this repetition from the very first verse of the Upanishad is not redundant; it serves to introduce a completely new layer of meditation (*upāsanā-antara*). Having previously established OM through its cosmic relations (the Sun) and physiological properties (Vyāna), the text now shifts to explaining how the meditation on OM grants absolute fearlessness and immortality by detaching the seeker from the domain of ritual action.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the re-introduction emphasizes the absolute centrality of this name of God. It ensures that while the student learns various secondary properties of meditation, their primary focus remains anchored on the syllable OM as the direct vehicle for connecting with the Supreme Person (*Purūṣottamā*).
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the repetition signals a shift in the hierarchy of meditation. The text is moving from the worship of the external limbs of ritual toward a deeper structural understanding of the immutable Lord (*Akṣara*) who lives within the phonetic sound of the Udgītha.

verses.dev: देवा वै मृत्योर्बिभ्यतस्त्रयीं विद्यां प्राविशंस्ते छन्दोभिरच्छादयन्यदेभिरच्छादयंस्तच्छन्दसां छन्दस्त्वम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: devā vai mṛtyorbibhyatastrayīṃ vidyāṃ prāviśaṃste chandobhiracchādayanyadebhiracchādayanstacchandasāṃ chandastvam || 2 ||
verses.trans: The Devas (gods), fearing death, entered upon the threefold knowledge (the three Vedas). They covered themselves with the sacred meters (Chandas). Because they covered themselves with these, the meters are called 'Chandas'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara unpacks the profound etymology of *Chandas* (meters) from the root *chad*, meaning 'to cover' or 'to shield'. The Devas represents souls who are afraid of mortality and change. They took refuge in the mechanical performance of Vedic sacrifices (*Trayī Vidyā*), wrapping themselves in the technical perfection of poetic meters to escape death. However, Shankara points out that ritual action can only provide temporary protection; it masks mortality but does not cure it.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that the Devas sought shelter in the liturgical actions ordained by the Lord in the early portions of the Vedas. The sacred meters act as a defensive armor against the karmic forces of decay, showing that scriptural discipline is the first, necessary layer of defense against spiritual degradation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the 'covering' as a layer of divine protection granted by the specific deities who reside over the Vedic meters. The Devas sought refuge in these subordinate cosmic structures to escape the influence of the death-deity, but this protection remains relative and conditional within the material universe.

verses.dev: तानु तिथे मृत्युर्मत्स्यमिवोदके परिपश्येदेवमृचि साम्नि यजुषि परिपश्यत्युत्थाय ते त्रय्या विद्यायाः स्वरमेव प्राविशन् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tānu tithe mṛtyurmatsyamivodake paripaśyedevamṛci sāmni yajuṣi paripaśyatyutthāya te trayyā vidyāyāḥ svarameva prāviṣan || 3 ||
verses.trans: Just as a fisherman might spot a fish in shallow water, Death saw the Devas hidden within the Rigveda, the Sāmaveda, and the Yajurveda. Discovering this, the Devas rose above the threefold knowledge and entered straight into Sound (Svara) — into OM itself.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A dramatic metaphor. Shankara writes that Death can easily catch anyone whose spiritual practice is limited to external rituals, just like a fisherman nets a fish through clear water. Rituals (*Karma*) belong to the world of names, forms, and results — which means they are inherently finite. Realizing that the three Vedas could not save them from cosmic time, the Devas dropped the external mechanics and entered *Svara* (OM). In Advaita, moving from the three Vedas to OM means transitioning from transactional ritualism to non-dual, meditative absorption.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that external works without deep internal devotion are transparent to the forces of worldly bondage. The Devas had to shift their awareness from the external, sacrificial formulas (*Karma-Kāṇḍa*) directly into the inner sonic nucleus of the scriptures — the sacred name OM — which connects the soul directly to the immortal protection of Nārāyaṇa.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that Death (*Mṛtyu*) has authority over all entities bound to material actions and outer forms. When the Devas rose above the external text and entered *Svara*, they were taking refuge in the Supreme Controller who commands Death. OM is the innermost chamber of divine power where the limitations of time and matter cannot enter.

verses.dev: यदा वा ऋचमाप्नोत्योमित्येवातिस्वरत्येवं सामैवं यजुरेष उ स्वरो यदेतदक्षरमेतदमृतमभयं तत्प्रविश्य देवा अमृता अभया अभवन् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yadā vā ṛcamāpnotyōmityevātisvaratyevaṃ sāmaivaṃ yajureṣa u svaro yadetadakṣarametadamṛtamabhayaṃ tatpraviśya devā amṛtā abhayā abhavan || 4 ||
verses.trans: When a person recites a verse of the Rigveda, they finish by sounding 'OM'; so do they with the Sāmaveda and the Yajurveda. This OM is that Sound; it is the Imperishable, the Immortal, the Fearless. By entering into it, the Devas became immortal and fearless.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara highlights that OM is the common seal and termination of all scriptural speech. The words 'entering into it' (*tat-praviśya*) are critical for Advaita: it means the complete dissolution of individual identity into the sound-less reality behind OM. By meditating on OM as the unshakeable substrate of the universe, the seeker loses their egoic vulnerability. Since fear only arises from a sense of separate duality, realizing oneness with this imperishable syllable brings absolute fearlessness (*Abhayam*).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: To enter OM means to align the soul's total will with the divine name, achieving close communion with the Lord. Raṅgarāmānuja explains that immortality (*Amṛtatvam*) and fearlessness are qualities belonging to the divine realm (*Vaikuṇṭha*), which are granted to the soul the moment it abandons reliance on its own egoic efforts and surrenders completely to the divine name.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva maintains that 'entering' means obtaining deep conceptual knowledge of and steady devotion to the Lord within OM. The Devas did not blend into or dissolve their individual identities; rather, they found an unshakeable safe haven. By recognizing Lord Viṣṇu as the source of all Vedic sounds, they were saved from the cycle of birth and death by His absolute, independent grace.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवं विद्वानक्षरं प्रणौत्येतदेवाक्षरममृतमभयं प्रविशति तत्प्रविश्य यदमृता देवास्तदमृतो भवति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etadevaṃ vidvānakṣaraṃ praṇōtyetadēvākṣaramamṛtamabhayaṃ praviśati tatpraviśya yadamṛtā devāstadamṛto bhavati || 5 ||
verses.trans: He who, knowing this thus, praises this Imperishable Syllable, enters this very same Immortal and Fearless sound. Entering it, he becomes immortal, just as the Devas became immortal.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara concludes by showing that spiritual liberation is completely democratic: it is not reserved for ancient cosmic gods. Anyone who possesses the exact same internal realization (*etad evaṃ vidvān*) can achieve the exact same state of immortality. The knowledge of the real nature of OM acts as the flame that burns away the illusion of death, turning a mortal human being into the deathless Brahman here and now.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The verse guarantees that the path of divine meditation is open to any sincere seeker. True knowledge is not book learning; it is the deep, experiential realization that the sacred syllable is the home of the Lord. The reward of this continuous meditation is eternal service in the divine presence, free from the anxieties of the material world.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the phrase 'knowing this thus' proves that mere mechanical chanting of OM yields limited results. True spiritual efficacy requires a clear, systematic understanding of the cosmic hierarchies and the supreme independence of Viṣṇu. When a human soul gains this insight, they are granted the same status of liberation (*Mukti*) enjoyed by the higher Devas.

verses.dev: अथ खलु य उद्गीथः स प्रणवो यः प्रणवः स उद्गीथ इति असौ वा आदित्य उद्गीथ एष प्रणव ओमिति ह्येष स्वरन्नेति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalu ya udgīthaḥ sa praṇavo yaḥ praṇavaḥ sa udgītha iti asau vā āditya udgītha eṣa praṇava omiti hyeṣa svaranneti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, verily, that which is Udgītha is Pranava (OM), and that which is Pranava is Udgītha. Yonder Sun is indeed the Udgītha, and He is also the Pranava, for He moves along chanting 'OM'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this verse unifies two distinct Vedic traditions. The Rigveda uses the term *Pranava* to denote the sacred syllable OM, while the Sāmaveda calls it the *Udgītha*. By declaring them absolutely identical, the Upanishad removes any sense of division between the sacrificial paths. Furthermore, the text identifies this phonetic sound with the celestial Sun (*Āditya*). The Sun's daily journey across the sky is allegorized as a continuous acoustic projection of OM, proving that the universe is structurally vibrating with pure conscious energy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the complete oneness (*ekatva*) of Udgītha and Pranava shows that different scriptural names point to the same ultimate destination. The Sun moving along and 'chanting OM' signifies that the cosmic bodies are completely subservient to the dynamic will of the Supreme Lord, performing their daily duties as an act of natural worship to Nārāyaṇa.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that *Udgītha* and *Pranava* are two distinct, functional names of the Lord, highlighting His attributes of being 'praised aloud' and 'eternally fresh'. The Sun is called the Udgītha because the indwelling form of Viṣṇu within the solar orb manifests His glory openly to all living beings, serving as the cosmic guide of creation.

verses.dev: एतमु एवाहमभ्यगासिषं तस्मान्मम त्वमेकोऽसीति ह कौषीतकिः पुत्रमुवाच रश्मींस्त्वं पर्यावर्तयाद्बहवो वै ते पुत्रा भविष्यन्तीति साधिदैवतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: etamu evāhamabhyagāsiṣaṃ tasmānmama tvameko'sīti ha kauṣītakiḥ putramuvāca raśmīṃstvaṃ paryāvartayādbahavo vai te putrā bhaviṣyantīti sādhidevatam || 2 ||
verses.trans: Sage Kauṣītaki once said to his son: 'I meditated on the Sun only as a single object; therefore you are my only son. Do you meditate on each of the multi-colored rays of the Sun individually, so that you may be blessed with many children.' This is the meditation with reference to the cosmic deities (Adhidevata).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara treats this verse as a pragmatic lesson in the physics of meditation. Sage Kauṣītaki realized that his own limited, singular meditation on the collective solar disc resulted in a limited, singular outcome (an only son). He advises his child to diversify his meditation by focusing on the individual solar rays (*raśmīs*), which represent different expressions of the cosmic life-force. In Advaita, this shows how relative meditations yield relative, specific karmic fruits within the realm of nature, contrasting with the absolute meditation on the attributeless Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that this verse confirms the relationship between specialized worship and its rewards. The individual rays are unique energetic limbs (*Vibhūtīs*) of the Lord. Meditating on the richness of God's manifestations expands the soul's capacity to receive blessings, ensuring prosperity and continuity in the material world as an act of divine favor.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that various minor deities reside within the different rays of the Sun, all working under the ultimate command of the Supreme Lord. By meditating on the specific functions of these individual rays, the seeker honors the complex divine order, which naturally results in the multiplication of ancestral blessings and children.

verses.dev: अथाध्यात्मं य एवायं मुख्यः प्राणस्तमुद्गीथमुपासीतोमिति ह्युद्गाति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: athādhyātmaṃ ya evāyaṃ mukhyaḥ prāṇastamudgīthamupāsītomiti hyudgāti || 3 ||
verses.trans: Now, next, with reference to the individual body (Adhyātma). One should meditate on the chief vital life-force (Mukhya Prāṇa) inside the body as the Udgītha, for it is this breath that silently chants 'OM' within.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara shifts the student's gaze from the sky (*Adhidevata*) directly back into their own physical body (*Adhyātma*). The cosmic Sun is outside, but the internal Sun is the *Mukhya Prāṇa*—the silent, unattached life-force animating our organs. Just as the Sun moves across the cosmos radiating energy, the internal breath flows through the nervous system, sustaining life. Meditating on this internal breath as OM allows the seeker to discover the immutable presence of Consciousness within the biological matrix.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the internal life-force is the direct, uncorrupted representative of the Lord within the human temple. By transitioning the meditation from the external Sun to the internal breath, the seeker recognizes that God is not a distant sky deity, but the innermost companion who continuously breathes life into their soul.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights this verse as key confirmation of the status of Mukhya Prāṇa. He is the cosmic vehicle of the Lord both outside (as the solar energy) and inside (as the vital life-force). Meditating on the internal breath as the Udgītha means recognizing that our very capacity to think, live, and breathe is a direct gift administered by the highest servant of Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: एतमु एवाहमभ्यगासिषं तस्मान्मम त्वमेकोऽसीति ह कौषीतकिः पुत्रमुवाच प्राणांस्त्वं पर्यावर्तयाद्बहवो वै मे पुत्रा भविष्यन्तीति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: etamu evāhamabhyagāsiṣaṃ tasmānmama tvameko'sīti ha kauṣītakiḥ putramuvāca prāṇāṃstvaṃ paryāvartayādbahavo vai me putrā bhaviṣyantīti || 4 ||
verses.trans: Sage Kauṣītaki then told his son: 'I meditated on the breath only as a single unified force; therefore you are my only son. Do you meditate on the many specialized modifications of the breath individually, so that you may be blessed with many children.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara correlates this with the previous solar verse. The single vital force manifests as various specialized physiological streams (Prāṇa, Apāna, Vyāna, Udāna, Samāna). Meditating on these individual internal currents reveals the complex tapestry of life. Shankara emphasizes that while these analytical divisions are useful for obtaining specific material rewards, the highest goal remains the ultimate reduction of all distinct modifications back into the single, absolute substrate of Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The modifications of the internal breath represent the beautiful, coordinated craftsmanship of the Lord. Raṅgarāmānuja teaches that by appreciating how each specialized stream of breath maintains health and vitality, the seeker develops a deep sense of gratitude and continuous devotion toward the Supreme Creator.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva points out that the different sub-breaths are governed by distinct companion deities working in perfect union under the central authority of Mukhya Prāṇa. Meditating on them individually honors the complex, flawless hierarchy of the inner universe, leading to harmonious physical existence and abundance.

verses.dev: अथ खलु य उद्गीथः स प्रणवो यः प्रणवः स उद्गीथ इति होताषद्नात् हाप्युरुद्गीथं संहरतीत्युद्गीथं संहरतीति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalu ya udgīthaḥ sa praṇavo yaḥ praṇavaḥ sa udgītha iti hotāṣadnāt hāpyurudgīthaṃ saṃharatītyudgīthaṃ saṃharatīti || 5 ||
verses.trans: Now, verily, that which is Udgītha is Pranava, and that which is Pranava is Udgītha. Armed with this realization, even if the Hotṛ priest makes an error in his recitation, the Udgātṛ priest corrects and heals it through the power of the Udgītha. Yes, he completely heals it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara highlights the psychological healing power of knowledge. In a complex Vedic sacrifice, if a priest makes an error in the physical chanting, the entire ritual can collapse. However, if the priest possesses this deep internal realization of the identity of OM, Prāṇa, and the Sun, the sheer power of their meditative awareness acts as a spiritual solvent. The inner knowledge corrects and heals the external flaws. In Advaita, this proves that flawless inner wisdom completely neutralizes and transcends any imperfections in the outer, transactional world.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school interprets this healing power as the redemptive grace of the Lord's name. Human actions are naturally prone to errors and imperfections. However, when an action is thoroughly infused with the remembrance of OM—the primary name of God—the Lord forgives the mechanical shortcomings and accepts the offering as perfect, proving that love and devotion transcend ritual technicalities.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the supreme deities guiding the Udgītha and Pranava possess the unique cosmic capacity to overwrite and correct any operational errors made by subordinate human priests. This absolute spiritual security is a direct benefit of understanding the precise cosmic hierarchy, ensuring that the devotee's spiritual progress is never permanently blocked by accidental human mistakes.

verses.dev: इयमेवर्ग् अग्निः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। इयमेव साग्निरमस्तत्साम ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: iyamevarg agniḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | iyameva sāgniramastatsāma || 1 ||
verses.trans: The Earth is the Rik, and Fire is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Earth is 'Sā' and Fire is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this section begins a systematic meditation designed to merge the components of the vocal chant with cosmic realities. A Sāman melody cannot exist without the structural container of a Rik verse. Allegorically, the moving, transformative element of Fire (*Agni*) rests upon the stable foundation of the Earth (*Pṛthivī*). Shankara breaks down the word etymologically: *Sā* represents the foundational earth, and *Ama* represents the animating fire. This reveals that the very structure of scriptural song mirrors the physical balance of nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja reads this as a manifestation of the structural harmony built into creation by the Supreme Ruler. The Earth and Fire are not random elements; they are functional, interdependent limbs of the cosmic body of God. By visualizing the ritual chant as identical to these cosmic forces, the worshiper learns to see the physical planet as an altar sustained by divine music.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that *Sā* and *Ama* refer to the internal presiding deities of the earth and fire, respectively. These deities are structurally dependent upon one another because they are both animated by different, coordinated forms of Lord Viṣṇu. The meditation teaches the soul to recognize the singular, organizing power that coordinates independent cosmic forces.

verses.dev: अन्तरिक्षमेवर्ग् वायुः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। अन्तरिक्षमेव सा वायुरमस्तत्साम ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: antarikṣamevarg vāyuḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | antarikṣameva sā vāyuramastatsāma || 2 ||
verses.trans: The Mid-region (space) is the Rik, and the Air is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Mid-region is 'Sā' and the Air is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the ascent of meditation into the next cosmic layer. The vast, intermediate space (*Antarikṣa*) acts as the passive, open matrix (the Rik), while the active, moving wind (*Vāyu*) fills it and flows through it (the Sāman). The linguistic pairing remains consistent: *Sā* is the space, *Ama* is the air. By superimposing these qualities, the text helps the meditator expand their mind from the solid earth into the fluid, atmospheric dynamics of the universe.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that the relationship between space and wind shows how the invisible forces of the Lord operate. The wind cannot blow without the container of space, yet space is made dynamically alive by the movement of the wind. This perfect, natural cooperative system reflects the intentional design of the Supreme Controller.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the deity of Air is Mukhya Prāṇa in his atmospheric form, which rests within the space controlled by the deity of the sky. The meditation directs the seeker to look past the physical air currents to see the absolute dependence of the life-giving atmospheric gods upon the Supreme Lord.

verses.dev: द्यौरेवर्ग् आदित्यः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। द्यौरेव सादित्योऽमस्तत्साम ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: dyaurevarg ādityaḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | dyaureva sādityo'mastatsāma || 3 ||
verses.trans: The Heaven is the Rik, and the Sun is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Heaven is 'Sā' and the Sun is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Here the meditation reaches the highest visible cosmic plane. The distant, luminous sky (*Dyauḥ*) is the foundation, and the blazing Sun (*Āditya*) is the dynamic light resting within it. Shankara emphasizes that this upward movement of thought—from earth to mid-space to heaven—is deliberately designed to stretch the human intellect, preparing it to grasp the all-pervading nature of Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that the Sun in the heavens represents the grandest physical manifestation of divine splendor available to human eyes. Linking the Sun with the Sāman chant ensures that the priest performs the ritual with an expansive heart, fully aware that the liturgy connects directly with the highest cosmic realities.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this pairing highlights the relationship between the solar deity and the heavenly space. The independent energy of Viṣṇu operates through the Sun to illuminate the upper worlds, and this meditation honors that specific solar dynamic within the cosmic hierarchy.

verses.dev: नक्षत्राण्येवर्क् चन्द्रमाः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। नक्षत्राण्येव सा चन्द्रमा अमस्तत्साम ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: nakṣatrāṇyevark candramāḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | nakṣatrāṇyeva sā candramā amastatsāma || 4 ||
verses.trans: The Stars are the Rik, and the Moon is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Stars are 'Sā' and the Moon is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara handles the nocturnal sky. The scattered, fixed constellations (*Nakṣatras*) form the canvas, while the moving Moon (*Candramāḥ*) acts as the sovereign entity that coordinates and passes through them. This poetic cosmic identity reinforces the theme that every aspect of the observable universe is balanced by a passive foundation and an active agent, all pointing to the single underlying reality of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that the beautifully ordered movement of the moon through the stars is an expression of the cosmic aesthetic (*saundarya*) of the Lord. The meditation turns astronomy into an act of deep contemplation, helping the soul see divine symmetry in the night sky.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva identifies the Moon and the stars as home to specific administrative deities who govern time, tides, and human minds. Meditating on this relationship emphasizes that all celestial movements are bound by cosmic laws established by the Supreme Person.

verses.dev: अथ यदेतदादित्यस्य शुक्लं भाः सैवर्ग् अथ यन्नीलं परः कृष्णं तत्साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadetadādityasya śuklaṃ bhāḥ saivarg atha yannīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇaṃ tatsāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate || 5 ||
verses.trans: Now, that white radiance of the Sun is the Rik, and that which is intensely blue or deep black within it is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara dives deep into the visual anatomy of solar light. The blinding, outer white glow (*śuklaṃ bhāḥ*) of the Sun is identified as the Rik. If you gaze deeply past that white light, there is a mysterious, intensely dark, ultra-black core (*nīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇam*), which is identified as the Sāman. This dark, hidden core resting inside the brilliant white radiance is a profound metaphor for the unmanifested, ungraspable reality that sits right at the center of the most visible cosmic objects.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights this verse as a critical scriptural source for describing the beautiful form of the Lord. The deep, intense blackness within the sun is not a literal absence of light, but the mesmerizing, cloud-dark hue (*Megha-śyāma*) of the Supreme Person who dwells at the heart of the solar system, radiating beauty from the inside out.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the white light and the deep black core represent two different, superimposed energies of Lord Viṣṇu. The white light handles the material illumination of the physical worlds, while the inner, dark radiance represents the concentrated presence of pure spiritual power governing the inner functions of the cosmos.

verses.dev: अथ यदेतदादित्यस्य शुक्लं भाः सैव सा अथ यन्नीलं परः कृष्णं तदमस्तत्साम। अथ य एषोऽन्तरादित्ये हिरण्मयः पुरुषो दृश्यते हिरण्यश्मश्रुर्हिरण्यकेश आप्रणखात्सर्व एव सुवर्णः ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadetadādityasya śuklaṃ bhāḥ saiva sā atha yannīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇaṃ tadamastatsāma | atha ya eṣo'ntarāditye hiraṇmayaḥ puruṣo dṛśyate hiraṇyaśmaśrurhiraṇyakeśa āpraṇakhātsarva eva suvarṇaḥ || 6 ||
verses.trans: The white light of the sun is 'Sā' and the deep black core is 'Ama', making 'Sāman'. Now, this Golden Person who is seen deep within the Sun, with a golden beard, golden hair, and who is completely golden right to the very tips of his nails—

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides an essential warning regarding the word 'Golden' (*Hiraṇmayaḥ*). It does not mean the Supreme Lord is made of physical, metallic gold, which would make Him finite and subject to melting. Rather, 'golden' is a metaphor for pure, self-luminous light (*jyotirmaya*). This 'Person' (*Puruṣa*) is the cosmic soul, the localized expression of Brahman animating the solar sphere. He is described with a beard and hair to give the human mind a concrete, luminous focal point for advanced meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This is a foundational text for Ramanuja's theology. The description proves that the Supreme Brahman is not a featureless void, but possesses an eternal, non-material, incredibly beautiful divine form (*Divya-maṅgala-vigraha*). This form is made of pure spiritual substance (*Suddha-sattva*), shining like refined gold, and is visualized by advanced yogis through single-pointed devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that this Golden Person is Lord Narayana Himself, residing inside the sun to direct the universe. The form is completely spiritual and eternal. The description of being golden down to the fingernails emphasizes that every single limb of the divine form is independent, perfect, and completely free from any material limitations or decay.

verses.dev: तस्य यथा कप्यासं पुण्डरीकमेवमक्षिणी तस्योदिति नाम स एष सर्वेभ्यः पाप्मभ्य उदितः उदेति ह वै सर्वेभ्यः पाप्मभ्यो य एवं वेद ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: tasya yathā kapyāsaṃ puṇḍarīkamevamakṣiṇī tasyoditi nāma sa eṣa sarvebhyaḥ pāpmabhya uditaḥ udeti ha vai sarvebhyaḥ pāpmābhyo ya evaṃ veda || 7 ||
verses.trans: His eyes are like a lotus flower that has bloomed under the morning sun. His name is 'Ut' (Risen), for he has risen above and is completely free from all evil. He who knows this truth rises completely above all evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara handles the famous and controversial phrase *kapyāsaṃ puṇḍarīkam*. He interprets it linguistically to mean a lotus flower sitting on its stalk, fully opened by the warmth of the sun. The eyes of this cosmic person are wide, bright, and radiating pure awareness. His secret name is *Ut* because He is completely detached from (*udita*) the laws of cause, effect, and sin (*pāpma*). By realizing our ultimate identity with this cosmic person, we automatically rise above our own psychological baggage and past karma.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja famously refined the commentary on *kapyāsaṃ* to rescue it from crude translations, proving it means a lotus deeply rooted in rich waters and opened beautifully by the sun's rays. The lotus-eyes of the Lord convey deep, unconditional compassion (*Karuṇā*). His name *Ut* shows His absolute purity; He is untouched by material flaws, and by meditating on Him, the devotee is pulled out of the mire of material suffering.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that *Ut* is an exclusive name for the Independent Lord, indicating that He stands infinitely above all created things and flaws. The comparison of His eyes to a freshly bloomed lotus illustrates His gentle, life-giving gaze toward His creation. The seeker who understands the absolute purity of the Lord is cleansed of their sins through divine grace.

verses.dev: तस्य र्क् च साम च गेष्णौ तस्मादुद्गीथस्तस्मात्त्वेवोद्गातैतस्य हि गाता स एष ये चामुष्मात्पराञ्चो लोकास्तेषां चेष्टे देवकामानां च ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: tasya rk ca sāma ca geṣṇau tasmādudgīthastasmāttvevodgātaitanya hi gātā sa eṣa ye cāmuṣmātparāñco lokāsteṣāṃ ceṣṭe devakāmānāṃ ca || ८ ||
verses.trans: The Rik and the Sāman are his two joints or singers; therefore, he is the Udgītha. For this reason, the priest who sings his praise is called the Udgātṛ. This Golden Person rules over all the worlds that lie beyond the Sun, and over all the desires of the gods.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the Rik and Sāman are called His joints or singers (*geṣṇau*) because the entire vocal liturgy is powered by His cosmic energy. The verse establishes His macrocosmic sovereignty: He rules over everything above the solar sphere (*amutra*) and satisfies the desires of the gods. In Advaita, this cosmic ruler (*Iśvara*) is the personal aspect of Brahman, managing the vast networks of cosmic cause and effect with absolute intelligence.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The cosmic majesty of the Lord is displayed here. He is the master of all higher spiritual and material realms (*Parāñco Lokāḥ*). The Vedic liturgies are simply musical praises directed toward His cosmic body. Ramanuja teaches that the Lord's supreme rulership means He possesses the absolute capacity to grant any pure desire, whether it belongs to a cosmic deity or a humble human devotee.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes the words 'he rules' (*īṣṭe*). This confirms that Lord Narayana exercises complete, unshared sovereignty over the entire universe above the solar realm. The gods themselves are dependent on Him for their positions and desires, proving that no cosmic force possesses independent power outside the supreme will of Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: अथाध्यात्मं वागेवर्ग् प्राणः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। वागेव सा प्राणोऽमस्तत्साम ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: athādhyātmaṃ vāgevarg prāṇaḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | vāgeva sā prāṇo'mastatsāma || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, next, with reference to the individual body (Adhyātma). Speech is the Rik, and the Breath is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. Speech is 'Sā' and Breath is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this section beautifully mirrors the cosmic meditation (*Adhidevata*) of Section 6 down into the human physiology (*Adhyātma*). The Earth and Fire of the cosmos correspond directly to Speech (*Vāk*) and Breath (*Prāṇa*) inside the body. Speech is the static, formal structure (Rik), while the internal breath is the dynamic, vibrating force that gives it life (Sāman). By mapping the etymology of *Sā-ama* onto speech and breath, the seeker understands that the same harmony governing the external cosmos is running their internal biological machinery.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that speech and breath are dual instruments given to the soul to perform service (*Kaiṅkarya*) to the Lord. Speech cannot manifest without the driving power of the breath, and both are structurally dependent on the inner presence of the Supreme Ruler who coordinates our biological faculties.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the minor deities governing human speech are completely subordinate to and supported by the deity of vital life-force, Mukhya Prāṇa. This internal relationship mirrors how the lower planetary gods depend entirely on the central solar energy, illustrating a flawless spiritual hierarchy inside our own bodies.

verses.dev: चक्षुरेवर्ग् आत्मा साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। चक्षुरेव सात्मामस्तत्साम ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: cakṣurevarg ātmā sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | cakṣureva sātmāmastatsāma || 2 ||
verses.trans: The Eye is the Rik, and the Reflection or Soul within it is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Eye is 'Sā' and the Reflection is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the internal ascent. The physical organ of the eye (*Cakṣu*) corresponds to the intermediate cosmic sky, acting as a passive receiver. The *Ātman* here refers to the shadow, reflection, or the localized sensory power animating the eye (the Sāman). Shankara notes that the physical eye provides the structural field, but the underlying intelligent power of vision is what actually cognizes the world, illustrating how consciousness rests inside the physical frame.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that the eye and its functional capacity are designed by the Lord to perceive His creation. The 'soul within the eye' represents the structural dependence of our individual senses on the subtle optical powers ordained by God, teaching the seeker to treat their physical senses as sacred instruments.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets *Ātman* in this context as the specific, localized form of the divine energy that commands the visual senses. The physical eye cannot perceive anything on its own; it requires the animating presence of this micro-form of Viṣṇu, who provides the capacity for sight.

verses.dev: श्रोत्रमेवर्ङ् मनः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। श्रोत्रमेव सा मनोऽमस्तत्साम ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: śrotramevarṅ manaḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | śrotrameva sā mano'mastatsāma || 3 ||
verses.trans: The Ear is the Rik, and the Mind is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Ear is 'Sā' and the Mind is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A profound psychological insight. The ear (*Śrotra*) is the physical gateway for sound, but it is completely useless without the focus of the Mind (*Manas*). If your mind is drifting, acoustic waves strike your eardrum but you hear absolutely nothing. Therefore, the sensory capacity of hearing (Sāman) rests entirely upon the cognitive focus of the mind (Rik). Shankara points out that this integration forces the meditator to withdraw their attention from raw external noises and focus on the internal mechanics of attention itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that the mind must be trained to receive the sacred sounds of the scriptures through the ear. By declaring the ear and mind to be integrated as Rik and Sāman, the text instructs the seeker to create perfect internal alignment, ensuring that scriptural listening (*Śravaṇa*) turns directly into deep mental contemplation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the deity of the mind (Rudra) acts as the supervisor and director for the sensory information collected by the deity of hearing. This step-by-step structural dependence shows that our psychological apparatus is bound by a strict cosmic order, ultimately working under the supreme guidance of the Lord.

verses.dev: अथ यदेतदक्ष्णः शुक्लं भाः सैवर्ग् अथ यन्नीलं परः कृष्णं तत्साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadetadakṣṇaḥ śuklaṃ bhāḥ saivarg atha yannīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇaṃ tatsāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate || 4 ||
verses.trans: Now, that white radiance of the human eye is the Rik, and that which is intensely blue or deep black within it is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara beautifully bridges the gap between the macrocosm and microcosm. Just as the Sun has a brilliant white outer glare and a deep black core (Section 1.6.5), the human eye has a white sclera (*śuklaṃ bhāḥ*) and a dark pupil (*nīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇam*). This structural symmetry is not an accident; it shows that the human body is an exact miniature blueprint of the universe. Meditating on the dark pupil as the Sāman resting on the white background teaches the student to look for the deep center of awareness within themselves.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that this anatomical meditation bridges the cosmic form of God with His presence inside the human being. The deep black iris and pupil are physical reminders of the beautiful, dark-hued spiritual presence of the Lord, making the human eye an internal mirror of the solar deity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the same dual forces of Viṣṇu that govern the light and darkness of the solar orb are stationed within the physical anatomy of the human eye. This ensures that our personal capacity to interact with light and perceive forms is perfectly synchronized with the macrocosmic laws of nature.

verses.dev: अथ यदेतदक्ष्णः शुक्लं भाः सैव सा अथ यन्नीलं परः कृष्णं तदमस्तत्साम। अथ य एषोऽन्तरक्षिणि पुरुषो दृश्यते सैवर्क् तत्साम तदुक्थं तद्यजुस्तद्ब्रह्म ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadetadakṣṇaḥ śuklaṃ bhāḥ saiva sā atha yannīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇaṃ tadamastatsāma | atha ya eṣo'ntarakṣiṇi puruṣo dṛśyate saivark tatsāma tadukthaṃ tadyajustadbrahma || 5 ||
verses.trans: The white radiance of the eye is 'Sā' and the deep black core is 'Ama', making 'Sāman'. Now, this Person who is seen deep within the human eye — He is the Rik, He is the Sāman, He is the Uktha, He is the Yajus, He is Brahman itself.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is a critical breakthrough text for non-dualism. Shankara writes that the 'Person seen within the eye' (*antar-akṣiṇi puruṣaḥ*) is not a physical shadow, nor is it a minor eyeball deity. He is the ultimate Witness Consciousness (*Sākṣin*), the true Self. By declaring that this internal Seer is identical to all the Vedas and is Brahman itself, the Upanishad pulls the seeker out of the external sky and reveals that the infinite ruler of the universe is identical to the consciousness looking out from behind their own eyes.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja interprets this as the *Antaryāmin*—the indwelling form of the Supreme Lord who lives within the heart and manifests His presence within the eye of the pure-hearted yogi. He is called the Vedas and Brahman because He is the source, substrate, and ultimate goal of all scriptural revelation, living intimately within His creation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes that the Person within the eye is the independent Lord Viṣṇu, present in His micro-manifestation within the soul. He is called Rik, Sāman, and Yajus because all scriptural sounds are ultimately descriptions of His infinite qualities. He remains completely distinct from the human ego, ruling the individual soul from within.

verses.dev: तस्य एतस्य तदेव रूपं यदमुष्य रूपं यावमुष्य गेष्णौ तौ गेष्णौ यन्नाम तन्नाम ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: tasya etasya tadeva rūpaṃ ydamuṣya rūpaṃ yāvamuṣya geṣṇau tau geṣṇau yannāma tannāma || 6 ||
verses.trans: The form of this Person within the eye is exactly the same as the form of that Golden Person within the Sun. The joints of the one are the joints of the other; the name of the one (Ut) is the name of the other.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara shines here. This verse establishes absolute structural equality between the macrocosm (*Adhidevata*) and the microcosm (*Adhyātma*). The Golden Person in the Sun and the Person within the human eye are not two different rulers competing for power; they are the exact same conscious reality viewed from two different perspectives. The apparent difference in scale (cosmic vs. individual) is a trick of our conditioning. Once you pierce the outer shell, the cosmic light and the inner light are one and the same.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja teaches that the identical form, joints, and name prove that the exact same personal God who manages the celestial worlds down to the solar rays is also standing guard inside the individual soul. The Lord assumes the same beautiful lotus-eyed form within the seeker's consciousness to make meditation personal, intimate, and accessible.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the identity of form and name demonstrates perfect spiritual alignment, not individual blending. The form of Viṣṇu within the Sun and His form within the eye are identical in their absolute purity, power, and freedom from material flaws, showing that the Lord is completely consistent across all levels of reality.

verses.dev: स एष ये चैतस्मादर्वाञ्चो लोकास्तेषां चेष्टे मनुष्यकामानाम चेति तद्य इह वीणायां गायन्त्येतं ते गायन्ति तस्मात्ते धनसनयः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣa ye caitasmādarvāñco lokāsteṣāṃ ceṣṭe manuṣyakāmānāma ceti tadya iha vīṇāyāṃ gāyantyetam te gāyanti tasmātte dhanasanayaḥ || 7 ||
verses.trans: This Person within the eye rules over all the worlds that lie below the human plane, and over all the desires of human beings. Therefore, those who sing beautifully to the lute (Vīṇā) are actually singing praises to Him alone; hence they obtain worldly wealth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides a stunning secular commentary. When human musicians sing beautiful songs on a stringed lute (*vīṇā*), they might think they are praising human patrons, romantic themes, or their own talent. But Shankara points out that all beauty, melody, and creative genius are actually minor sparks of the divine consciousness shining through them. The inner Self is the real source of all aesthetic joy, and the wealth musicians earn is simply the natural byproduct of tapping into that divine energy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school uses this verse to explain the spiritual roots of art. All music, poetry, and beauty belong fundamentally to the Lord. Musicians who play the lute are unconsciously drawing from the infinite ocean of divine aesthetics (*Rasa*). When art is consciously redirected toward the Lord within, it transforms from a tool for earning money into a direct path to spiritual liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the Person within the eye rules over the lower material realms (*Arvāñco Lokāḥ*) and satisfies human desires. The creative talents of musicians are direct gifts from this indwelling Lord. When they play beautifully, they are tapping into the harmonic laws set by Viṣṇu, who rewards their alignment with material success and prosperity.

verses.dev: अथ य एतदेवं विद्वान् साम गायत्युभौ स गायति सोऽमुनैव स एष ये चामुष्मात्पराञ्चो लोकास्तेषां चेष्टे देवकामानां च ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: atha ya etadevaṃ vidvān sāma gāyatyubhau sa gāyati so'munaiva sa eṣa ye cāmuṣmātparāñco lokāsteṣāṃ ceṣṭe devakāmānāṃ ca || 8 ||
verses.trans: He who, knowing this truth, sings the Sāman chant, sings to both. Through the cosmic Person, he gains mastery over the worlds beyond the Sun and satisfies the desires of the gods—

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that the realized meditator (*evaṃ vidvān*) bridges both worlds simultaneously. By knowing that the inner observer is identical to the outer cosmic ruler, the seeker's awareness expands to cover the entire spectrum of existence. They are no longer a tiny ego locked inside a skin-bound body; they are the total field, mastering both the physical desires of the earthly plane and the cosmic laws of the higher realms.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The enlightened singer achieves a dual blessing. Because they understand that the Lord inside the eye is the same Lord ruling the cosmos, their prayers carry total spiritual power. Their meditation honors both the immanent and transcendent aspects of God, leading to complete harmony within the universe and the fulfillment of all righteous desires.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that this realization grants the devotee a shared vision of the Lord's complete domain. By understanding that the same independent Viṣṇu acts through both the internal life-forces and the external solar networks, the soul is freed from fragmented thinking and gains access to the spiritual rewards of both spheres.

verses.dev: अथैतेनैव स ये चैतस्मादर्वाञ्चो लोकास्तेषां चेष्टे मनुष्यकामानाम च तस्मादु हैवंविद् उद्गाता ब्रूयात् ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: athaitenaiva sa ye caitasmādarvāñco lokāsteṣāṃ ceṣṭe manuṣyakāmānāma ca tasmādu haivaṃvid udgātā brūyāt || 9 ||
verses.trans: —And through the internal Person, he gains mastery over the worlds below and satisfies all human desires. Therefore, let the Udgātṛ priest who possesses this deep knowledge say to the sacrificer:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara sets up the ultimate practical application of this meditation for the ritual space. The priest who understands this cosmic-internal identity becomes an incredibly powerful spiritual channel. They are no longer just mechanically pronouncing Sanskrit sounds; their voice is backed by the realization of absolute oneness, making their presence a source of profound transformation for anyone attending the ritual.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this knowledge changes the entire relationship between the priest and the devotee. Armed with the understanding that the Lord lives inside the human eye, the priest can look upon the seeker not as a client, but as a living temple of God, lifting their prayers into the divine presence with genuine compassion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the priest who understands this hierarchy acts with clear, conscious authority within the material world. They recognize that the lower realms and human needs are entirely managed by the inner form of the Lord, allowing them to direct the ritual prayers with absolute confidence and precision.

verses.dev: कं ते काममगायानीत्येष हि वै कामगानस्येष्टे य एवं विद्वान् साम गायति साम गायति ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast: kaṃ te kāmamagāyānītyeṣa hi vai kāmagānasyaīṣṭe ya evaṃ vidvān sāma gāyati sāma gāyati || 10 ||
verses.trans: 'What specific desire of yours shall I secure for you by singing?' For he who sings the Sāman with this profound knowledge has absolute power to fulfill desires through his song. Yes, he who has this knowledge has this power.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara concludes Section 7 by demonstrating the absolute efficacy of unified awareness. The priest can confidently ask the seeker, 'What desire shall I sing into reality for you?' (*kaṃ te kāmam agāyāni*). When the human mind drops its fragmented, defensive illusions and sings from the deep center of the *Atman*, its thoughts become naturally aligned with the cosmic laws of manifestation. Shankara reminds us that while material results are guaranteed here, the true wise seeker uses this unshakeable focus to realize the ultimate, desireless freedom of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The section finishes with a display of divine grace channeled through human realization. The priest's song becomes effective because it is an act of pure devotion to the Lord who lives inside the eye. The Lord rewards this deep, aligned meditation by immediately granting the prayers of the faithful, showing that complete surrender to the indwelling Divine fulfills all needs.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the repetition 'sings the Sāman, sings the Sāman' marks the absolute conclusion of this deep structural lesson. The priest's capacity to fulfill desires is not an independent magic trick; it is the direct result of their aligned devotion to the sovereign Lord who commands all desires. True knowledge is power precisely because it connects the soul directly to the source of all cosmic abundance.

verses.dev: त्रयो होद्गीथे कुशला आसुः शिलकः शालावत्यश्चैकितायनो दाल्भ्यः प्रवाहणो जैवलिरिति ते होचुरुद्गीथे वै कुशलाः स्मो हन्तोद्गीथे कथां वदाम इति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: trayo hodgīthe kuśalā āsuḥ śilakaḥ śālāvatyaścaikitāyano dālbhyaḥ pravāhaṇo jaivaliriti te hocurudgīthe vai kuśalāḥ smo hantodgīthe kathāṃ vadāma iti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Three men were well-versed in the Udgītha: Śilaka Śālāvatya, Caikitāyana Dālbhya, and Pravāhaṇa Jaivali. They said to one another, 'We are all experts in the Udgītha. Come, let us hold a discussion on this sacred sound.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this section shifts from purely meditative injunctions to an instructional narrative or debate (*Kthā*).
