The text introduces three historical scholars to emphasize that spiritual tracking requires dialogue and systematic elimination of shallow concepts. Two are Brahmins (*Śilaka* and *Dālbhya*) and one is a Kṣatriya king (*Pravāhaṇa*). This setting shows that the highest metaphysical wisdom (*Jñāna*) often bypassed rigid priestly formulas and rested with the philosophical rulers who looked beyond ritual technicalities.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this verse sets a beautiful example of spiritual fellowship (*Satsaṅga*). Even highly accomplished masters do not act out of intellectual pride; instead, they gather humbly to refine their understanding of the ultimate truth, recognizing that talking about the Lord's symbols clears the mind and purifies the heart.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the gathering of these three scholars was orchestrated by the divine will to reveal the true hierarchy of cosmic causes. Each scholar represents a different depth of perception, and the discussion is designed to lead the student step-by-step from material causes up to the supreme independent ruler, Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: तथेति ह समुपविविशुः स ह प्रवाहणो जैवलिरुवाच भगवन्तावग्रे वदतां ब्राह्मणयोर्वदतोः श्रोष्यामीति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tatheti ha samupaviviśuḥ sa ha pravāhaṇo jaivaliruvāca bhagavantāvagre vadatāṃ brāhmaṇayorvādatoḥ śroṣyāmīti || 2 ||
verses.trans: Saying, 'Let it be so,' they sat down together. Pravāhaṇa Jaivali said, 'You two venerable Brahmins may speak first. I will listen to your philosophical dialogue.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara highlights the cultural dynamics and politeness of the debate. King Pravāhaṇa, despite holding the deeper, final knowledge, yields the platform to the two Brahmin priests out of respect for their traditional position as teachers. In Advaita, this illustrates that transactional decorum and social humility are completely compatible with internal spiritual mastery.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school interprets the king's request as an example of genuine spiritual humility and patience. By choosing to listen first, the king embodies the quality of a true seeker who observes carefully before offering the ultimate context of divine reality, honoring the specialized learning of the priests.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the king stays quiet at first because he wants the limitations of the lower cosmic views to be fully exposed by the Brahmins themselves. This structural patience allows the final truth of the Supreme Lord's absolute independence to shine with complete clarity when it is finally spoken.

verses.dev: स ह शिलकः शालावत्यश्चैकितायनं दाल्भ्यमुवाच हन्त त्वा पृच्छानीति पृच्छेति होवाच ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa ha śilakaḥ śālāvatyaścaikitāyanaṃ dālbhyamuvāca hanta tvā pṛcchānīti pṛccheti hovāca || 3 ||
verses.trans: Then Śilaka Śālāvatya said to Caikitāyana Dālbhya, 'If you permit, may I question you?' Dālbhya replied, 'Please, ask.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the precise rules of engagement here. By asking permission to question, Śilaka sets up a formal, analytical investigation (*Vicāra*). This step-by-step dialectic ensures that the search for the core of the Udgītha moves forward logically, peeling away superficial understandings through question and answer.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The dialogue form shows that scriptural truths are best understood when tested through inquiry. Raṅgarāmānuja points out that this formal question-and-answer dynamic opens up the student's intellect, transforming mechanical ritual knowledge into a deeper, living quest for ultimate realities.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights this verse as the beginning of a deliberate cosmic countdown. The examination begins between the two traditional scholars to trace the stream of energy back to its source, demonstrating that every intermediate power in creation must answer to a higher authority.

verses.dev: का साम्नो गतिरिति स्वर इति होवाच स्वरस्य का गतिरिति प्राण इति होवाच प्राणस्य का गतिरित्यन्नमिति होवाच अन्नस्य का गतिरित्याप इति होवाच ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: kā sāmno gatiriti svara iti hovāca svarasya kā gatiriti prāṇa iti hovāca prāṇasya kā gatirityannamiti hovāca annasya kā gatirityāpa iti hovāca || 4 ||
verses.trans: 'What is the ultimate support of the Sāman?' asked Śilaka. 'Sound or breath-pitch (Svara),' replied Dālbhya. 'What is the support of sound?' 'The Breath (Prāṇa).' 'What is the support of the Breath?' 'Food (Anna).' 'What is the support of food?' 'Water (Āpas).'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A masterpiece of logical reductionism. Shankara tracks how Dālbhya traces the material and biological layers backward. The musical Sāman depends on vocal pitch (*Svara*); pitch depends on the physical movement of breath (*Prāṇa*); breath cannot exist without the fuel of food (*Anna*); and food cannot grow without water (*Āpas*). Each step represents a deep conditioning dependency, moving from the subtle phonetic expression down to the gross material elements of nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that this chain of dependencies beautifully demonstrates the physical interconnectedness of the Lord's material universe. No biological function operates in isolation. The breath, food, and water are all linked links in a chain of life, showing how the physical body relies entirely on the elements provided by divine providence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva points out that this sequence maps the hierarchy of presiding elemental deities. The deity of sound depends on the deity of breath, who relies on the food-deity, who is sustained by the water-deity. This systematic trace proves to the student that no natural element holds independent power; each answers to a superior cosmic law.

verses.dev: अपां का गतिरित्यसौ लोक इति होवाच अमुष्य लोकस्य का गतिरिति न स्वर्गं लोकादतिनयामीति होवाच स्वर्गं वयँ लोकं सामाभिसंस्थापयामः स्वर्गसंस्तावँ हि सामेति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: apāṃ kā gatirityasau loka iti hovāca amuṣya lokasya kā gatiriti na svargaṃ lokādatinayāmīti hovāca svargaṃ vayaṃ lokaṃ sāmābhisaṃsthāpayāmaḥ svargasaṃstāvaṃ hi sāmeti || 5 ||
verses.trans: 'What is the support of water?' 'Yonder heavenly world (Svarga),' said Dālbhya. 'And what is the support of yonder heavenly world?' Dālbhya replied, 'Do not question further, for I cannot go beyond the heavenly world. We establish the Sāman within the paradise of heaven, for the Sāman is praised as heaven itself.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara exposes the dead end of purely ritualistic thinking. Dālbhya traces water back to the rain clouds of the heavenly world (*Svarga*), but when asked what supports heaven, he hits a wall and says, 'Do not push me further' (*na atinayāmi*). For a ritualist, paradise is the final station, the absolute ceiling of happiness. Shankara explains that this is Dālbhya's error: he mistakes a temporary, conditional celestial realm for the boundless substrate of reality, exposing the limitations of material religion.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that Dālbhya's stop at heaven shows he has not yet discovered the transcendental home of God (*Parama-padam*). He treats heaven as the final goal because it satisfies the highest ritual desires, but he fails to realize that the material heavens are still finite realms created, sustained, and dissolved by the Supreme Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva states that Dālbhya's knowledge ends at the deity of heaven because his spiritual vision is limited to karmic rewards. He believes the Sāman chant exists simply to secure a place in paradise. This verse reveals that without deep metaphysical insight, a ritualist remains trapped within the created worlds, unable to see the true source of all planes.

verses.dev: तँ ह शिलकः शालावत्यश्चैकितायनं दाल्भ्यमुवाचाप्रतिष्ठितं वै किल ते दाल्भ्य साम यस्त्वेतर्हि ब्रूयान्मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति मूर्धा ते विपतेदिति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ ha śilakaḥ śālāvatyaścaikitāyanaṃ dālbhyamuvācāpratiṣṭhitaṃ vai kila te dālbhya sāma yastvetharhi brūyānmūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mūrdhā te vipatediti || 6 ||
verses.trans: Then Śilaka Śālāvatya said to Caikitāyana Dālbhya, 'Verily, Dālbhya, your Sāman is without a final foundation. If someone who knows the true foundation were to tell you at this moment, "Your head will fall off," your head would indeed fall off.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This severe idiomatic warning ('your head will fall off') is explained by Shankara not as a literal physical threat or a magic curse, but as a deep metaphysical law. If a teacher confidently preaches an ultimate truth but stops at a limited, incomplete layer (like heaven), they are living in a state of intellectual fragmentation. If they are challenged in public by absolute truth, their false pride and spiritual ego are completely shattered. Their conceptual 'head' falls off because their philosophy collapses under reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views this sharp rebuke as a necessary wake-up call for ritualists who mistake heavenly comfort for final liberation (*Mokṣa*). Stopping short of the Divine means your spiritual structure is unstable (*apratiṣṭhitam*). The warning emphasizes that teaching partial truths as absolute reality carries heavy spiritual responsibility.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that stopping at any subordinate deity without reaching the Supreme independent source violates the laws of cosmic truth. Your spiritual understanding is ungrounded. The warning about the head falling off indicates that an incorrect understanding of the divine hierarchy halts all spiritual progress and brings downstream confusion.

verses.dev: हन्ताहमेतद्भगवतो वेदानीति विद्धीति होवाचामुष्य लोकस्य का गतिरित्ययमेव लोक इति होवाचास्य लोकस्य का गतिरिति न प्रतिष्ठां लोकादतिनयामीति होवाच प्रतिष्ठां वयँ लोकं सामाभिसंस्थापयामः प्रतिष्ठासंस्तावँ हि सामेति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: hantāhametadbhagavato vedānīti viddhīti hovācamuṣya lokasya kā gatirityayameva loka iti hovācāsya lokasya kā gatiriti na pratiṣṭhāṃ lokādatinayāmīti hovāca pratiṣṭhāṃ vayaṃ lokaṃ sāmābhisaṃsthāpayāmaḥ pratiṣṭhāsaṃstāvaṃ hi sāmeti || 7 ||
verses.trans: 'Let me, then, learn the truth from you, venerable sir,' said Dālbhya. 'Learn it,' replied Śilaka. 'What is the support of yonder heavenly world?' 'This earth-world (Ayam Loka),' said Śilaka. 'And what is the support of this earth-world?' Śilaka replied, 'I cannot go beyond this earth, which is our physical foundation. We establish the Sāman on this earth, for the Sāman is praised as the foundation itself.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the next turn of the debate. Dālbhya admits his limitation and becomes a student. Śilaka takes the step forward, tracing heaven back down to *This Earth* (*Ayam Loka*). Why? Because the rituals performed on earth feed the gods in heaven; heaven relies on earth for its existence through sacrifice. But when asked what supports the earth, Śilaka hits his own wall, claiming the physical earth is the absolute foundation (*Pratiṣṭhā*). He goes lower than heaven, but remains stuck inside material nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that Śilaka understands the law of mutual dependence between heaven and earth through sacrifice, which is a step deeper than Dālbhya's isolated view. However, Śilaka makes the same mistake by stopping at the physical planet, failing to see the invisible, omnipotent spiritual energy that keeps the planet floating in space.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, Śilaka’s view is grounded in the material world. He recognizes that the higher worlds are fed by sacrifices on earth, which shows a broader understanding of cosmic machinery. Yet, by locking his philosophy onto physical nature as the final stop, he misses the ultimate source who anchors the earth.

verses.dev: तँ ह प्रवाहणो जैवलिरुवाचाप्रतिष्ठितं वै किल ते शालावत्य साम यस्त्वेतर्हि ब्रूयान्मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति मूर्धा ते विपतेदिति हन्ताहमेतद्भगवतो वेदानीति विद्धीति होवाच ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ ha pravāhaṇo jaivaliruvācāpratiṣṭhitaṃ vai kila te śālāvatya sāma yastvetharhi brūyānmūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mūrdhā te vipatediti hantāhametadbhagavato vedānīti viddhīti hovāca || 8 ||
verses.trans: Then Pravāhaṇa Jaivali said to him, 'Verily, Śālāvatya, your Sāman has also turned out to be without a final foundation. If someone who knows the ultimate truth were to tell you now, "Your head will fall off," your head would indeed fall off.' Śilaka said, 'Let me, then, learn the truth from you, venerable sir.' The king replied, 'Learn it.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The climax of Section 8. King Pravāhaṇa steps in and uses the exact same philosophical check on Śilaka. Both priests have failed because one stopped at a subjective heaven and the other stopped at a physical earth. They are trapped in worldly cause and effect. Śilaka immediately drops his teaching persona, humbles himself, and asks the king for instruction. This completes the transformation of the debate, preparing the stage for Section 9 to reveal the single, infinite element that supports all worlds.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights this moment as the triumph of comprehensive spiritual insight over rigid ritual philosophy. Both priests are forced to admit that neither heaven nor earth can support themselves. They turn to the king as students, proving that the hunger for ultimate truth transcends social caste and ritual hierarchy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this final collapse of both priestly views clears away the misconceptions of material cosmology. By reducing both heaven and earth to dependent entities, the text sets up an open runway for the king to introduce the ultimate, uncreated, independent support of the universe in the upcoming section.

verses.dev: अस्य लोकस्य का गतिरित्याकाश इति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतान्याकाशादेव समुत्पद्यन्त आकाशं प्रत्यस्तं यन्त्याकाशो ह्येवैभ्यो ज्यायानाकाशः परायणम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: asya lokasya kā gatirityākāśa iti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtānyākāśādeva samutpadyanta ākāśaṃ pratyastaṃ yantyākāśo hyevaibhyo jyāyānākāśaḥ parāyaṇam || 1 ||
verses.trans: 'What is the ultimate destination of this world?' asked Śilaka. 'Space (Ākāśa),' replied Pravāhaṇa. 'Verily, all these created beings arise from Space alone, and they return back into Space. For Space alone is greater than these; Space is the ultimate goal.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara delivers one of his most celebrated philosophical definitions here. He clarifies that *Ākāśa* in this absolute context does not mean the material element of physical space or sky (*Bhūtākāśa*). Instead, it means the Supreme Brahman (*Paramātman*). Physical objects cannot create or dissolve other physical objects from scratch; only the infinite, non-dual Consciousness acts as the material and efficient cause of the universe. All beings sprout from Consciousness, are sustained by it, and collapse back into it. Consciousness is *jyāyān* (greater than everything) and *parāyaṇam* (the final resort).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja argues that *Ākāśa* is a direct name for the Supreme Lord (*Nārāyaṇa*), who possesses the quality of infinite expansiveness. The verse explicitly states that all elements originate from and dissolve into this space. Because material space is itself a created thing, the 'Space' mentioned here must be the sentient, independent Divine Ground who supports both the spiritual soul and physical matter.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the word *Ākāśa* etymologically means 'He who is luminous everywhere' (*ā-samantāt kāśate*), which is an exclusive title of Lord Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu is the primary cause from whom the secondary element of space and all other elements emerge. He is 'greater' because He is completely independent, while all of creation depends on Him for its existence, movement, and preservation.

verses.dev: स एष परोवरीयानुद्गीथः स एषोऽनन्तः परोवरीयो हास्य भवति परोवरीयसो लोकाञ्जयति य एतदेवं विद्वान्परोवरीयाँसमुद्गीथमुपास्ते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣa parovarīyānudgīthaḥ sa eṣo'nantaḥ parovarīyo hāsya bhavati parovarīyaso lokāñjayati ya etadevaṃ vidvānparovarīyāṃsamudgīthamupāste || 2 ||
verses.trans: This Udgītha, identical with Space, is the most excellent and supreme; it is infinite. He who, knowing this truth, meditates upon the Udgītha as the supreme and infinite, attains what is most excellent, and wins supreme and excellent worlds.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the meaning of *parovarīyān* (higher than the high, greater than the great). By identifying the Udgītha chant with infinite Space (Brahman), the meditation expands from a localized ritual act into a cosmic realization. The 'infinite worlds' won by the seeker are not temporary heavenly domains; they represent the breakdown of limitation itself. To know the infinite is to become infinite.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that meditating on the Udgītha as the infinite Lord brings the seeker into alignment with the highest realms of existence. The rewards described here represent step-by-step spiritual elevation, culminating in the attainment of the supreme, imperishable spiritual abode (*Vaikuṇṭha*) where there is no return to material suffering.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that the repetition of excellence highlights the absolute supremacy of Viṣṇu over all other paths of worship. The devotee who recognizes the Lord as the infinite, independent master of all cosmic layers breaks through lower karmic entanglements, receiving eternal spiritual joy directly from the hands of the Almighty.

verses.dev: तद्वैतदतिधन्वा शौनक उददरशाण्डिल्यायोक्त्वोवाच यावत्त एनं प्रजायामुद्गीथं वेदिष्यन्ते परोवरीयो हैभ्यस्तावदस्मिँल्लोके जीवनं भविष्यति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tadvaitadabhidhanvā śaunaka udadaraśāṇḍilyāyoktvovāca yāvatta enaṃ prajāyāmudgīthaṃ vediṣyante parovarīyo haibhyastāvadasmiँlloke jīvanaṃ bhaviṣyati || 3 ||
verses.trans: Atidhanvan, the son of Śunaka, taught this profound doctrine of the Udgītha to Udaraśāṇḍilya and said: 'As long as your descendants continue to understand this deep meaning of the Udgītha, their lives in this world will be supreme and excellent.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara points out that this verse traces the historical lineage (*Vaṃśa*) of the knowledge to prove its authenticity and unshakeable track record. The promise of an 'excellent life' for future generations means that when a family or culture maintains a living realization of the infinite, their worldly life remains free from the anxious desperation of materialistic ignorance.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that spiritual wisdom should be preserved and passed down through generations as a sacred family inheritance. When children are raised with the deep understanding that the universe is pervaded by the Lord, their lives are naturally filled with righteousness, stability, and purpose.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this lineage-based blessing shows that the true understanding of the divine hierarchy acts as a protective shield for society. Generations that honor the independent supremacy of Viṣṇu remain anchored in cosmic truth, experiencing prosperity in this life and spiritual liberation hereafter.

verses.dev: तथाऽमुष्मिँल्लोके लोक इति परोवरीयो हास्य भवति परोवरीयसो लोकाञ्जयति य एतदेवं विद्वान्परोवरीयाँसमुद्गीथमुपास्ते ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: tathā'muṣmiँlloke loka iti parovarīyo hāsya bhavati parovarīyaso lokāñjayati ya etadevaṃ vidvānparovarīyāṃsamudgīthamupāste || 4 ||
verses.trans: 'And likewise, their state in the next world will be supreme and excellent.' He who, knowing this truth, meditates upon the Udgītha as the supreme and infinite, becomes supreme in this life and wins supreme worlds in the life to come.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara wraps up Section 9 with a comprehensive seal of realization. The phrase *tathā amuṣmin loke* guarantees that the fruits of absolute realization are unbroken across time and space. Whether existing in this physical body or passing into other dimensions of reality, the knower of Brahman remains centered in the same unmoving, boundless source, entirely free from fear.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The conclusion of this section emphasizes that true devotion protects the seeker across both horizons of existence. The soul is taken care of here on earth, and upon departure, it inherits the timeless spiritual kingdom, demonstrating that knowledge of the Lord as the ultimate space fulfills all paths.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva concludes that this absolute declaration settles the debate once and for all. Neither ritual paradise nor the physical earth can offer final safety; only the independent, all-pervading Lord Viṣṇu offers unshakeable shelter. Recognizing Him as the heart of the Udgītha guarantees ultimate success across all cosmic planes.

verses.dev: मटचीहतेषु कुरुषु आटक्या सह जायया उषस्तिर्ह चाक्रायण इभ्यग्रामे प्रद्राणक उवास ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: maṭacīhateṣu kuruṣu āṭakyā saha jāyayā uṣastirha cākrāyaṇa ibhyagrāme pradrāṇaka uvāsa || 1 ||
verses.trans: When the crops in the Kuru country were destroyed by locusts (or a severe hailstorm), Uṣasti Cākrāyaṇa, living in a state of extreme poverty, lived in the village of a wealthy elephant-owner with his very young wife.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that the text switches to a historical narrative (*Ākhyāyikā*) to make the difficult doctrine of meditation accessible and to illustrate a crucial moral exception: *Āpaddharma* (righteous action during times of extreme emergency). The term *maṭacī* refers to locusts or a devastating frost that wiped out the food supply. Even a highly realized sage like Uṣasti is subject to the physical biological pressures of the material world, showing that until the body drops, the physical vehicle must be kept alive, even under humbling social conditions.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this narrative shows that external circumstances do not change the internal spiritual wealth of a true sage. Although Uṣasti was reduced to living as a wanderer in a wealthy village (*ibhyagrāme*), his spiritual status remained untouched. It highlights that the material world fluctuates, but the devotion of a knower of God remains steady.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that this famine was an expression of divine cosmic adjustments. The suffering of the Kurus serves as a background to show how a true representative of God preserves his life and maintains his spiritual focus even when traditional, clean options are completely unavailable.

verses.dev: स हेभ्यं कुल्माषान्खादन्तं बिभिक्षे तं होवाच नेतोऽन्ये विद्यन्ते य इम एवेम उपनिहिता इति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa hebhyaṃ kulmāṣānkhādantaṃ bibhikṣe taṃ hovāca neto'nye vidyante ya ima evema upanihitā iti || 2 ||
verses.trans: Seeing the elephant-owner eating some low-grade boiled beans (kulmāṣa), Uṣasti begged him for food. The elephant-owner said, 'I have no other food left except these which are placed here before me in my bowl.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the raw reality of the situation. *Kulmāṣa* are cheap, poor-man's beans, flatulent and split. The wealthy elephant-owner himself is down to his last scrap due to the famine. The master of spiritual truths is begging from a wealthy worldly person, showing that the physical body levels all social hierarchies when survival is on the line.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the compassion and honesty of the householder. Even though he had very little, he did not hide it. He openly showed his bowl, indicating that sharing whatever little one has with a holy person is a path of cleansing, even if the food is humble and half-eaten.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the elephant-owner's answer was guided by Providence to test Uṣasti's understanding of the cosmic laws of survival. The dialogue sets up a fundamental lesson in spiritual flexibility over external, rigid ritualism.

verses.dev: एतेषां मे देहीति होवाच तानस्मै प्रददौ हन्तानुपानमित्युच्छिष्टं वै मे पीतँ स्यादिति होवाच ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: eteṣāṃ me dehīti hovāca tānasmai pradadau hantānupānamityucchṣṭaṃ vai me pītaṃ syāditi hovāca || 3 ||
verses.trans: Uṣasti said, 'Give me some of those.' The man gave them to him and then said, 'Here is some water in this cup for you to drink.' Uṣasti replied, 'If I drank that, I would be drinking your leftover, defiled water (ucchṣṭa).'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A profound logical tension emerges here. Shankara explains that Uṣasti gladly accepted the defiled, half-eaten beans (*ucchṣṭa*), but flatly refused the water. The elephant-owner is confused and challenges him. This action prepares the student for the next verse, which reveals the exact boundary line between rational self-preservation and unnecessary ritual rule-breaking.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the sage acts with careful discrimination. He accepts the food because it is a life-or-death situation, but his refusal of the water demonstrates that a spiritual person does not abandon all discipline or cleanliness. They only compromise on what is absolutely necessary to stay alive.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the sage's behavior shows that even during emergencies, a devotee respects the cosmic purity scales. Water was widely available elsewhere, but the unique food energy needed to keep his vital breath (*Prāṇa*) from failing was completely unavailable. Therefore, his choice is based on real biological necessity, not casual laziness.

verses.dev: न स्विदेतेऽप्युच्छिष्टा इति न वा अजीविष्यमिमानखादन्निति होवाच कामो म उदपानमिति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: na svidete'pyucchṣṭā iti na vā ajīviṣyamimānakhādanniti hovāca kāmo ma udapānamiti || 4 ||
verses.trans: The elephant-owner said, 'But are these beans not also leftovers and defiled?' Uṣasti replied, 'Without eating these beans, I would have died of starvation. But as for drinking water, I can find clean water whenever I wish.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: This is the core pillar of the *Āpaddharma* doctrine in Advaita. Shankara notes that eating defiled food to save your life is not a sin (*Pāpa*), because life is the foundation for all future spiritual practice. If you die of artificial pride, you cannot meditate. However, breaking rules when an easy, clean alternative exists (like finding clean water elsewhere) is driven by sensory craving, which directly damages the clarity of the intellect.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school emphasizes that the preservation of the physical body is sacred because the body is the temple of the indwelling Lord. Uṣasti balances common sense with spiritual rules. He eats the beans to protect his vital energies, but protects his spiritual boundaries by refusing the water because clean water was still accessible.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva delivers a sharp distinction: rules are created to support life and spiritual growth, not to cause destruction. The vital air (*Prāṇa*) belongs to the Lord, and letting it collapse over an external food rule is an insult to the life force inside. The sage's answer sets a clear standard for analyzing priorities during crises.

verses.dev: स ह खादित्वा अतिशेषाञ्जायाया आजहार सा अग्रत एव सुभिक्षा बभूव सा तान्प्रतिगृह्य निदधौ ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: sa ha khāditvā atiśeṣāñjāyāyā ājahāra sā agrata eva subhikṣā babhūva sā tānpratigṛhya nidadhau || 5 ||
verses.trans: Having eaten, Uṣasti took the remaining beans home to his wife. She had already received some food through alms earlier, so she took her husband's gifts and carefully put them away.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara comments on the behavior of the young wife. Being naturally modest and having already satisfied her hunger elsewhere, she does not greedily consume the scraps. Instead, she saves them, showing the domestic harmony and careful planning required to navigate a crisis without losing your dignity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The wife's actions show deep respect for her husband's offering. Even though she wasn't hungry, she recognized the food as a blessing gathered through hardship and stored it safely, illustrating a family lifestyle centered on contentment and resourcefulness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva treats this moment as a demonstration of family care. The sage’s first instinct after saving his own life is to protect his partner. The storage of the remaining food acts as a bridge for the next day's events, showing how simple actions can set up major spiritual turning points.

verses.dev: स ह प्रातः संजिहान उवाच यद्वतेदमन्नस्य लभेमहि लभेमहि धनमात्रां राजासौ यक्ष्यते स मा सर्वैर्आर्त्विज्यैर्वृणीतेति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: sa ha prātaḥ saṃjihāna uvāca yadvatedamannasya labhemahi labhemahi dhanamātrāṃ rājāsau yakṣyate sa mā sarvairārtvijyairvṛṇīteti || 6 ||
verses.trans: The next morning, on waking up from sleep, Uṣasti said, 'Alas! If we could only get a small portion of food, we might obtain some wealth. The king nearby is about to perform a great sacrifice; he would surely choose me to direct all the sacrificial duties.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the human side of the sage. He needs a burst of physical energy to walk to the sacrifice and claim his rightful place as a top teacher. His focus is not on hoarding money, but on using the king's grand ritual platform to reveal the ultimate essence of the chanting elements, which the local court priests are chanting without real understanding.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the sage seeks involvement in the sacrifice because it is a natural opportunity to perform service and spread spiritual clarity. His desire for wealth is purely practical—intended to restore his household so he can continue his spiritual duties without being dependent on others.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the sage knows his inner value. He doesn't go to the sacrifice as an ordinary beggar; he goes as an authority who can correct structural errors in the ritual, showing that true spiritual expertise remains authoritative even after sleeping on famine scraps.

verses.dev: तं जायोवाच हन्तेम एव कुल्माषा इति तान्खादित्वा अमुं यज्ञं विततमेयाय ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ jāyovāca hantema eva kulmāṣā iti tānkhāditvā amuṃ yajñaṃ vitatameyāya || 7 ||
verses.trans: His wife said to him, 'Well, sir, here are the leftover beans you gave me yesterday.' He ate them and immediately walked over to the grand sacrificial assembly.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara shows the beautiful closing of this loop. The very scraps he saved out of compassion the day before now provide him with the physical energy to walk to the palace. This loop demonstrates that compassionate actions return to protect you, turning simple emergency food into the fuel needed for an ultimate spiritual mission.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The preservation of the food by the wife becomes the catalyst for the sage's journey. Eating these simple beans out of deep gratitude, the sage goes forth to illuminate the king’s sacrifice, showing that great spiritual works are often built on quiet, humble moments at home.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the leftover food satisfies his hunger, showing that the Lord uses the simplest means to sustain his devotees. Energized by the beans, Uṣasti approaches the sacrifice ready to challenge the superficial ritualists and establish the correct divine order.

verses.dev: तत्रोद्गातॄनस्तावे स्तोष्यमाणानुपोपविवेश स ह प्रस्तोतारमुवाच ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: tatrodgātṝnastāve stoṣyamāṇānupopaviveśa sa ha prastotāramuvāca || 8 ||
verses.trans: There he found the Prastotṛ priests sitting together in the place of chanting, about to sing their hymns. He walked up, sat close beside the Prastotṛ, and addressed him directly:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara sets up the dramatic entry. Uṣasti doesn't make a loud, angry scene; he quietly sits next to the head chanter (*Prastotṛ*). This close positioning signifies that a true teacher addresses the student exactly where they are working, ready to insert ultimate awareness right into the middle of their mechanical, everyday actions.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views this as the arrival of an emissary of truth. The priests are preparing to sing without fully understanding the inner cosmic ruler of the hymn. Uṣasti’s arrival represents the descent of grace, offering the chanters an immediate chance to upgrade their dry performance into a living act of devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that sitting close signifies an immediate intellectual challenge. By targeting the lead chanters, Uṣasti prepares to expose the missing link in their training, laying the groundwork for the next section to reveal the specific deities who govern the parts of the sacred sound.

verses.dev: प्रस्तोतर्या देवता प्रस्तावमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रस्तोष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: prastotaryā devatā prastāvamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānprastoṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Uṣasti said: 'O Prastotṛ, if you sing the introductory praise (Prastāva) without knowing the deity that is innerly connected with it, your head will fall off.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara repeats his interpretation of this severe idiomatic warning. He notes that Uṣasti is not acting out of malice or practicing black magic. Rather, he is stating an unalterable epistemological law: when an individual takes on the role of a spiritual teacher or direct guide in a public ritual, yet remains completely ignorant of the underlying metaphysical reality (*devatā*), they are living in a divided state of mind. When confronted by a true knower of reality, their false confidence and intellectual ego are completely shattered. Their conceptual 'head' falls off as their superficial assertions dissolve.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this verse serves as a crucial warning against mechanical and blind ritualism. Performing a sacred action simply for monetary reward or social standing, without knowing the indwelling Lord who gives that action its power, turns the ritual into a empty husk. The warning emphasizes that true spiritual action requires active, internal awareness of the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that every part of the sacred chant is governed by a distinct cosmic administrator under the control of the Supreme Lord. To invoke the sound without honoring the specific presiding deity violates the cosmic order, leading to a loss of spiritual capacity and mental clarity.

verses.dev: एवमेवोद्गातुरुवाचोद्गातर्या देवताोद्गीथमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वानुद्गास्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: evamevodgātūruvācodgātaryā devatodgīthamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānudgāsyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti || 2 ||
verses.trans: In the same manner, he addressed the Udgātṛ: 'O Udgātṛ, if you sing the loud chant (Udgītha) without knowing the deity that is innerly connected with it, your head will fall off.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that Uṣasti moves down the line of officiating priests systematically. The *Udgītha* is the central, core portion of the Sāman song. Because it is the most prominent element, performing it without realizing its true substrate is an even deeper spiritual mistake, leading to a complete collapse of inner focus when challenged by truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that because the Udgītha represents the highest cosmic resonance, chanting it blindly is a tragedy of missed opportunity. The warning is repeated to wake up the main singer, challenging him to look beyond his musical technique and seek the ultimate object of devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the Udgātṛ invokes a higher level of cosmic energy. Ignorance at this stage disrupts the chain of divine command, showing that high offices in spiritual practice require matching levels of inner realization.

verses.dev: एवमेव प्रतिहर्तारमुवाच प्रतिहर्तर्या देवता प्रतिहारमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रतिहरिष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति ते ह विररामुरुपससेदुः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: evameva pratihartāramuvāca pratihartaryā devatā pratihāramanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānpratihariṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti te ha virarāmurupasaseduḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: Finally, he addressed the Pratihartṛ: 'O Pratihartṛ, if you sing the response (Pratihāra) without knowing the deity that is innerly connected with it, your head will fall off.' Hearing this, all the priests stopped their chanting immediately and sat down silently to become his disciples.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the psychological shift here. The phrase *te ha virarāmuḥ* (they stopped immediately) shows that the priests recognized the voice of a true master. Instead of getting angry, arguing, or defending their status, they instantly dropped their ritual instruments and sat down as humble students (*upasaseduḥ*). This action shows genuine integrity: they prioritized discovering the truth over saving face in the king's court.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja praises the humility of the court priests. Despite being surrounded by the king and the wealth of the sacrifice, they chose to stop chanting the moment they realized their inner lack. This instant surrender transforms them from proud performers into true seekers of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that the absolute stillness that fell over the assembly proves the natural authority of spiritual realization. When a representative of the supreme independent truth speaks, all lower, dependent actions naturally pause to align themselves with the correct cosmic hierarchy.

verses.dev: अथ हैनं राजावाच भगवन्तं वा अहमुपविद्यां कस्यै भगवन्मयूढ उषस्तिरस्मीति होवाच ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṃ rājāvāca bhagavantaṃ vā ahamupavidyāṃ kasyai bhagavanmayūḍha uṣastirasmīti hovāca || 4 ||
verses.trans: Then the king said to him: 'Venerable sir, I wish to know who you are.' The sage replied: 'I am Uṣasti, the son of Cakra.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that the king recognizes that this visitor is no ordinary wanderer. By asking for his identity, the king is showing formal respect. When the sage gives his name, the king immediately remembers his stellar reputation, realizing that the master he had spent months looking for has finally arrived.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The king's inquiry represents the awakening of a leader's discrimination. He realizes that the success of his entire kingdom's sacrifice depends on the purity and vision of the directors. Learning that it is Uṣasti brings him immense relief and joy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the name of the sage carries weight because it is tied to an unbroken lineage of realization. The king's immediate shift in attitude shows that worldly power naturally honors the spiritual authority assigned by God.

verses.dev: स होवाच भगवन्तं वा अहमेभिः सर्वैर्आर्त्विज्यैः पर्यैषिषं भगवतो वा अहमलाभेऽन्यानवृषि ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: sa hovāca bhagavantaṃ vā ahamebhiḥ sarvairārtvijyaiḥ paryaiṣiṣaṃ bhagavato vā ahamalābhe'nyānavṛṣi || 5 ||
verses.trans: The king said: 'Venerable sir, I searched everywhere for you to direct all these sacrificial offices. But when I could not find you, I was forced to choose these other priests.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the king's explanation. The king clarifies that choosing the other priests was not a slight against Uṣasti; it was a choice made out of necessity because the famine had scattered everyone. In Advaita, this reflects how the world functions: people choose secondary options when the ultimate truth seems temporarily hidden from view.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights the king's sincere devotion. He was actively looking for a qualified teacher, proving that his heart was aligned with proper spiritual values. His explanation shows that his intentions were pure, even if his execution lacked full clarity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the king's inability to find Uṣasti initially was part of a divine plan. It allowed the court priests to reach their limits and humble themselves, ensuring that the ultimate truth would be received with deep appreciation by the entire court.

verses.dev: भगवांस्त्वेव मे सर्वैर्आर्त्विज्यैः परिक्रियतामिति तथेत्यथैत एव हि संविदानाः स्तोत्रं स्तुवन्तु केवलं तु मे त्वं धनमात्रां दा इति तथेति ह राजा उवाच ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: bhagavāṃstveva me sarvairārtvijyaiḥ parikriyatāmiti tathetyathaita eva hi saṃvidānāḥ stotraṃ stuvantu kevalaṃ tu me tvaṃ dhanamātrāṃ dā iti tatheti ha rājā uvāca || 6 ||
verses.trans: 'Therefore, venerable sir, please take charge of my entire sacrifice.' Uṣasti replied: 'Let it be so. However, let these same priests continue to sing the hymns under my direction, since they have now been instructed. But you must give me the same amount of wealth that you would have given to all of them combined.' The king replied: 'Venerable sir, it shall be done.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A fascinating ethical and practical resolution. Shankara points out that Uṣasti does not fire the local priests or take away their livelihood. Instead, he includes them under his higher awareness (*saṃvidānāḥ*). His demand for all their salaries combined is not a sign of greed. Rather, it restores his household after the famine and asserts the value of absolute metaphysical wisdom over mechanical labor.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the sage's deep kindness. Instead of humiliating the priests by taking their places, he empowers them to sing under his inner guidance. His demand for wealth is accepted by the king because true spiritual insight is priceless, and a righteous leader happily supports the pillars of wisdom.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the distribution of wealth and duties balances the cosmic order. The priests perform the physical labor of singing, while the sage provides the vitalizing consciousness. This dynamic shows that all elements must work together under the supervision of the supreme guide.

verses.dev: अथ हैनं प्रस्तोतोपससाद प्रस्तोतर्या देवता प्रस्तावमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रस्तोष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति माऽवोचः कतमा सा देवतेति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṃ prastotopasasāda prastotaryā devatā prastāvamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānprastoṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mā'vocaḥ katamā sā devatāiti || 7 ||
verses.trans: Then the Prastotṛ approached him and asked: 'Venerable sir, you told me: "O Prastotṛ, if you sing the introductory praise without knowing the deity that is innerly connected with it, your head will fall off." Which is that deity?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the formal transition back to philosophy. The structural warning has done its job, breaking through the chanter's pride. Now, the chanter returns to the original question with a clean, receptive mind. He asks the core question directly: Who is the living intelligence animating my performance?
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The chanter's question reflects a heart ready for true transformation. He no longer cares about his prestige in front of the king; he simply wants to know the identity of the indwelling light that moves his lips, seeking a deeper relationship with the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva highlights that this question marks the beginning of the correction process. The priest asks for the specific deity in charge of his section, starting a step-by-step climb up the ladder of cosmic administration.

verses.dev: प्राण इति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतानि प्राणमेवाभिसंविशन्ति प्राणमभ्युज्जिहते सैषा देवता प्रस्तावमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रास्तोष्यो मूर्धा तेऽविपत्यत्तथोक्तस्य मयेति ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: prāṇa iti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtāni prāṇamevābhisaṃviśanti prāṇamabhyujjihate saiṣā devatā prastāvamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvāuprāstoṣyo mūrdhā te'vipatya ttathoktasya mayeti || 8 ||
verses.trans: Uṣasti said: 'It is Breath (Prāṇa). Verily, all these created beings merge into Breath alone when they sleep, and they arise from Breath when they wake. This is the deity innerly connected with the Prastāva. If you had sung it without knowing this after my warning, your head would have indeed fallen off.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a profound definition of *Prāṇa*. In this absolute context, it refers to the Supreme Brahman acting as the ultimate life force of the cosmos. During deep sleep (*Suṣupti*), all individual faculties, senses, and thoughts dissolve back into this fundamental life-energy. Upon waking, the entire world emerges from it once more. Because the *Prastāva* is the preliminary, life-giving kickstart of the song, its inner essence is this ultimate cosmic breath.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that *Prāṇa* here represents the Supreme Lord as the absolute sustainer of all living souls. He is the breath within the breath. The universe breathes because He wills it. Identifying the introductory chant with this vital force reminds the singer that every breath they take is a gift from the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this verse identifies the chief deity of life force (*Mukhya Prāṇa*), who acts as the supreme instrument of Lord Viṣṇu. All created beings depend entirely on this primary energy to breathe and exist. This realization grounds the introductory praise in the reality of absolute dependency on God's life-giving power.

verses.dev: अथ हैनमुद्गातोपससोदोद्गातर्या देवताोद्गीथमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्उद्गास्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति माऽवोचः कतमा सा देवतेति ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: atha hainamudgātopasasododgātaryā devatodgīthamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānudgāsyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mā'vocaḥ katamā sā devatāiti || 9 ||
verses.trans: Then the Udgātṛ approached him and asked: 'Venerable sir, you told me: "O Udgātṛ, if you sing the loud chant without knowing the deity that is innerly connected with it, your head will fall off." Which is that deity?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The middle chanter now steps forward, following the same procedure. He sees that his colleague was answered with a universal truth, and he eagerly seeks the secret behind his own assignment—the central Udgītha chant.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Udgātṛ’s approach shows that the spirit of inquiry has spread across the entire assembly. The systematic questioning shows a collective desire to align their performance with true, internal devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that the main chanter asks his question to discover his place in the divine order, preparing the ground for the next verse to introduce the cosmic source of light.

verses.dev: आदित्य इति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतान्यादित्यमुच्चैः सन्तं गायन्ति सैषा देवताोद्गीथमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वानुदगास्यो मूर्धा तेऽविपत्यत्तथोक्तस्य मयेति ॥ १० ॥
verses.iast: āditya iti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtānyādityamuccaiḥ santaṃ gāyanti saiṣā devatāodgīthamanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānudagāsyo mūrdhā te'vipatya ttathoktasya mayeti || 10 ||
verses.trans: Uṣasti said: 'It is the Sun (Āditya). Verily, all these created beings sing the praises of the Sun when he rises high above. This is the deity innerly connected with the Udgītha. If you had sung it without knowing this after my warning, your head would have indeed fallen off.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the transition to the cosmic sphere. *Āditya* (the Sun) represents the supreme, self-luminous Brahman illuminating the external universe, just as Prāṇa animates it internally. The Udgītha is chanted loudly and clearly (*uccaiḥ*), mirroring how the rising sun proclaims its presence across the sky. Meditating on the chant as the sun expands the mind, breaking down the walls between the inner self and the outer cosmos.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the Sun is the visible symbol of the glorious, radiant form of the Supreme Lord (*Sūrya-nārāyaṇa*). He resides at the center of the solar orb, dispelling both physical and spiritual darkness. Linking the main chant to the sun inspires the singer to fill their heart with divine light.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva states that the sun-deity is a direct, radiant instrument of Viṣṇu. All living beings naturally look up to the sun for light, warmth, and time. Recognizing this connection ensures that the central part of the sacrifice honors the independent source of all light and life.

verses.dev: अथ हैनं प्रतिहर्तोपससाद प्रतिहर्र्या देवता प्रतिहारमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रतिहरिष्यसि मूर्धा ते विपतिष्यतीति माऽवोचः कतमा सा देवतेति ॥ ११ ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṃ pratihartopasasāda pratihartaryā devatā pratihāramanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānpratihariṣyasi mūrdhā te vipatiṣyatīti mā'vocaḥ katamā sā devatāiti || 11 ||
verses.trans: Then the Pratihartṛ approached him and asked: 'Venerable sir, you told me: "O Pratihartṛ, if you sing the response without knowing the deity that is innerly connected with it, your head will fall off." Which is that deity?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The final chanter steps forward to complete the circle of learning. This final question ensures that no part of the ritual is left in the dark, bringing full clarity to the entire structure of the Sāman song.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The final question represents the closing movement of this spiritual adjustment. The response singer seeks his own connection, completing the transformation of the court into a unified temple of clean devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that this final step completes the cosmic countdown, preparing the audience for the revelation of the element that sustains the physical world.

verses.dev: अन्नमिति होवाच सर्वाणि ह वा इमानि भूतान्यन्नमेव प्रतिहरमाणानि जीवन्ति सैषा देवता प्रतिहारमन्वायत्ता तां चेदविद्वान्प्रत्यहरिष्यो मूर्धा तेऽविपत्यत्तथोक्तस्य मयेति ॥ १२ ॥
verses.iast: annamiti hovāca sarvāṇi ha vā imāni bhūtānyannameva pratiharamāṇāni jīvanti saiṣā devatā pratihāramanvāyattā tāṃ cedavidvānpratyahariṣyo mūrdhā te'vipatya ttathoktasya mayeti || 12 ||
verses.trans: Uṣasti said: 'It is Food (Anna). Verily, all these created beings live by consuming food alone. This is the deity innerly connected with the Pratihāra response. If you had sung it without knowing this after my warning, your head would have indeed fallen off.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara beautifully closes Section 11 by bringing the cosmic back down to the immediate. *Anna* (Food) represents Brahman as the immediate, physical matter that gathers and holds life together. The word *pratiharamāṇāni* means bringing food to oneself or consuming it. The *Pratihāra* is the closing response that holds the song together. By identifying it with food, the Upanishad loops back to Uṣasti's story: the very beans he begged for are revealed to be a manifestation of the Divine Spirit, showing that nothing lies outside the absolute reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that food is a direct expression of the Lord’s nurturing compassion (*Anugraha*). He sustains the soul internally through grace and externally through food. Remembering this connection during the final response fills the devotee with deep gratitude for the physical and spiritual nourishment provided by God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva concludes that identifying the final response with food honors the complete circle of dependency. The breath moves, the sun shines, and food sustains the physical frame—all operating as orderly instruments under the absolute rule of Viṣṇu. This realization brings the sacrifice into full, perfect alignment with cosmic truth.

verses.dev: अथातः शौव उद्गीथः तद्ध बको दाल्भ्यो ग्लावो वा मैत्रेयः स्वाध्यायमुद्वव्राज ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: athātaḥ śauva udgīthaḥ taddha bako dālbhyo glāvo vā maitreyaḥ svādhyāyamudvavrāja || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now begins the Udgītha of the dogs. The sage Baka, the son of Dalbha—also known as Glāva, the son of Mitrā—went out of the village to a quiet place to study the Veda.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this section follows naturally from the previous section's conclusion, which identified Food (Anna) as the deity of the Pratihāra. This story illustrates a specific meditation designed to secure food. The sage Baka Dālbhya went to a solitary place near a riverbank to chant, seeking a vision of the deities who provide sustenance.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the sage's departure to a quiet place highlights the necessity of solitude for deep meditation. The term 'Udgītha of the dogs' (Śauva Udgītha) indicates a profound allegory where the divine reveals itself in unexpected, humble forms to teach the sage about absolute reliance on God for worldly and spiritual nourishment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the sage was performing severe austerities to understand the secret forces driving the universe. The unusual event that follows is not a mundane occurrence but a divine revelation orchestrated by the Supreme Lord to unveil the esoteric relationship between the life force and the senses.

verses.dev: तस्मै श्वा श्वेताः प्रादुर्बभूव तमन्ये श्वान उपसमेत्योचुर्अन्नं नो भगवानागायत्वशनायाम वा इति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tasmai śvā śvetah prādurbabhūva tamanye śvāna upasametyocurannaṃ no bhagavānāgāyatvaśanāyāma vā iti || 2 ||
verses.trans: A white dog appeared before him. Other dogs gathered around this white dog and said, 'Venerable sir, please sing to obtain food for us, for we are hungry.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara asserts that the 'white dog' is a celestial deity (such as Vāyu, the wind god, or Sūrya, the sun god) who, pleased by the sage's study, took this animal form to bestow grace. The other dogs represent lesser deities or vital energies. The request for food reinforces the Upanishadic theme that the physical vehicle must be nourished to support the pursuit of ultimate knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views the white dog as an embodiment of purity and divine leadership within the material realm. The lesser dogs begging for the chant demonstrate that even the hidden forces of nature must perform sacred actions (chanting) and rely on a higher spiritual authority to receive their sustenance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva provides a brilliant internal allegory. The 'white dog' (śvā śvetah) is Mukhya Prāṇa, the chief vital breath. The word 'śvā' is derived from a root meaning movement or breath. The 'other dogs' are the sensory organs (eyes, ears, etc.). The senses are entirely dependent on the central life force for their energy (food). When the senses are depleted, they instinctively 'beg' the vital breath to revitalize them.

verses.dev: तान्होवाचािह्येव मा प्रातरुपसमीयातेति तद्ध बको दाल्भ्यो ग्लावो वा मैत्रेयः प्रतिपालयांचकार ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tānhovācehaiva mā prātarupasameeyāteti taddha bako dālbhyo glāvo vā maitreyaḥ pratipālayāṃcakāra || 3 ||
verses.trans: The white dog said to them: 'Come back to me here tomorrow morning.' Baka Dālbhya (Glāva Maitreya) kept watch, waiting for their return.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the white dog delays the chant until the next morning because the morning is the auspicious time associated with the sun god (Āditya), who is the ultimate bestower of food and vitality. The sage Baka, realizing that this is a divine teaching unfolding before his eyes, patiently waits, demonstrating the necessary vigilance of a true spiritual seeker.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The delay tests the sincerity of the request and aligns the ritual with the cosmic rhythms of dawn. Baka Dālbhya’s patient observation signifies that spiritual truths are often revealed not instantly upon request, but after a period of attentive waiting and purification.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the dawn represents the awakening of consciousness and the proper time for the life force (Prāṇa) to gather the energies of the sun. Baka's observation of this internal and external cosmic alignment shows his deep qualification to receive the highest knowledge.

verses.dev: ते ह यथैवेदं बहिष्पवमानेन स्तोष्यमाणाः संरब्धाः सर्पन्तीत्येवमाससृपुस्ते ह समुपविश्य हिं चक्रुः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: te ha yathaivedaṃ bahiṣpavamānena stoṣyamāṇāḥ saṃrabdhāḥ sarpantītyevamāsasṛpuste ha samupaviśya hiṃ cakruḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: The dogs returned the next morning. Just as the priests, when they are about to chant the Bahiṣpavamāna hymn, glide along holding each other's robes, the dogs glided along in the same manner. Then they sat down and uttered the syllable 'Hiṃ'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara describes the ritualistic precision of the dogs. In standard Vedic sacrifices, priests walk in a connected line, each holding the robe of the one in front, symbolizing the unbroken continuity of the ritual intent. The dogs perfectly replicate this, confirming they are deities in disguise. The utterance of 'Hiṃ' is the traditional phonetic starting point of the Sāman chant.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights the harmony between the animal forms and the highest Vedic strictures. It teaches that the Divine Order permeates all layers of existence. The connected gliding represents unity of purpose; whether priests in a court or deities in a vision, obtaining the grace of the Lord requires collective harmony and proper procedure.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the 'gliding together' as the perfect synchronization of the bodily senses following the lead of the primary vital breath (Prāṇa). When Prāṇa moves, the senses follow it in an unbroken chain. The 'Hiṃ' sound is the initial vibration of life energy preparing to draw in sustenance from the universe.

verses.dev: ओ३मदामो३म्पिबामो३म्देवो वरुणः प्रजापतिः सविताऽन्नमिहा२हरत अन्नपतेऽन्नमिहा२हरा२ओ३मिति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: o3madāmo3mpibāmo3mdevo varuṇaḥ prajāpatiḥ savitā'nnamihā2harata annapate'nnamihā2harā2o3miti || 5 ||
verses.trans: They sang: 'Om, let us eat! Om, let us drink! Om, may the divine Varuṇa, Prajāpati, and Savitṛ bring us food! O Lord of food, bring food here, bring it here! Om!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara concludes that this chant is the actual 'Udgītha of the dogs'. It invokes the specific deities responsible for sustaining the physical world: Varuṇa (rain/water), Prajāpati (the creator/protector), and Savitṛ (the sun/source of energy). By properly chanting this Sāman with the visualization of these deities, one secures the physical nourishment needed to continue living a life of spiritual inquiry.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views this closing chant as a beautiful prayer of dependence. Even though specific deities are named, the ultimate address is to the 'Lord of food' (Annapati), which is the Supreme Lord acting through Varuṇa, Prajāpati, and Savitṛ. It reinforces that while we must act to sustain ourselves, true nourishment is a direct blessing from God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees this as the ultimate prayer of the life force. Mukhya Prāṇa (the white dog) invokes the cosmic administrators of nature to provide fuel for the body's senses. The repetition of 'bring it here' is the raw, vibrating demand of existence itself. It proves that all action, down to the basic need to eat and drink, is essentially a Vedic ritual managed by the vital breath in service to the Supreme Lord.

verses.dev: अयं वाव लोको हाउकारो वायुर्हाइकारश्चन्द्रमा अथकार आत्मेहकारोऽग्निरीकारः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ayaṃ vāva loko hāukāro vāyurhāikāraścandramā athakāra ātmehakāro'gnirīkāraḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Verily, this physical world is the syllable 'hāu'; the wind is the syllable 'hāi'; the moon is the syllable 'atha'; the self is the syllable 'iha'; and the fire is the syllable 'ī'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that *Stobhas* are musical syllables inserted during the singing of a Sāman chant that do not belong to the original verse text. To prevent these syllables from being treated as meaningless sounds, the Upanishad provides a profound meditation (*upāsana*). The syllable 'hāu' represents this earth because this world is stable and vast, matching the deep resonant tone of the sound. The syllable 'hāi' represents the wind (*Vāyu*) because wind connects everything, and 'hāi' acts as a musical bridge. Each syllable is mapped to a cosmic reality, turning vocal music into an act of meditation on the Absolute.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that these syllables are not random sounds; they are specific vibrations through which the Supreme Lord manifests His cosmic body. By meditating on the syllable 'iha' as the individual self (*ātman*) and 'ī' as fire (*agni*), the chanter realizes that the inner self and the outer elements are harmonized under the single ruler of the cosmos.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that specific cosmic administrators (*devatās*) dwell within these musical sounds. The syllable 'atha' is governed by the deity of the moon, who controls the mind and the tides. This mapping proves that the entire Sāman chant is a precise structural map of the dependent universe under the control of Lord Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: आदित्य ऊकारो निहव एकाकारो विश्वे देवा औहोयिकारः प्रजापतिर्हिकारः प्राणः स्वरोऽन्नं या वाग्विराट् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: āditya ūkāro nihava ekākāro viśve devā auhoyikāraḥ prajāpatirhikāraḥ prāṇaḥ svaro'nnaṃ yā vāgvirāṭ || 2 ||
verses.trans: The sun is the syllable 'ū'; the invocation is the syllable 'e'; the All-Gods are the syllable 'auhoi'; Prajāpati is the syllable 'hiṃ'; breath is the tone 'svara'; food is 'yā'; and speech is 'virāṭ'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the transition to higher cosmic spheres. The sun is mapped to 'ū' because the sun stays high above (*ūrdhva*). The invocation (*nihava*) is mapped to 'e' because it calls upon the gods. The syllable 'hiṃ', which starts the chant, belongs to Prajāpati, the lord of creation, because creation begins with an initial stir of sound. Breath is called 'svara' because it is self-luminous sound itself, and speech is 'virāṭ', the radiant manifest universe.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that these mappings elevate the ritual chanter's awareness from pure music to cosmic devotion. When the chanter sings 'auhoi' or 'hiṃ', they are instructed to see the collective gods and the Creator Himself acting through their vocal cords, reminding them that all talents come from the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that 'svara' (tone) represents the primary life force (*Mukhya Prāṇa*) which gives power to all sounds. Food is mapped to 'yā' because food sustains movement (*yāti*). This shows that the physical and spiritual needs of the chanter are built directly into the structure of the sacred sound vibrations.

verses.dev: अनिरुक्तस्त्रयोदशः स्तोभः संचरो हुंकारः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: aniruktastrayodaśaḥ stobhaḥ saṃcaro huṃkāraḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: The thirteenth Stobha syllable is 'huṃ'. It is undefinable, variable, and all-pervading.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a profound analysis of *anirukta* (the undefinable). The thirteenth syllable, 'huṃ', represents the unmanifest, supreme Brahman (*Nirguṇa Brahman*). Because it cannot be limited by a single name, form, or definition, it is called variable and all-pervading (*saṃcara*). It is the foundational background sound from which all other specific syllables emerge and into which they return.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that 'huṃ' represents the mysterious, infinite nature of the Supreme Lord. While the other twelve syllables represent clear, visible aspects of creation, 'huṃ' reminds the devotee that God always exceeds human language and definitions, inspiring a sense of awe during the chant.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, 'huṃ' is the specific seed sound (*bīja*) of the independent Lord of all forces. It is called variable because it moves through all forms of matter and spirit, holding the entire universe together while remaining untouched by material changes.

verses.dev: दुग्धेऽस्मै वाग्दोहं यो वाचो दोहोऽन्नवानन्नादो भवति य एतामेवं साम्नामुपनिषदं वेदोपनिषदं वेद इति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: dugdhe'smai vāgdohaṃ yo vāco doho'nnavānannādo bhavati ya etāmevaṃ sāmnāmupaniṣadaṃ vedopaniṣadaṃ veda iti || 4 ||
verses.trans: For him, speech yields its milk, which is the full fruit of speech itself. He becomes rich in food and a consumer of food—he who knows this secret doctrine of the Sāman syllables, yea, he who knows this secret doctrine.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara beautifully closes the first chapter of the Chandogya Upanishad. The phrase 'speech yields its milk' is a metaphor: just as a cow yields milk, the power of speech yields all desired spiritual and material fruits to the knower. Knowing the *upaniṣadaṃ* (the secret inner meaning) of these sounds transforms the chanter from a mechanical singer into a realized sage who sees the absolute Brahman in every vibration. The repetition marks the conclusion of Chapter 1.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views the final verse as a promise of both physical and spiritual fulfillment. A devotee who understands these inner secrets receives the grace of the Lord through speech. They are blessed with physical food for the body and spiritual truth to nourish the soul, ensuring complete harmony in life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva concludes that this verse establishes the ultimate reward of correct knowledge. Understanding the cosmic hierarchy behind the Stobha syllables grants the practitioner mastery over their speech and senses. They become an 'eater of food' (*annādaḥ*), meaning they are no longer controlled by material nature, but instead master it through their clear alignment with the Supreme Lord Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: ॐ । समस्तस्य खलु साम्न उपासनं साधु । यत्खलु साधु तत्सामेत्याचक्षते यदसाधु तदसामेति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: oṃ | samastasya khalu sāmna upāsanaṃ sādhu | yatkhalu sādhu tatsāmetyācakṣate yadasādhu tadasāmeti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Om. Meditation on the entire Sāman is excellent. Whatever is good and righteous, people call it Sāman; whatever is not good or unrighteous, they call it asāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes the transition in text structure: Chapter 1 focused on meditating on individual parts of the chant, like the Udgītha or specific musical syllables. Chapter 2 elevates this to *samastasya*—the entire, undivided Sāman. Shankara links the word *Sāman* etymologically to *sādhu* (what is wholesome, beautiful, and ethically right). In common speech, when a situation is balanced and flawless, people say 'it is Sāman'. This verse teaches that meditating on the Sāman means meditating on the pervasive, cosmic thread of goodness and harmony that underlies reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that 'goodness' (*sādhu*) is an inherent attribute of the Supreme Divine Body. The whole Sāman chant represents the flawless divine order (*Dharma*) instituted by God. Meditating on the entire Sāman allows the devotee to see that all moral goodness, cosmic balance, and aesthetic beauty are expressions of the Divine Will working through creation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that *Sāman* means that which brings ultimate balance or peace. The Supreme Lord is called Sāman because He is the source of all ultimate goodness. The term *asāman* refers to actions or states devoid of His presence or alignment. Meditating on the whole Sāman means consciously training the mind to recognize the righteous, sustaining power of Viṣṇu in every aspect of life.

verses.dev: तदुतोताप्याहुः साम्नैनमुपागादिति साधुनैनमुपागादित्येव तदाहुः । असाम्नाइ नमुपागादित्यसाधुनैनमुपागादित्येव तदाहुः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tadutotāpyāhuḥ sāmnainamupāgāditi sādhunainamupāgādityeva tadāhuḥ | asāmnāi namupāgādityasādhunainamupāgādityeva tadāhuḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: Hence, people say about a man who approaches with a good cause: 'He has approached with Sāman,' meaning he has approached with goodness. If he approaches with a bad cause, they say: 'He has approached with asāman,' meaning he has approached with unrighteousness.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara illustrates this with everyday human behavior to prove that the equation between Sāman and goodness is embedded in human consciousness. When someone acts with kindness, fairness, or reconciliation, it is linguistically called 'Sāman'. This worldly consensus serves as a launchpad for spiritual meditation: if human goodness is called Sāman, then cosmic, unbroken goodness is the ultimate reality of the Sāman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the interpersonal aspect of this verse. Righteous approach, gentle speech, and noble intentions reflect the qualities of a soul aligned with God. The verse uses social relationships to show that spiritual meditation must reflect practical righteousness (*sadācāra*) in daily actions.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets 'approaching with Sāman' as arriving with an internal disposition aligned with truth and scriptural duty. When a person's intellect is clear and dedicated to the Lord, their actions naturally yield harmony, whereas a mind tracking selfish desires brings *asāman*—chaos and friction.

verses.dev: अथोताप्याहुः साम नो बतेति यत्साधु भवति साधुवत्येवेमाहुः । असाम नो बतेति यदसाधु भवति असाधुवत्येवेमाहुः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: athotāpyāhuḥ sāma no bateti yatsādhu bhavati sādhuvatyevemāhuḥ | asāma no bateti yadasāhu bhavati asādhuvatyevemāhuḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: They also say: 'Oh, this is Sāman for us!' when something good happens, meaning 'This is excellent!' And they say: 'Oh, this is asāman for us!' when something bad happens, meaning 'This is terrible!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that this verse demonstrates how humans instinctively identify joy, prosperity, and harmony with Sāman, and suffering or disruption with its absence. The goal of this pedagogical repetition is to firmly anchor the practitioner’s understanding: the Sāman is not just musical notes sung by priests; it is the universal matrix of order, beauty, and auspiciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that when people experience unexpected blessings or relief from suffering, they exclaim it is 'Sāman'. This teaches the seeker to recognize all joyful and harmonious events as direct gifts from the Divine Ruler, training the soul in constant gratitude during the meditative path.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that worldly joy is a tiny, reflection of the absolute bliss of the Lord. When people say 'this is Sāman for us,' they are experiencing a brief flash of harmony. The text uses these moments of everyday happiness to motivate the seeker to seek the permanent, unshakeable Sāman—the Lord Himself.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवं विद्वान् साधु सामेल्युपास्तेऽभ्याशो ह यदेनं साधवो धर्मा आ च गच्छेयुरुप च नमेयुः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etadevaṃ vidvān sādhu sāmelyupāste'bhyāśo ha yadenaṃ sādhavo dharmā ā ca gaccheyurupa ca nameyuḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this and meditates on the Sāman as goodness—to him quickly come all righteous deeds, and they bend down to serve him.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains the *Phala* (fruit) of this holistic meditation. For a sage who looks past the physical syllables and sees the Sāman as the universal principle of goodness, an internal transformation occurs. Noble qualities and virtuous thoughts (*sādhavo dharmāḥ*) naturally flock to his mind without intense manual effort. Because his mind has become identical with universal harmony, righteous actions seek him out as their natural home.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: According to this school, when a devotee meditates on the Sāman as the flawless, loving nature of God, divine grace purifies their character. Virtues do not just arrive; they 'bend down' (*upa-nameyuḥ*), meaning they become entirely natural and effortless habits of the soul, serving the devotee on their path toward total liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes that for a realized soul who understands how the independent Lord manages all elements of goodness, the cosmic laws (*dharmas*) bend to their spiritual will. Nature itself aligns to assist a true devotee who sees the universe as an ordered temple of Lord Viṣṇu, ensuring that their life becomes an unbroken flow of service.

verses.dev: लोकेषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत पृथिवी हिंकारोऽग्निः प्रस्तावोऽन्तरिक्षमुद्गीथ आदित्यः प्रतिहारो द्यौर्निधनमित्यूर्ध्वेषु ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: lokeṣu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāsīta pṛthivī hiṃkāro'gniḥ prastāvo'ntarikṣamudgītha ādityaḥ pratihāro dyaurnidhanamityūrdhveṣu || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the fivefold Sāman as manifest in the worlds. The earth is the introductory syllable 'Hiṃ'; the fire is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the intermediate space is the loud chant (Udgītha); the sun is the response (Pratihāra); and the sky/heaven is the finale (Nidhana). This is the meditation on the worlds in an ascending order.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that the text now initiates the practical method of cosmic expansion through the fivefold (*pañcavidha*) schema of the Sāman chant. The five parts are the *Hiṃkāra* (vocal initialization), *Prastāva* (introductory body), *Udgītha* (the principal core), *Pratihāra* (the holding back or response), and *Nidhana* (the concluding shelter). This physical universe is mapped from the ground up: the solid earth matches the foundational *Hiṃkāra*. Fire, which blazes upon the earth, matches *Prastāva*. The vast intermediate space (*Antarikṣa*) matches the high, hollow resonance of the *Udgītha*. The sun acts as the *Pratihāra* because it draws human eyes and collects waters, and heaven (*Dyaus*) acts as the *Nidhana* because it serves as the final upper boundary where everything seems to find its ultimate rest.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that this vertical mapping (*ūrdhveṣu*) shows that the material universes are not chaotic spaces but are organized as a structured temple of the Supreme Creator. By aligning the parts of the song with the layers of the worlds, the devotee is trained to view the entire physical creation as a living, singing instrument entirely dependent on the Divine Will.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that each cosmic layer is governed by a specific administrator working directly under the orders of Lord Viṣṇu. Meditating on the fivefold Sāman creates an inner understanding of how the divine administrative order runs from the lowest earthly elements to the highest heavenly fields, establishing the complete dependency of all worlds on the Supreme Lord.

verses.dev: अथावृत्तेषु द्यौर्हिंकार आदित्यः प्रस्तावोऽन्तरिक्षमुद्गीथोऽग्निः प्रतिहारः पृथिवी निधनम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: athāvṛtteṣu dyaurhiṃkāra ādityaḥ prastāvo'ntarikṣamudgītho'gniḥ pratihāraḥ pṛthivī nidhanam || 2 ||
verses.trans: Now follows the meditation on the worlds in a descending, returning order. The sky/heaven is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the sun is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the intermediate space is the loud chant (Udgītha); the fire is the response (Pratihāra); and the earth is the finale (Nidhana).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the reversal of the meditation (*āvṛtteṣu*). Having expanded the mind upward to the heavens, the seeker must now trace the energy back down to earth. This ensures that the lower world is not dismissed as false or dirty; instead, it is integrated into the same spiritual vibration. Heaven becomes the starting point (*Hiṃkāra*), and the solid earth acts as the ultimate resting place and shelter (*Nidhana*), proving that the non-dual consciousness is equally present in both the source and the manifestation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that the returning order represents the downpouring of divine grace and cosmic energy from the higher realms back to the earthly plane. Tracing the Sāman downwards reminds the seeker that the Lord descends to meet the soul, making the physical earth a sacred vessel for divine activity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the descending order represents the cosmic cycle of dissolution and material creation. It highlights the dual nature of the Lord's control: He rules the ascent of spiritual evolution and the descent of material manifestation. Seeing the earth as the final shelter (*Nidhana*) reminds the devotee that all physical bodies are ultimately returned to their elemental building blocks by divine command.

verses.dev: कल्पन्ते हास्मै लोका ऊर्ध्वाश्चावृत्ताश्च य एतदेवं विद्वाँल्लोकेषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: kalpante hāsmai lokā ūrdhvāścāvṛttāśca ya etadevaṃ vidvāँllokeṣu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāste || 3 ||
verses.trans: The worlds—both ascending and descending—become supportive and fully available to him who, knowing this truth, meditates on the fivefold Sāman across the cosmic worlds.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains the phrase *kalpante hāsmai* (they serve or become available to him). For an ordinary person, the physical worlds are obstacles, limitations, and sources of fear. But for a sage whose consciousness flows equally through the ascending and descending paths of the Sāman, the environment ceases to be a limitation. The worlds conform to his expanded awareness, losing their capacity to restrict or bind his inner freedom.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights that when a devotee recognizes the Lord as the underlying thread of all dimensions, those worlds naturally welcome and support him. The seeker experiences no hostility from any aspect of nature, as both the spiritual paths and the physical world yield their best fruits to one who honors the indwelling master.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this final declaration confirms the mastery over material nature granted to a true servant of God. By knowing how the independent Lord coordinates the ascending and descending channels of cosmic power, the devotee gains unshakeable protection, ensuring success across both worldly and afterlife states.

verses.dev: वृष्टौ पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत पुरोवातो हिंकारो मेघो जायते स प्रस्तावो वर्षति स उद्गीथो विद्योतते स्तनयति स प्रतिहारः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: vṛṣṭau pañcavidaṃ sāmopāsieta purovāto hiṃkāro megho jāyate sa prastāvo varṣati sa udgītho vidyotate stanayati sa pratihāraḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the fivefold Sāman in rain. The forward wind that blows before a storm is the introductory syllable 'Hiṃ'; when the cloud is formed, it is the introductory praise (Prastāva); when it rains, it is the loud chant (Udgītha); and when it lightens and thunders, it is the response (Pratihāra).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that after mapping the stable physical worlds, the text moves to the vital natural process that links heaven and earth: rain (*vṛṣṭi*). This meditation coordinates the sequence of a storm with the musical timeline of the chant. The *Hiṃkāra* represents the early, sweeping wind (*purovātaḥ*) because it signals the arrival of the event. The gathering of the cloud is the *Prastāva*, showing structural preparation. The actual downpour is mapped to the *Udgītha* because rain is the primary, vital center of the whole meteorological cycle, just as *Udgītha* is the heart of the song.
