The flashes of lightning and crashes of thunder represent the *Pratihāra* because they spread across the dark sky, collecting and holding human attention.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that this meditation trains the devotee to look past seasonal weather and see the deliberate hand of the Divine. The transition from a dry wind to dense clouds, followed by nourishing rain and majestic thunder, demonstrates the step-by-step display of the Lord's creative majesty, turning a passing storm into a lesson in active worship.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya states that the cosmic controllers of wind, cloud, moisture, and lightning function in a strict order under the direction of the Supreme Lord Viṣṇu. Mapping these stages to the five movements of the Sāman shows that natural laws are synchronized by an independent divine intelligence, reinforcing that nature relies entirely on God to thrive.

verses.dev: उद्गृह्णाति तन्निधनं वर्षति हास्मै वर्षयति ह य एतदेवं विद्वान्वृष्टौ पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: udgṛhṇāti tannidhanaṃ varṣati hāsmai varṣayati ha ya etadevaṃ vidvān vṛṣṭau pañcavidaṃ sāmopāste || 2 ||
verses.trans: When the storm ceases and the clouds lift, that is the finale (Nidhana). It rains for him, and he can bring rain for others, whoever knows this truth and meditates on the fivefold Sāman in rain.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the final stage: *udgṛhṇāti* (the gathering up or cessation of the rain). When the sky clears and the storm withdraws into its unmanifest state, it represents the *Nidhana*—the concluding shelter where all active energies return to peace. For a sage who realizes that the entire water cycle is a sacred Sāman song, nature responds to his internal alignment. He is no longer at the mercy of chaotic elements; his mind is beautifully synchronized with the cosmic source.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the blessing of service built into this verse. A devotee who meditates on rain as a direct expression of the Lord’s life-giving compassion becomes a vessel for that same blessing. They are granted both personal abundance (*varṣati hāsmai*) and the spiritual power to uplift and nourish their entire community.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that understanding the divine hierarchy behind the rain cycle brings specific spiritual powers (*siddhis*) as a byproduct of genuine devotion. By knowing how the independent Lord governs the life-giving waters through His cosmic administrators, the devotee's prayers gain immense clarity, allowing them to channel blessings to the world around them.

verses.dev: अप्सु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत मेघो यत्संप्लवते स हिंकारो यद्वर्षति स प्रस्तावो याः प्राच्यः स्यन्दन्ते स उद्गीथो याः प्रतीच्यः स प्रतिहारः समुद्रो निधनम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: apsu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāsieta megho yatsaṃplavate sa hiṃkāro yadvarṣati sa prastāvo yāḥ prācyaḥ syandante sa udgītho yāḥ pratīcyaḥ sa pratihāraḥ samudro nidhanam || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the fivefold Sāman in all waters. When the clouds gather together, that is the introductory syllable 'Hiṃ'; when it rains, that is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the rivers that flow eastward are the loud chant (Udgītha); the rivers that flow westward are the response (Pratihāra); and the ocean is the finale (Nidhana).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that while the previous section dealt specifically with the active event of rain (*vṛṣṭi*), this section expands into *Apsu*—the element of water in all its planetary forms. The gathering of mist and vapor into thick clouds is the *Hiṃkāra*, representing the origin point of the cycle. The falling rain is the *Prastāva*. The rivers flowing toward the east (the direction of the rising sun and traditional spiritual dawn) are mapped to the *Udgītha*, representing the primary forward movement of life energy. The rivers flowing toward the west are mapped to the *Pratihāra*. Finally, the vast ocean (*Samudra*) acts as the *Nidhana* because it is the ultimate destination, boundary, and resting place where all distinct flowing rivers lose their names and forms, merging into a single watery mass.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this meditation traces the journey of individual souls back to their divine source. Just as rivers flow in different directions based on geography but ultimately find absolute peace and union in the deep ocean, individual souls (*jīvas*) follow diverse pathways in life but are destined to find their true rest and shelter (*Nidhana*) in the infinite ocean of the Supreme Lord's presence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya observes that the waters are guided by distinct cosmic controllers under the direct command of the Lord. The division of rivers into eastern and western streams shows that the physical world is explicitly organized to distribute life across continents. The ocean represents the supreme collecting pool of material energy, proving that all diverse physical systems are bound together in a beautifully managed network designed by an independent divine intelligence.

verses.dev: न हाप्सु प्रमीयते य एतदेवं विद्वानप्सु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: na hāpsu pramīyate ya etadevaṃ vidvānapsu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāste || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this truth and meditates on the fivefold Sāman in all waters does not perish in water; he becomes master of water.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies the immediate reward (*Phala*) of this meditation: *na hāpsu pramīyate* (he does not die or drown in water). This can be understood both literally and metaphorically. Literally, a sage whose consciousness is perfectly synchronized with the universal spirit of water develops an intuitive alignment with nature, shielding him from watery disasters. Metaphorically, water represents the shifting, turbulent stream of relative existence (*saṃsāra*). One who sees the supreme Sāman order operating through all changes will never drown in the emotional or material currents of life.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views this verse as an assurance of divine protection. Water is the primordial element of life and cleansing. A devotee who contemplates the Lord as the inner ruler of the world's waters is blessed with fearlessness. They transcend the fear of physical drowning and, more importantly, are protected from slipping into spiritual stagnation, as the living waters of grace keep their heart completely pure.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that a practitioner who understands how the independent Lord rules over the water element through His cosmic administrative deities gains a unique mastery over material nature. The elements cease to act as hostile forces. Because the devotee treats water as a sacred expression of divine management, they receive its support across their daily duties and spiritual journeys, ensuring they emerge victorious over the limitations of the physical world.

verses.dev: ऋतुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत वसन्तो हिंकारो ग्रीष्मः प्रस्तावो वर्षा उद्गीथः शरत्प्रतिहारो हेमन्तो निधनम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ṛtuṣu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāsieta vasanto hiṃkāro grīṣmaḥ prastāvo varṣā udgīthaḥ śaratpratihāro hemanto nidhanam || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the fivefold Sāman in the seasons. Spring is the introductory syllable 'Hiṃ'; Summer is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the Rainy Season is the loud chant (Udgītha); Autumn is the response (Pratihāra); and Winter is the finale (Nidhana).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that after mapping the physical layout of spatial worlds, rain, and planetary waters, the Upanishad directs the seeker's attention to the dimension of Time through the annual cycle of seasons (*ṛtus*). The five movements of the chant are mapped onto this natural wheel. Spring (*Vasanta*) is the *Hiṃkāra* because it marks the initialization, rebirth, and opening of the agricultural and natural year. Summer (*Grīṣma*) represents the *Prastāva*, a period of intensifying solar heat and preparation. The Rainy Season (*Varṣā*) is mapped to the *Udgītha* because it is the vital, life-sustaining climax that brings crops to fruition, matching the central importance of *Udgītha* in the liturgy. Autumn (*Śarat*) is the *Pratihāra*, a time of harvest and collection. Winter (*Hemanta*) acts as the *Nidhana* because it brings a withdrawal of energy, a freeze in outer growth, and a quiet state of rest where nature returns to its seeds.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this sequence as the rhythmic heartbeat of the Supreme Lord's temporal manifestation. Time is not a destructive, blind force; it is an ordered melody. By identifying the seasonal shifts with the components of the Sāman, the devotee recognizes that whether life is in a phase of abundant growth (Rainy/Udgītha) or frozen dormancy (Winter/Nidhana), the entire process is a harmonious expression of divine administration working for the ultimate refinement of the soul.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya observes that time is entirely dependent on the independent will of Lord Viṣṇu, who commands specific cosmic deities (*Kālātmaka Devatās*) to govern each season. The transitioning of seasons ensures that the material world cycles smoothly between production and rest. Meditating on this fivefold framework prevents the soul from lamenting structural changes like aging or hardship, as all shifts are seen as parts of a grand, divinely managed arrangement.

verses.dev: कल्पन्ते हास्मै ऋतव ऋतुमान्भवति य एतदेवं विद्वानृतुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: kalpante hāsmai ṛtava ṛtumānbhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānṛtuṣu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāste || 2 ||
verses.trans: The seasons become supportive and fully available to him, and he becomes rich in the virtues of the seasons—he who knows this truth and meditates on the fivefold Sāman across the seasons.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the precise reward (*Phala*): *kalpante hāsmai ṛtavaḥ* (the seasons serve or conform to him). For an unillumined mind, time is an external tyrant that brings decay, anxiety, and unfulfilled desires. However, for a sage who meditates on the underlying Sāman harmony within time, the seasons lose their hostile or binding power. He becomes *ṛtumān*—master of the seasonal laws, internally stable and undisturbed by outer fluctuations, experiencing the eternal present.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that being 'rich in the virtues of the seasons' means the devotee's life becomes entirely fruitful and balanced. Just as each season brings its unique gift to the earth, the devotee's character flourishes with appropriate spiritual virtues at every stage of their life, completely aligned with the divine rhythm.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that an enlightened practitioner who understands how the independent Lord coordinates the wheel of time through His administrative deities is granted an unshakeable state of peace. They are freed from the suffering caused by seasonal changes or worldly transitions, as their intellect is securely anchored in the eternal, unchanging nature of God.

verses.dev: पशुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत अजा हिंकारोऽवर्यः प्रस्तावो गाव उद्गीथः अश्वाः प्रतिहारः पुरुषो निधनम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: paśuṣu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāsieta ajā hiṃkāro'vayaḥ prastāvo gāva udgīthaḥ aśvāḥ pratihāraḥ puruṣo nidhanam || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the fivefold Sāman in animals. Goats are the introductory syllable 'Hiṃ'; sheep are the introductory praise (Prastāva); cows are the loud chant (Udgītha); horses are the response (Pratihāra); and man is the finale (Nidhana).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that after meditating on cosmic spaces, weather patterns, and the flow of time, the student is directed to the biological sphere of living creatures (*paśus*). Goats (*ajāḥ*) are mapped to *Hiṃkāra* because they are often at the forefront of herds and their vocalizations resemble an opening sound. Sheep (*avayaḥ*) represent the *Prastāva*, immediately following in sacrificial and agricultural order. Cows (*gāvaḥ*) are identified with the *Udgītha* because they are the most valuable, primary, and life-sustaining animals in Vedic culture, just as *Udgītha* is the foundational essence of the Sāman chant. Horses (*aśvāḥ*) match the *Pratihāra* because of their swiftness and power to carry or draw back weight. Finally, man (*puruṣaḥ*) acts as the *Nidhana* because he is the shelter, protector, and master of all these domestic animals, serving as the conscious destination where the livestock find their purpose and safety.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that the living kingdoms are interconnected expressions of the indwelling soul (*antaryāmin*). This hierarchy shows that humans carry a unique responsibility. By mapping man as the *Nidhana* (finale/shelter) for the animal kingdom, the text teaches that human consciousness must act as a protective, loving guardian for all living beings, reflecting the ultimate shelter that God provides to all creation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that every animal group is guided by specific minor deities working under the sovereign governance of Lord Viṣṇu. The clear division of animals based on utility and nature demonstrates a deliberate cosmic order. Meditating on this fivefold distribution helps the seeker perceive that livestock are not mere objects for selfish human consumption, but are sacred components of a divinely managed network.

verses.dev: भवन्ति हास्य पशवः पशुमान्भवति य एतदेवं विद्वान्पशुषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: bhavanti hāsya paśavaḥ paśumānbhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānpaśuषु पञ्चविधं सामोपास्ते || 2 ||
verses.trans: Animals belong to him, and he becomes rich in animals—he who knows this truth and meditates on the fivefold Sāman across animals.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the outcome (*Phala*): *bhavanti hāsya paśavaḥ* (animals come to belong to him). For an ordinary person, keeping animals requires constant physical labor, anxiety, and defensive measures. For a sage who meditates on the underlying Sāman harmony within the animal kingdom, an internal spiritual kinship forms with all living things. Animals instinctively feel safe around such a realized soul, and he becomes *paśumān*—wealthy not just in physical herds, but in his deep, non-dual connection with the life force animating all creatures.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that being 'rich in animals' represents prosperity that is completely free from greed or exploitation. When a devotee views animals as sacred expressions of the Lord's wealth, their livelihood becomes blessed, peaceful, and abundant. Nature naturally cooperates with a soul that treats all living beings with divine reverence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that an enlightened practitioner who understands how the independent Lord coordinates the animal kingdoms through His administrative deities receives material stability as a natural byproduct of their meditation. Their heart is freed from fear of loss, as their focus remains fixed on the true owner and sustainer of all biological life—the Supreme Lord.

verses.dev: प्राणेषु पञ्चविधं सामोपासीत प्राणो हिंकारो वाक्प्रस्तावश्चक्षुरुद्गीथः श्रोत्रं प्रतिहारोऽपानो निधनम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: prāṇeṣu pañcavidaṃ sāmopāsieta prāṇo hiṃkāro vākprastāvaścakṣurudgīthaḥ śrotraṃ pratihāro'pāno nidhanam || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the fivefold Sāman in the vital breaths. The breath that moves outward (Prāṇa) is the introductory syllable 'Hiṃ'; speech (Vāk) is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the eye (Cakṣus) is the loud chant (Udgītha); the ear (Śrotram) is the response (Pratihāra); and the downward breath (Apāna) is the finale (Nidhana).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that having purified the external senses by finding the Sāman in the worlds, rain, waters, seasons, and animals, the Upanishad now turns inward to the bodily life-forces (*prāṇas*). The primary forward breath (*prāṇaḥ*) is mapped to *Hiṃkāra* because it is the initial gateway and first indicator of organic life. Speech (*vāk*) is the *Prastāva* because all formal introductions, praises, and ritual announcements are brought forth by speech. The eye (*cakṣus*) is identified with the *Udgītha* because of its supreme, dominant position among the senses—it reveals the light of the sun and commands the highest informational value, matching the pre-eminent status of *Udgītha*. The ear (*śrotram*) represents the *Pratihāra* because it collects sound waves coming from all directions and holds them in check. Finally, the downward, anchoring breath (*apānaḥ*) acts as the *Nidhana* because it serves as the bodily repository and lower stabilizing center where waste and energy are safely returned and collected.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this somatic mapping to show that the human body is a functioning temple of the Divine Engine (*antaryāmin*). The organs of sense and action do not belong to the ego; they are functional instruments vibrating in a coordinated, sacred hymn. Recognizing the Sāman inside the vital breaths transforms ordinary biological maintenance into a profound, continuous act of silent liturgical worship.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the internal physiological functions are administered by specific *Devatās* operating under the supreme authority of the principal life-force, Prāṇa, who itself acts as the prime minister to Lord Viṣṇu. The division of breaths and senses highlights the dependent nature of the individual self (*jīva*). Meditating on this fivefold setup destroys the false pride of 'I-ness' and 'my-ness' relative to the body, keeping the devotee anchored in the reality that God is the ultimate animator of human life.

verses.dev: सर्वं हास्यैतदायुर्भवति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या प्राणान्प्रति मुञ्चेत् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sarvaṃ hāsyaitadāyurbhavati jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā prāṇānprati muñcet || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this truth and meditates on the fivefold Sāman in the vital breaths attains a full lifespan, lives resplendently, becomes great with progeny and cattle, and grows magnificent in spiritual renown. One should never look down upon or deprecate the vital breaths; that is the sacred vow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the precise somatic reward (*Phala*). To live *sarvaṃ āyuḥ* means completing a healthy, unimpeded human life (traditionally one hundred years), free from premature decay. Because the mind is synchronized with the natural flow of bodily vitality, the practitioner lives radiantly (*jyok*). Furthermore, the verse institutes a life-long behavioral vow: *prāṇān na nindet* (one should never deprecate or curse the biological processes or systems of life). Rejecting, hating, or insulting the body's natural mechanics is a violation of the cosmic Sāman layout.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school emphasizes that the spiritual reputation (*kīrti*) mentioned here is the fragrance of a holy life, entirely dedicated to the Lord. The vow to never despise the vital forces ensures that the devotee maintains deep gratitude for the physical frame. They do not abuse it through extreme asceticism or careless indulgence, treating it instead as an essential, sanctified vehicle given by God to achieve liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the physical and lineage blessings (children, livestock, fame) are manifestations of the grace of Mukhyaprāṇa (the chief life-force) and Lord Viṣṇu. The strict vow to avoid insulting the vital breaths means recognizing that every intake of breath is an act of divine mercy. This constant awareness protects the seeker from depression, despair, and spiritual negligence, granting them full vitality to serve God through their entire allotted time on earth.

verses.dev: अथ सप्तविधस्य वाचि सप्तविधं सामोपासीत यत्किंच वाचो हुमिति स हिंकारो यत्प्रतीति स प्रस्तावो यदेति स आदिः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha saptavidhasya vāci saptavidhaṃ sāmopāseeta yatkiṃca vāco humiti sa hiṃkāro yatpratīti sa prastāvo yadeti sa ādiḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now begins the instruction on the sevenfold Sāman. One should meditate on the sevenfold Sāman in Speech. Whatever in speech is represented by the sound 'Hum' is the Hiṃkāra; whatever begins with 'Pra' is the Prastāva; and whatever begins with 'Ā' is the Ādi.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that the text introduces a structural upgrade here, moving from the five structural parts of the chant to a sevenfold (*saptavidha*) classification. The two additional parts inserted into this liturgical sequence are the *Ādi* (the initial letter or syllable, usually *Om*) and the *Upadrava* (the penultimate chant movement leading to the finale). In this verse, Speech (*Vāk*) is analyzed by its phonetic and intent-based structure. The guttural sound *Hum*, which signifies a clearing of the throat or initial intent to articulate, represents the *Hiṃkāra*. The prefix *Pra-*, which initiates formal statements or praise, corresponds to the *Prastāva*. The syllable *Ā-*, which marks the expansive entry into core concepts or the sacred syllable *Om*, represents the *Ādi*.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that speech is a progressive disclosure of meaning, mirroring how the Supreme Lord gradually manifests the universe from unmanifest sound (*parā vāk*) down to articulate syllables (*vaikharī*). The sevenfold division forces the practitioner to slow down their perception of language. Every utterance becomes an orderly, liturgical ladder leading consciousness back to its source.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya observes that the acoustic components of language are controlled by specific *Devatās* operating in a strict hierarchy under Vāk and Prāṇa. Sound is not a random byproduct of vocal cord friction; it is a precisely engineered instrument of divine communication. By meditating on these phonetic milestones (*Hum*, *Pra*, *Ā*), the seeker learns to respect the sacred nature of vocal expression.

verses.dev: यदुदिति स उद्गीथो यत्प्रतीति स प्रतिहारो यदुपेति स उपद्रवो यन्निनीति तन्निधनम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: yaduditi sa udgītho yatpratīti sa pratihāro yadupeti sa upadravo yannineeti tannidhanam || 2 ||
verses.trans: Whatever begins with 'Ut' is the Udgītha; whatever begins with 'Prati' is the Pratihāra; whatever begins with 'Upa' is the Upadrava; and whatever ends in an absolute termination or conclusion is the Nidhana.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the remaining four movements of the sevenfold chant. The syllable *Ut*, which represents elevation, upward movement, and vocal resonance, matches the *Udgītha*. The prefix *Prati-*, indicating a counter-response or holding back, represents the *Pratihāra*. The prefix *Upa-*, meaning 'approaching closely' or drawing near to the end, maps to the newly introduced *Upadrava*. Finally, any phonetic termination (*ni-*) or complete quietude where sound dissolves back into silence is the *Nidhana*. This covers the complete anatomy of a spoken sentence or thought.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school suggests that the expansion into seven parts demonstrates that as spiritual maturity deepens, the soul perceives more intricate layers of the Lord's design. The transition from *Upadrava* (approaching the end) to *Nidhana* (the final rest) symbolizes the individual soul's ultimate path—gradually winding down its worldly involvements to enter the permanent, tranquil shelter of the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva focuses on the word *Ut* as a pointer to *Uttama* (the Supreme Being, Puruṣottama). All speech components that elevate the mind toward God are inherently *Udgītha*. The sevenfold meditation cleanses human speech of profane or useless gossip, transforming every conversation into an orderly sequence that resolves in *Nidhana*—the absolute resting state in the will of Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: दुग्धेऽस्मै वाग्दोहं यो वाचो दोहोऽन्नवानन्नादो भवति य एतदेवं विद्वान्वांछि सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: dugdhe'smai vāgdohaṃ yo vāco doho'nnavānannādo bhavati ya etadevaṃ vidvānvāñchi saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste || ३ ||
verses.trans: Speech yields its secret essence—which is the milk of wisdom and truth—to him, and he becomes rich in nourishment and a devourer of food, he who knows this truth and meditates on the sevenfold Sāman in Speech.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara interprets the phrase *dugdhe'smai vāgdoham* as speech yielding its milk. Just as a cow yields nourishing milk, speech yields its ultimate fruit—truth, flawless articulation, and scriptural insight—to the sage. He becomes *annādaḥ* (a devourer of food), which metaphorically means his digestive and assimilation capacities—both physically and intellectually—are perfectly balanced. He is no longer consumed by external worldly influences; rather, he cleanly digests experience.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that the milk of speech is nothing other than the capacity to sing praises to the Lord without obstruction. A tongue purified by this sevenfold meditation loses the ability to lie, slander, or complain. It becomes a source of spiritual nourishment for everyone who hears it.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the worldly benefits of sustenance and robust health are gifts from the deity Sarasvatī (governing speech) acting under the Lord's direction. When speech is aligned with the sevenfold Sāman, the practitioner's words carry an innate spiritual authority and power (*Vāk-Siddhi*), ensuring their material and spiritual environment remains stable and well-supported.

verses.dev: अथ खल्वमुमादित्यं सप्तविधं सामोपासीत सर्वदा समस्तेन समस्तन्मां प्रति मां प्रतीति सर्वेण समः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalvamumādityaṃ saptavidhaṃ sāmopāseeta sarvadā samastena samastanmāṃ prati māṃ pratīti sarveṇa samaḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Next, one should meditate on the sun as the sevenfold Sāman. The sun is always the same (Sama) to everyone, because everyone thinks: 'He faces me, he faces me!' Thus, being equal to all, the sun is the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara highlights a beautiful psychological and cosmic observation here. The word *Sāman* is etymologically tied to *Sama* (equality or sameness). The sun represents the ultimate visible emblem of this equality. No matter where a person stands on earth, the sun appears to look directly at them (*māṃ prati*). It does not discriminate based on geography, morality, or status. Because of this absolute, unbroken impartiality, the sun is identical to the universal harmony of the Sāman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this equality is an essential attribute of the Supreme Lord (*Sambhava*). The sun, as the eye of the cosmic body of God, shines on all souls with equal love and opportunity. This meditation breaks down the narrow, competitive ego of the seeker, reminding them that divine grace is infinitely distributed and accessible to everyone.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the indwelling deity of the sun (*Sūrya-Nārāyaṇa*) acts with perfect justice and impartiality under the direct orders of Lord Viṣṇu. While individual souls vary in their capacity to receive light due to their own past actions (*karma*), the sun itself offers its vitalizing energy equally to the entire dependent universe.

verses.dev: तस्मिन्निमानि सर्वाणि भूतान्यन्वायत्तानीति विद्यात्तस्य यत्पुरोदयात्सा हिंकारस्तदस्य पशवोऽन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते हिं कुर्वन्ति हिंकारभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tasminnimāni sarvāṇi bhūtānyanvāyattānīti vidyāttasya yatpurodayātsā hiṃkārastodasya paśavo'nvāyattāstasmātte hiṃ kurvanti hiṃkārabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: One should know that all living beings depend on the sun. That form of the sun which appears before its actual sunrise is the Hiṃkāra. On this stage, the animals are dependent; therefore, animals make the 'Hiṃ' sound at dawn, for they share in this initial part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the first of the seven solar stages: the pre-dawn glow (*purodayāt*). This dim, initial twilight is mapped to *Hiṃkāra*. The text notes that domestic animals and livestock are deeply sensitive to this specific frequency of morning energy. They awake and make guttural noises (*hiṃ-kurvanti*). By meditating on this connection, the chanter recognizes that animal behavior is naturally hardwired into the cosmic choir of the Sāman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school points out that the morning awakening of animals demonstrates how physical nature responds instinctively to the first stirrings of divine light. This stage teaches the human seeker to wake up early and prepare their mind, ensuring they do not miss the initial daily downpouring of spiritual vitality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the minor deities ruling the animal kingdom activate the biological clocks of creatures during pre-dawn twilight. This structural dependency proves that animal life is not an isolated mechanical process but is explicitly synchronized with the solar administration designed by the Lord.

verses.dev: अथ यत्प्रथमोदिते स प्रस्तावस्तदस्य मनुष्या अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्रकटाः प्रशंसाकामाः प्रस्तावभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha yatprathamodite sa prastāvastodasya manuṣyā anvāyattāstasmātte prakaṭāḥ praśaṃsākāmāḥ prastāvabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: The form of the sun when it first rises is the Prastāva. On this stage, humans are dependent; therefore, humans desire advancement, public presentation, and praise, for they share in this introductory praise part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the second stage: the actual moment of sunrise (*prathamodite*), mapped to *Prastāva* (the introductory praise). This is the sphere of human dependency. As the sun rises, human society springs into action, looking for growth, public activity (*prakaṭāḥ*), and social validation or praise (*praśaṃsā-kāmāḥ*). The prefix *Pra-* links the psychological drives of humanity directly to the structural properties of the *Prastāva* chant.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that human ambition and the urge to perform good deeds are activated by the rising sun. The text highlights this drive to remind seekers that their daily work and desire for excellence should be offered up as an intentional praise-offering (*prastāva*) to the indwelling Divine Lord rather than being wasted on pure ego inflation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the rising sun stimulates the intellectual and physical faculties of human beings. Humans are uniquely endowed with the capacity to praise the Lord. Recognizing this connection helps the devotee understand that their daily duties and social responsibilities are structural assignments given by the solar administration.

verses.dev: अथ यत्संगववेलायां सा आदिस्तदस्य वयांस्यन्वायत्तानि तस्मात्तान्यन्तरिक्षेऽनारम्भणान्यात्मानमुत्पतन्त्युपादायैतस्य साम्नः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha yatsaṃgavavelāyāṃ sā ādistodasya vayāṃsyanvāyattāni tasmāttānyantarikṣe'nārambhaṇānyātmānamutpatantyupādāyaitasya sāmnaḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: The form of the sun at the hour of cow-gathering (mid-morning) is the Ādi. On this stage, the birds are dependent; therefore, they fly about in the intermediate space without any support, holding themselves up, for they share in this initial part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies *saṃgava-velā*, the mid-morning hour when cows are gathered together to be milked or driven to pasture. This time corresponds to the *Ādi* syllable (typically *Om*). Birds (*vayāṃsi*) are the creatures dependent on this specific solar hour. Sustained by the warming air currents of mid-morning, they launch themselves into the trackless sky, flying gracefully without physical pillars or support (*anārambhaṇāni*). They match the soaring, independent quality of the sacred sound *Om*.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school interprets the flight of the birds as a metaphor for spiritual surrender. Just as a bird trusts the invisible air of mid-morning to hold it aloft without any solid ground beneath its feet, the soul must trust the all-pervading grace of the Lord (*Ādi* / *Om*) to sustain them in the spiritual sky.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the mid-morning solar rays provide the exact thermodynamic conditions that allow birds to thrive and fly. This demonstrates that even the movements of wild animals in the sky are meticulously supported by the cosmic infrastructure managed by Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: अथ यन्मध्यंदिने स उद्गीथस्तदस्य देवा अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्राजापत्यानां दायादानां श्रेष्ठा उद्गीथभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yanmadhyaṃdine sa udgīthastodasya devā anvāyattāstasmātte prājāpatyānāṃ dāyādānāṃ śreṣṭhā udgīthabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 5 ||
verses.trans: The form of the sun at exact midday is the Udgītha. On this stage, the gods (Devas) are dependent; therefore, they are the most excellent among the offspring of Prajāpati, for they share in this supreme part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the apex of the solar journey: noon (*madhyaṃdine*), which is mapped to the *Udgītha*, the highest and most glorious portion of the chant. This peak energy belongs to the gods (*devas*). Because they are synchronized with the sun at its brightest, most shadowless point, the gods possess radiant bodies and represent the most excellent (*śreṣṭhāḥ*) branch of the creation of Prajāpati.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that midday represents the full manifestation of light and knowledge, entirely free from the shadows of ignorance. The gods thrive in this clarity. Meditating on this peak teaches the seeker to aspire for midday clarity in their own consciousness, driving away the dark shadows of doubt and attachment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that at noon, the solar administrative deities operate at maximum strength. The excellence of the gods over demons and humans is due to their pristine alignment with this peak solar force, showing that spiritual hierarchy in the universe is determined by proximity to divine light.

verses.dev: अथ यदपराह्णे स प्रतिहारस्तदस्य गर्भा अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते प्रतिहृता नावपद्यन्ते प्रतिहारभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadapārāhṇe sa pratihārastodasya garbhā anvāyattāstasmātte pratihṛtā nāvapadyante pratihārabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 6 ||
verses.trans: The form of the sun in the afternoon is the Pratihāra. On this stage, embryos (fetuses in the womb) are dependent; therefore, they are held back and secured in the womb without falling out, for they share in this protective, holding part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a fascinating medical and esoteric insight on the afternoon sun (*aparāhṇe*), mapping it to the *Pratihāra* (which literally means checking, holding back, or defending). The entities dependent on this phase are embryos (*garbhāḥ*). The closing, cooling energy of the afternoon matches the biological mechanism that keeps a fetus safely contained and held up within the mother's womb, preventing miscarriage (*nāvapadyante*). This links cosmic cooling to biological stabilization.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights the deep, protective aspect of this verse. The Lord does not just create; He holds and preserves. By identifying the afternoon sun with the retention of life in the womb, the text reminds the seeker that they are constantly held, sheltered, and nurtured by the divine motherly grace of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the afternoon solar energies are governed by deities specializing in preservation and structural stability. The retention of life in the womb during this shifting period of the day is a testament to the intricate protective laws established by the independent will of Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: अथ यदस्तमिते स उपद्रवस्तदस्य आरण्या अन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्ते पुरुषं दृष्ट्वा कक्षं श्वभ्रभित्यूपद्रवन्त्युपद्रवभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्नः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadastamite sa upadravastodasya āraṇyā anvāyattāstasmātte puruṣaṃ dṛṣṭvā kakṣaṃ śvabhramityūpadravantyupadravabhājino hyetasya sāmnaḥ || 7 ||
verses.trans: The form of the sun immediately after sunset is the Upadrava. On this stage, the wild animals of the forest are dependent; therefore, when they see a human, they run away to their dens or thickets, for they share in this fleeing part of the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the sixth stage: the immediate aftermath of sunset (*astamite*), mapped to the *Upadrava* (which means running away, escaping, or a secondary phase). The wild animals of the forest (*āraṇyāḥ*) depend on this hour. As darkness falls, their defensive instincts sharpen; if they spot a human, they bolt (*upadravanti*) into caves or thickets. The wordplay connects the flight of the wild beasts directly to the musical *Upadrava* phase.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that sunset marks the transition where wild creatures reclaim their domain. Their instinct to flee humans is a protective boundary set by the Lord. This stage teaches the seeker that when the dark periods of life arrive, one must withdraw from outer social distractions and retreat into the inner sanctuary of the heart.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the sunset triggers the nocturnal and defensive operations of forest life. The instinctual fear and retreat of wild animals show that even the emotions of fear and self-preservation are regulated by cosmic cycles under divine governance.

verses.dev: अथ यद्रहोऽस्तमिते तन्निधनं तदस्य पितरोऽन्वायत्तास्तस्मात्तान्निदधति निधनभाजिनो ह्येतस्य साम्न एवं खल्वमुमादित्यं सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadraho'stamite tannidhanaṃ tadasya pitaro'nvāyattāstasmāttānnidadhati nidhanabhājino hyetasya sāmna evaṃ khalvamumādityaṃ saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste || 8 ||
verses.trans: The form of the sun when it is completely hidden (midnight/deep night) is the finale (Nidhana). On this stage, the ancestors (Pitris) are dependent; therefore, people deposit funeral offerings for them or lay them down, for they share in this final resting part of the Sāman. Thus, verily, one meditates on the sun as the sevenfold Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara concludes this magnificent section with the status of the sun when it is entirely out of sight (*raho'stamite*), meaning midnight or the deep unmanifest dark. This corresponds to the *Nidhana* (the final dissolution or storage house). The ancestors (*pitaraḥ*) depend on this phase, as the unmanifest night connects our world with the realm of the departed. The word *Nidhana* matches *nidadhati* (the ritual act of placing down or depositing offerings for the dead). This completes the sevenfold solar journey, proving that light and dark, life and death, are bound within a single cosmic song.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that *Nidhana* represents the ultimate rest of the soul. The ancestors residing in the higher spiritual planes find their peace in the deep unmanifest phase of the divine cycle. It reassures the seeker that death is not absolute destruction, but a temporary resting phase inside the infinite memory of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the deep night is administered by deities ruling over sleep, rest, and the realms of the Pitris. Depositing pindas or memory offerings during specific cycles connects the living with the departed under the single, overarching law of Lord Viṣṇu, who preserves the souls of the ancestors just as carefully as He maintains the living.

verses.dev: अथ खल्वात्मसंमितमतिमृत्यु सप्तविधं सामोपासीत । हिङ्कार इति त्र्यक्षरं प्रस्ताव इति त्र्यक्षरं तत्समम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha khalv ātmasaṃmitam atimṛtyu saptavidhaṃ sāmopāsīta | hiṅkāra iti tryakṣaraṃ prastāva iti tryakṣaraṃ tat samam || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, verily, one should meditate on the sevenfold Sāman as measured by itself and as leading beyond death. The word 'hiṅkāra' has three syllables; the word 'prastāva' has three syllables — so they are equal.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains 'ātmasammita' as the Sāman measured against its own members — each part balancing the others — and 'atimṛtyu' as that which carries the meditator beyond death, since the syllable-count itself transcends the twenty-one-fold world of mortality. The equality of syllables points to the underlying sameness (sāmya) that is the real meaning of Sāman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The syllable-equivalence is a divinely ordained harmony within the liturgy; meditating on this balance as the Lord's own design purifies the worshipper. The Sāman 'beyond death' means that meditation performed through it wins the Lord's realm, where there is no return to mortality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the balanced syllables as testimony to Viṣṇu's perfect ordering of sacred speech. 'Beyond death' signifies that the Lord alone, propitiated by this meditation, grants release from saṃsāra — the syllables have no independent power.

verses.dev: आदिरिति द्व्यक्षरं प्रतिहार इति चतुरक्षरं तत इहैकं तत्समम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: ādir iti dvyakṣaraṃ pratihāra iti caturakṣaraṃ tata ihaikaṃ tat samam || 2 ||
verses.trans: The word 'ādi' has two syllables; the word 'pratihāra' has four syllables. Taking one syllable from there to here — they become equal.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The transfer of a syllable to create balance shows that apparent inequality in the empirical realm conceals an underlying equality — a hint of the non-dual substrate behind diverse forms.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Even apparent excess and deficiency in the Lord's liturgy resolve into harmony, teaching the worshipper that all parts of creation find their proper measure in relation to Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The adjustment of syllables is scripture's own device, sanctioned by the Lord; it teaches that gradation and order pervade even sacred sound, all dependent on Viṣṇu's will.

verses.dev: उद्गीथ इति त्र्यक्षरमुपद्रव इति चतुरक्षरं त्रिभिस्त्रिभिः समं भवत्यक्षरमतिशिष्यते त्र्यक्षरं तत्समम् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: udgītha iti tryakṣaram upadrava iti caturakṣaraṃ tribhis tribhiḥ samaṃ bhavaty akṣaram atiśiṣyate tryakṣaraṃ tat samam || 3 ||
verses.trans: The word 'udgītha' has three syllables; the word 'upadrava' has four syllables. Three and three are equal, and one syllable is left over; being itself counted as three-syllabled ('a-kṣa-ra'), it too is equal.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes the subtlety: the leftover syllable, when named 'akṣara', itself contains three syllables and thus restores balance. 'Akṣara' also means the Imperishable — the remainder beyond all counting is Brahman itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The leftover syllable resolving into equality shows that nothing in the Lord's creation is ever truly surplus or deficient; what seems a remainder is itself a fullness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The word 'akṣara' (imperishable) appearing as the balancing remainder points to Viṣṇu, the Imperishable, who alone completes and harmonises all things.

verses.dev: निधनमिति त्र्यक्षरं तत्सममेव भवति तानि ह वा एतानि द्वाविंशतिरक्षराणि ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: nidhanam iti tryakṣaraṃ tat samam eva bhavati tāni ha vā etāni dvāviṃśatir akṣarāṇi || 4 ||
verses.trans: The word 'nidhana' has three syllables; it is likewise equal. These, verily, are the twenty-two syllables.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The total of twenty-two syllables completes the symmetric body of the Sāman. The perfection of the count prepares the next verse's teaching: twenty-one reaches the sun, the twenty-second goes beyond.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The complete count represents the fullness of the Lord's liturgical form; the worshipper who contemplates the whole attains the whole — the Lord Himself.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees the fixed number as proof of the eternal, authorless precision of the Veda, whose meaning rests in Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: एकविंशत्यादित्यमाप्नोत्येकविंशो वा इतोऽसावादित्यो द्वाविंशेन परमादित्याज्जयति तन्नाकं तद्विशोकम् ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: ekaviṃśaty ādityam āpnoty ekaviṃśo vā ito 'sāv ādityo dvāviṃśena param ādityāj jayati tan nākaṃ tad viśokam || 5 ||
verses.trans: With the twenty-one syllables one reaches the sun — for the sun is the twenty-first from here. With the twenty-second one conquers what is beyond the sun: that is heaven (nāka), that is freedom from sorrow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara counts twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and the sun as the twenty-first. The twenty-second step 'beyond the sun' is the sorrowless state — 'nāka' is glossed as 'where there is no sorrow' — ultimately pointing to liberation beyond all relative worlds.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The passage maps the soul's ascent: through the cosmic order up to the sun, and by the Lord's grace beyond it to His supreme abode, which is free of all grief — attained by the devoted meditator.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva stresses that the 'beyond' is not an impersonal state but the realm of Viṣṇu, higher than all cosmic stations including the sun-deity; only His grace carries the soul across.

verses.dev: आप्नोती हादित्यस्य जयं परो हास्यादित्यजयाज्जयो भवति य एतदेवं विद्वानात्मसंमितमतिमृत्यु सप्तविधं सामोपास्ते सामोपास्ते ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: āpnotī hādityasya jayaṃ paro hāsyādityajayāj jayo bhavati ya etad evaṃ vidvān ātmasaṃmitam atimṛtyu saptavidhaṃ sāmopāste sāmopāste || 6 ||
verses.trans: He who, knowing this thus, meditates on the sevenfold Sāman as measured by itself and as beyond death, gains the conquest of the sun; indeed a victory higher than the conquest of the sun becomes his — he who meditates on the Sāman, who meditates on the Sāman.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The repetition ('sāmopāste sāmopāste') marks the close of the meditation. The 'victory beyond the sun' is the fruit transcending all relative attainments — the meditator is led along the path of light toward brahmaloka and, with the dawning of knowledge, to liberation itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The double statement seals the promise: the fruit of this upāsanā is not merely the solar world but the supreme realm of Nārāyaṇa beyond it, where the freed soul enjoys uninterrupted communion with the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the higher victory as entry into Viṣṇu's eternal world, surpassing every cosmic dominion; the emphatic repetition affirms the certainty of the result for the qualified devotee.

verses.dev: प्राणो हिंकारो वाक्प्रस्तावश्चक्षुरुद्गीथः श्रोत्रं प्रतिहारोऽपानो निधनमेतद्गायत्रं प्राणेषु प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: prāṇo hiṃkāro vākprastāvaścakṣurudgīthaḥ śrotraṃ pratihāro'pāno nidhanametadgāyatraṃ prāṇeṣu protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The outward breath (Prāṇa) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; speech (Vāk) is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the eye (Cakṣus) is the loud chant (Udgītha); the ear (Śrotram) is the response (Pratihāra); and the downward breath (Apāna) is the finale (Nidhana). This is the Gāyatra Sāman as woven into the vital breaths and senses.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that having covered the generalized fivefold and sevenfold meditations, the Upanishad now introduces specific, highly potent musical compositions (*Sāman* melodies), beginning with the *Gāyatra-Sāman*. The Gāyatra melody is the foundational meter and chief song of the Sāmaveda. Here, it is described as *protam*—interwoven or threaded like warp and weft—into the organic human faculties. While the structural divisions match Section 7, the meditation changes in quality: the seeker must perceive the specific, rapid, and purifying acoustic vibration of the *Gāyatra* rhythm operating through their breath, sight, and hearing, making the body an active vessel for this melody.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that *Gāyatra* means 'that which protects the singer' (*gāyantam trāyate*). By understanding that this protective melody is tightly woven into our organic faculties by the Divine, the devotee realizes that their physical life is continuously guarded. The eyes, ears, and breath are not independent biological machines; they are parts of a protective rhythm managed by the indwelling Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya notes that the Gāyatra meter is presided over by the Supreme Lord Viṣṇu in His Gāyatrī manifestation. The word *protam* confirms that the vital breaths do not function by their own power; they are completely dependent on and bound to the divine blueprint. Recognizing this layout prevents the soul from claiming independent mastery over their health or physical senses.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्गायत्रं प्राणेषु प्रोतं वेद प्राणवान्भवती सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या महामनाः स्यात्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadgāyatraṃ prāṇeṣu protaṃ veda prāṇavānbhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā mahāmanāḥ syāttadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Gāyatra Sāman as woven into the vital breaths becomes rich in vital energy, attains a full lifespan, lives resplendently, becomes great with progeny and cattle, and grows magnificent in spiritual renown. His sacred vow should be to remain broad-minded and magnanimous (Mahāmanāḥ).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the unique psychological vow (*Vrata*) assigned to this meditation: *mahāmanāḥ syāt* (one should be broad-minded and magnanimous). A person who understands that the supreme Gāyatra rhythm flows through all life cannot afford to be narrow-minded, petty, or bogged down by small attachments. Broad-mindedness is not just a moral suggestion here; it is the natural, structural consequence of realizing that your breath and your neighbor's breath are expressions of the same universal melody.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that *mahāmanāḥ* means keeping the mind completely fixed on the greatness of the Lord and His creation. Petty thoughts, jealousy, and anxiety vanish when the mind expands to see the Gāyatra melody operating within. This mental expansiveness allows the devotee to experience full vital energy (*prāṇavān*) and use their lifespan entirely for spiritual service.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the blessing of a broad mind (*mahāmanas*) is a direct gift from Mukhyaprāṇa, who is the deity of absolute intelligence and vitality. When a practitioner avoids small, selfish thinking, their capacity to receive and hold divine wisdom increases, ensuring that their spiritual renown (*kīrti*) reflects their true alignment with God's vast, independent management.

verses.dev: अभिमन्थति स हिंकारो धूमो जायते स प्रस्तावो ज्वलति स उद्गीथोऽङ्गारा भवन्ति स प्रतिहारो उपशाम्यति तन्निधनं संशाम्यति तन्निधनमेतद्रथन्तरमग्नौ प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: abhimanthati sa hiṃkāro dhūmo jāyate sa prastāvo jvalati sa udgītho'ṅgārā bhavanti sa pratihāro upaśāmyati tannidhanaṃ saṃśāmyati tannidhanametadrathantaramagnau protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The churning of the fire-sticks is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the rising of smoke is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the bursting into flame is the loud chant (Udgītha); the formation of glowing embers is the response (Pratihāra); the dying down of the flame is the finale (Nidhana); and its complete extinguishment is the absolute finale (Nidhana). This is the Rathantara Sāman as woven into fire.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that the *Rathantara-Sāman* (literally 'that which crosses or transports the chariot') is historically and liturgically bound to the element of Fire (*Agni*). The Upanishad breaks down the mechanical generation of fire as an exact liturgical dance. The friction-based rubbing of the wooden churning sticks (*abhimanthati*) represents *Hiṃkāra*, the initialization. The first physical byproduct—the rising of columned smoke (*dhūmaḥ*)—is the *Prastāva*. The brilliant, hot emergence of the open flame (*jvalati*) corresponds to the *Udgītha*, the peak expression. The calming of the dynamic flame into steady, radiating coals (*aṅgārāḥ*) represents the protective *Pratihāra*. The cooling process (*upaśāmyati*) and the absolute cold silence of spent ash (*saṃśāmyati*) serve as the double *Nidhana* where the visible energy returns to its unmanifest state. This teaches that thermodynamic cycles are micro-reflections of the universal Sāman sound.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this chemical sequence as a profound lesson on spiritual awakening. The churning represents the intense self-discipline (*tapas*) of the seeker. The smoke is the initial confusion or purifying stage. The bright flame is the sudden manifestation of direct spiritual knowledge (*jñāna*), which stands as the *Udgītha*. The final rest in the cooling embers shows the transition of ecstatic devotion into *Sānti*—the permanent, peaceful, quiet balance inside the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the element of Fire is governed by the deity Agni under the sovereign command of Lord Viṣṇu. The stages of combustion are not unguided physical chances; they are controlled expressions of divine law. Meditating on the Rathantara Sāman in fire helps the seeker realize that warmth, light, and final rest are structural provisions engineered by the Lord to support and ultimately gather back material creation.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्रथन्तरमग्नौ प्रोतं वेद ब्रह्मवर्चस्यन्नादो भवति सुवर्णं गच्छेत्सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या न प्रत्यङ्ङग्निमाचामेन्न निष्ठीवेत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadrathantaramagnau protaṃ veda brahmavarcasyannādo bhavati suvarṇaṃ gacchetsarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā na pratyaṅṅagnimācāmenna niṣṭhīvettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Rathantara Sāman as woven into fire becomes radiant with spiritual luster (Brahmavarcas), possesses a robust digestion, attains a glorious and bright life, lives out a full lifespan, and becomes great in progeny, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to rinse his mouth facing fire, nor to spit toward it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the special reward of *Brahmavarcas* (the aura of spiritual learning and scriptural purity). Because the meditator internalizes the purifying nature of fire, their intellect shines brightly. The strict physical taboos instituted by this meditation—*na pratyaṅ agnim ācāmet* (one should never rinse or eject water toward fire) and *na niṣṭhīvet* (one must never spit toward it)—are behavioral vows of environmental reverence. Insulting or defiling the elements with bodily waste breaks the practitioner's non-dual empathy with the cosmic Sāman layout.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that avoiding disrespect toward fire means treating all natural elements as sacred extensions of the Lord's universal temple. *Brahmavarcas* is the outer signature of a soul that lives in clean harmony with creation. By keeping this vow of respect, the devotee preserves their inner spiritual heat (*tapas*) and uses their health to praise the Divine without drop-offs.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the explicit prohibitions against spitting or rinsing toward fire serve to honor the deity Agni, who acts as the primary conveyor of ritual offerings to Viṣṇu. Breaking this discipline invites spiritual dullness. Observing this vow with care aligns the seeker with the divine cosmic hierarchy, granting long life, line-age stability, and unshakeable peace.

verses.dev: उपमन्त्रयते स हिंकारो ज्ञपयते स प्रस्तावः स्त्रिया सह शेते स उद्गीथः प्रति स्त्रीणा सह शेते स प्रतिहारः कालं गच्छति तन्निधनं पारं गच्छति तन्निधनमेतद्वामदेव्यं मिथुने प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: upamantrayate sa hiṃkāro jñapayate sa prastāvaḥ striyā saha śete sa udgīthaḥ prati strīṇā saha śete sa pratihāraḥ kālaṃ gacchati tannidhanaṃ pāraṃ gacchati tannidhanametadvāmadevyaṃ mithune protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The act of mutual beckoning or romantic approach is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the mutual expression of interest or courtship is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the state of union is the loud chant (Udgītha); the shared alignment or resting together is the response (Pratihāra); the approaching of the climax is the finale (Nidhana); and the absolute completion of the union is the final rest (Nidhana). This is the Vāmadevya Sāman as woven into pairing and union.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara observes that the *Vāmadevya-Sāman* is explicitly identified across Vedic liturgies with the principle of union and pairing (*mithuna*). The text elevates the biological and psychological drive of procreation by structuring its development as an exact Sāman ritual. The preliminary approach or sign of attraction (*upamantrayate*) is the *Hiṃkāra*. The deliberate signaling of romantic intent (*jñapayate*) represents the *Prastāva*. The peak, sacred moment of biological and emotional union (*saha śete*) is the *Udgītha*. The protective, reciprocal embrace that follows represents the *Pratihāra*. The winding down of physical energy (*kālaṃ gacchati*) and the absolute quietude and fulfillment of completion (*pāraṃ gacchati*) serve as the double *Nidhana*. This meditation de-mystifies and purifies sexuality, transforming it from an animalistic impulse into a conscious, orderly participation in the universal melody of creation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that the *Vāmadevya* meditation highlights the sacred responsibility of lineage and procreation under divine law. Attraction and pairing are not random material events, but are fueled by the creative joy (*ānanda*) of the Supreme Lord, who sustains the world through the preservation of lineages. When the householder views family life through this liturgical lens, biological impulses are sanctified into an act of cosmic stewardship.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the Vāmadevya melody is supervised by specialized deities who govern domestic life, marital harmony, and reproductive health under the direct authority of Lord Viṣṇu. Understanding this framework prevents the soul from seeing relationships through the lens of pure selfish enjoyment (*kāma*), orienting them instead toward fulfilling their scriptural duties (*dharma*) with respect for the cosmic layout.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्वामदेव्यं मिथुने प्रोतं वेद मिथुनीभवति मिथुनान्मिथुनात्प्रजायते सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या न कांचन परिहरेत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadvāmadevyaṃ mithune protaṃ veda mithunībhavati mithunānmithunātprajāyate sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā na kāṃcana pariharettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Vāmadevya Sāman as woven into pairing becomes blessed with an ideal partner, propagates healthy lineages from generation to generation, attains a complete lifespan, lives resplendently, and becomes great in children, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to exclude or treat any woman with disrespect.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the exceptional ethical vow (*Vrata*) connected to this meditation: *na kāñcana pariharet* (one should never reject, shun, or treat any woman with disrespect). Since woman is the primary earthly crucible and physical temple for the Vāmadevya-Sāman's creative energy, any form of misogyny, neglect, or casual objectification completely shatters the chanter's spiritual practice. The vow demands a deep, non-dual reverence for the feminine principle as the essential matrix through which the universal harmony manifests its continuity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school emphasizes that the strict prohibition against disrespecting or turning away any woman honors the motherly and creative energy of the Divine Mother, Lakṣmī. A householder who maintains this vow creates an auspicious atmosphere in the home, ensuring that their family lineage thrives in health, spiritual education, and unbroken devotion to the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that respecting women in the context of the Vāmadevya meditation honors the governing female deities (*devatās*) who manage birth and nourishment under Viṣṇu's command. Breaking this discipline invites serious karmic blockages and cuts off domestic peace. Observing this vow diligently guarantees physical health, stable lineage progression, and the growth of spiritual reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: उद्यन्हिंकारो मुदितः प्रस्तावो मध्यंदिन उद्गीथोऽपराह्नः प्रतिहारोऽस्तं यन्निधनमेतद्बृहदादित्ये प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: udyanhiṃkāro muditaḥ prastāvo madhyaṃdina udgītho'parāhnaḥ pratihāro'staṃ yannidhanametadbṛhadāditye protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The rising sun is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the freshly risen sun when it gladdens all creatures is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the exact midday sun is the loud chant (Udgītha); the afternoon sun is the response (Pratihāra); and the setting sun is the finale (Nidhana). This is the Bṛhat Sāman as woven into the sun.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that the word *Bṛhat* signifies vastness, majesty, and immense cosmic scale. While Section 9 analyzed the sun through a broad sevenfold structure, this section introduces the specific *Bṛhat-Sāman* melody, mapping its five rhythmic components directly onto the solar milestones. The sun breaking the horizon (*udyan*) is *Hiṃkāra*. The *muditaḥ* state—when the sun is fully above the horizon, warming the morning and gladdening the hearts of living beings—is *Prastāva*. Midday (*madhyaṃdina*), when the sun stands majestic, direct, and unshadowed at its highest point, is the *Udgītha*. The slanting, cooling afternoon rays (*aparāhnaḥ*) are the *Pratihāra*, and the act of sinking below the western horizon (*astaṃ yan*) is the *Nidhana*. This meditation teaches the seeker to experience the day not as a series of mundane hours, but as the unfolding of a vast, majestic song.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this solar alignment as an expression of the supreme majesty (*bṛhatva*) of the Lord's cosmic form. The sun is the great wheeling clock of the universe. The *muditaḥ* (joyful morning) phase reminds the devotee that divine light is inherently bliss-giving. By aligning the midday sun with the *Udgītha*, the text guides the soul to recognize that the peak of human capability and spiritual realization must be centered on the absolute, brilliant clarity of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the *Bṛhat-Sāman* is explicitly overseen by the independent majesty of Lord Viṣṇu working through the solar deity. The term *protam* means the solar cycle is tightly threaded into this musical matrix. The specific stages of daylight are carefully measured outlays of spiritual energy designed to maintain universal order, ensuring that no creature can exist outside of the sovereign layout established by the Almighty.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्बृहदादित्ये प्रोतं वेद तेजस्व्यन्नादो भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या तपन्तं न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadbṛhadāditye protaṃ veda tejasvyannādo bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā tapantaṃ na nindettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Bṛhat Sāman as woven into the sun becomes radiant with spiritual energy, possesses excellent digestion, lives out a complete and glorious lifespan, and grows great in progeny, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to speak ill of the burning sun.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara unpacks the vital behavioral vow (*Vrata*) associated with this meditation: *tapantaṃ na nindet* (one must never criticize or complain about the burning sun). When the summer heat or afternoon rays become intense, humans naturally tend to complain about the heat. Shankara points out that criticizing the heat is a sign of spiritual ignorance; it means you are insulting the *Udgītha* phase of the majestic Bṛhat melody. The vow forces an inner psychological shift from defensive ego-discomfort to non-dual acceptance, honoring the fierce intensity of the element that sustains all global ecosystems.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that honoring the burning sun means recognizing that even the harsh, scorching trials of life (*tapas*) are sent by the Lord for our purification. Complaining about the heat or our trials breaks our alignment with the divine plan. Keeping this vow preserves the seeker's inner spiritual radiance (*tejas*) and keeps them anchored in gratitude under all circumstances.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the burning sun is a direct manifestation of the raw power and administrative authority of Sūrya-Nārāyaṇa. Speaking ill of the sun's heat is an act of irreverence toward the planetary controllers appointed by Viṣṇu. Observing this vow strictly purifies the mind, builds resilience, and grants the material rewards of health, lineage continuity, and stellar reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: अभ्रं जायते स हिंकारो मेघो भवति स प्रस्तावो वर्षति स उद्गीथो विद्योतते स्तनयति स प्रतिहार उद्गृह्णाति तन्निधनमेतद्वैरूप्यं पर्जन्ये प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: abhraṃ jāyate sa hiṃkāro megho bhavati sa prastāvo varṣati sa udgītho vidyotate stanayati sa pratihāro udgṛhṇāti tannidhanametadvairūpyaṃ parjanye protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The gathering of thin mists or vapors is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the formation of thick, dark clouds is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the falling of rain is the loud chant (Udgītha); the flashing of lightning and rolling of thunder represent the response (Pratihāra); and the clearing or cessation of the storm is the finale (Nidhana). This is the Vairūpya Sāman as woven into the rain-god (Parjanya).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that *Vairūpya* literally means 'having diverse, multiform shapes.' This name perfectly suits the atmospheric theater of rain (*Parjanya*). The text maps this weather cycle step-by-step. The initial rising of transparent vapor (*abhram*) is the *Hiṃkāra*. As these vapors pack tightly into dark, heavy rain-clouds (*meghaḥ*), it reaches the *Prastāva*. The downpour itself (*varṣati*) is the crowning *Udgītha*—the high point that directly nourishes the Earth. The dramatic, striking flash of lightning and thunder (*vidyotate stanayati*) forms the *Pratihāra*. Finally, the clearing up of the skies and withholding of water (*udgṛhṇāti*) is the *Nidhana*. Shankara states that by meditating on these shifting clouds as the Vairūpya song, the sage stays unmoved by external chaos, recognizing it as a passing phase of one universal harmony.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that the multi-shaped clouds reveal the endless creative imagination (*vaicitrya*) of the Supreme Lord. Rain is an act of sheer divine mercy (*kṛpā*). By mapping the downpour onto the *Udgītha*, the text shows that God's grace falls generously on everyone. The thunder and lightning serve as a reminder of His absolute power, guiding the devotee to view a storm not with fear, but as a grand display of divine majesty.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the *Vairūpya-Sāman* is managed by the deity Parjanya under the direct sovereign authority of Lord Viṣṇu. The changing shapes of clouds are not random accidents of weather. They are carefully organized layers of cosmic energy designed to sustain life on earth, proving that everything operates under a flawless, independent divine blueprint.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्वैरूप्यं पर्जन्ये प्रोतं वेद विरूपांश्च सुरूपांश्च पशून्अवरुन्धे सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या वर्षन्तं न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadvairūpyaṃ parjanye protaṃ veda virūpāṃśca surūpāṃśca paśūnavarundhe sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā varṣantaṃ na nindettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Vairūpya Sāman as woven into the rain-god acquires livestock of all forms—both uniquely shaped and beautifully formed; he enjoys a full and glorious lifespan, and grows great in children, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to speak ill of the pouring rain.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the specific psychological vow (*Vrata*) assigned here: *varṣantaṃ na nindet* (one must never complain about the rain). People naturally grumble when downpours disrupt their daily plans or travel. Shankara explains that complaining about rain shows a deep separation from nature; it means you are insulting the *Udgītha* phase of the Vairūpya melody. The vow forces an inner shift from self-centered annoyance to non-dual acceptance, honoring the very water cycle that prevents global famine.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that honoring the pouring rain means recognizing that even when life feels damp, cloudy, or inconvenient, it is all part of the Lord's nurturing plan. Complaining about the weather breaks our harmony with His creation. Keeping this vow preserves the seeker's inner peace and keeps them grounded in gratitude, no matter how stormy life gets.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the rain is a direct blessing from the corporate administrators appointed by Viṣṇu. Speaking ill of rain displays an ungrateful heart and invites negative karma. Observing this vow with care aligns the seeker with the cosmic hierarchy, granting long life, family stability, and an excellent spiritual reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: वसन्तो हिंकारो ग्रीष्मः प्रस्तावो वर्षा उद्गीथः शरत्प्रतिहारो हेमन्तो निधनमेतद्वैराजमृतुषु प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: vasanto hiṃkāro grīṣmaḥ prastāvo varṣā udgīthaḥ śaratpratihāro hemanto nidhanametadvairājamṛtuṣu protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: Spring (Vasanta) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; Summer (Grīṣma) is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the Rainy Season (Varṣā) is the loud chant (Udgītha); Autumn (Śarat) is the response (Pratihāra); and Winter (Hemanta) is the finale (Nidhana). This is the Vairāja Sāman as woven into the seasons.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that *Vairāja* comes from *Virāj*, which represents the radiant macrocosmic physical universe and the principle of rhythmic sovereignty. In this section, the Vairāja melody is mapped directly onto the wheel of seasons (*ṛtus*). Spring, the season of rebirth, budding flora, and natural quickening, acts as *Hiṃkāra*. Summer, with its ripening heat and preparatory dry spell, is *Prastāva*. The Rainy Season, bringing the vital downpour that directly yields food and sustains life, is the central *Udgītha*. Autumn, bringing golden harvests and clear post-storm skies, acts as the balancing *Pratihāra*. Winter, where nature gathers its sap, draws inward, and enters a quiet chill, serves as *Nidhana*, the final rest. By internalizing this progression, the seeker ceases to look at weather changes as personal inconveniences, experiencing them instead as the breathing patterns of the cosmic Virāj.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the word *Vairāja* highlights how the Supreme Lord shines in unique splendor through the changing seasons. The year is a beautifully arranged liturgy. The rainy season as *Udgītha* demonstrates that the peak of cosmic song is always centered on nourishment and life-giving grace. The entire seasonal wheel is a rhythmic, predictable manifestation of the indwelling Lord's creative care for His devotees.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the *Vairāja-Sāman* is overseen by specialized solar and temporal deities who report directly to Lord Viṣṇu. The term *protam* demonstrates that time is not an unguided, chaotic sequence of months. Each season has a specific, immutable duty to fulfill under divine decree, proving that the physical planet functions within a rigid, dependable layout engineered by the Supreme Ruler.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्वैराजमृतुषु प्रोतं वेद विराजति प्रजया पशुभिर्ब्रह्मवर्चसेन सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या ऋतूंस्तपन्तं न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadvairājamṛtuṣu protaṃ veda virājati prajayā paśubhirbrahmavarcasena sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā ṛtūṃstapantaṃ na nindettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Vairāja Sāman as woven into the seasons shines with sovereign luster through his children, cattle, and spiritual aura (Brahmavarcas); he attains a full, radiant lifespan, and grows great in progeny, livestock, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to speak ill of the seasons.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the psychological and behavioral vow (*Vrata*) unique to this practice: *ṛtūn na nindet* (one must never criticize the seasons). People frequently complain when it is too cold in winter or too dry in summer, wishing the weather would adapt to their comfort. Shankara notes that finding fault with a season is a direct insult to the Vairāja melody. The vow forces an inner shift toward non-dual equanimity—accepting the natural extremes of frost and heat as essential phases of a singular global balance that clears out the old to make way for the new.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that honoring the turning seasons means understanding that both the harsh winters and burning summers of our lives are designed to mature the soul. Complaining about the time or season indicates an unresigned ego. Keeping this vow allows the seeker to tap into *Brahmavarcas* (spiritual radiance), ensuring that their character remains steady and bright regardless of external cycles.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, speaking ill of the seasons is an act of disrespect toward the structural time-controllers (*Kāla-devatās*) appointed by Viṣṇu. Embracing each season with a calm, cooperative attitude purifies the mind, stabilizes the household, and brings the material rewards of health, lineage stability, and lasting spiritual reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: पृथिवी हिंकारोऽन्तरिक्षं प्रस्तावो द्यौरुद्गीथो दिशः प्रतिहारः समुद्रो निधनमेताः शक्वर्यो लोकेषु प्रोताः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: pṛthivī hiṃkāro'ntarikṣaṃ prastāvo dyaurudgīthaḥ diśaḥ pratihāraḥ samudro nidhanametāḥ śakvaryo lokeṣu protāḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: The Earth (Pṛthivī) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the intermediate space or atmosphere (Antarikṣa) is the introductory praise (Prastāva); Heaven (Dyaus) is the loud chant (Udgītha); the cardinal directions (Diśaḥ) are the response (Pratihāra); and the great Ocean (Samudra) is the finale (Nidhana). These are the Śakvarī Sāmans as woven into the cosmic worlds.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that the word *Śakvarī* is pluralized here (*etāḥ śakvaryaḥ*) because this specialized melody represents the multi-layered powers or capacities (*śakti*) that maintain stability across the entire cosmic layout. The meditation scales upward through the structure of reality. The physical, solid base of Earth (*pṛthivī*) serves as *Hiṃkāra*, the foundational entry point. The vast atmospheric midspace (*antarikṣa*) is the *Prastāva*. The highest, luminescent realm of pure light and consciousness, Heaven (*dyaus*), is crowned as the *Udgītha*. The structural pillars that segment this space—the cardinal directions (*diśaḥ*)—act as the *Pratihāra*, gathering the space together. Finally, the Ocean (*samudra*), which acts as the ultimate earthly basin where all rivers terminate and find peace, is the *Nidhana*. This meditation expands the seeker's view, transforming geographical landscapes and space into a unified, resonant choir.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that the *Śakvarī* verses highlight the absolute sovereignty and administrative power (*śakti*) of the Supreme Lord over the nested spheres of creation. The worlds are not disconnected regions; they are dynamically bound together. Elevating Heaven to the *Udgītha* signals that the highest note of reality is always oriented toward the blissful, unclouded realm of divine light, while the Ocean as *Nidhana* shows that all moving paths eventually come to rest in the bottomless expanse of God's grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya explains that the *Śakvarī-Sāman* is directed by the cosmic powers ruling the elements, all moving under the independent authority of Lord Viṣṇu. The term *protam* emphasizes that the physical planet, the atmosphere, and the celestial spheres do not slide out of orbit or collapse into chaos. They are stitched firmly into a divine rhythmic framework, showing that material reality is a tightly governed layout built to serve the Lord's evolutionary plan.

verses.dev: स य एवमेताः शक्वर्यो लोकेषु प्रोता वेद लोकी भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या लोकान्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametāḥ śakvaryo lokeṣu protā veda lokī bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā lokānna nindettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows these Śakvarī Sāmans as woven into the cosmic worlds becomes the master of those worlds (Lokī); he attains a full, bright lifespan, and grows great in progeny, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to speak ill of the worlds.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the psychological weight of the vow (*Vrata*) given here: *lokān na nindet* (one must never criticize or condemn the worlds). People under stress often fall into dark, pessimistic states, cursing the world, life, or existence as inherently miserable or broken. Shankara states that condemning the world is a direct insult to the *Śakvarī* melody; it means you are blind to the underlying sacred rhythm. The vow forces an inner shift toward non-dual appreciation—training the mind to see that even the challenges of material life are structural components of an exquisitely ordered universe.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that *lokī bhavati* means the meditator is no longer restricted or trapped by material conditions; they gain spiritual access to higher planes of existence because they view all worlds as the body of the Lord. Speaking ill of the world is equivalent to defaming God's temple. Maintaining this vow keeps the devotee anchored in divine optimism, converting their everyday life into an act of unshakeable worship.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, cursing the world or expressing nihilistic thoughts disrespects the *Loka-devatās* (the guardians of the spheres) appointed by Viṣṇu. Genuine spiritual maturity requires honoring the cosmic hierarchy and recognizing that this world is a highly purposeful training ground. Observing this discipline strictly brings health, a steady home, and an excellent spiritual reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: अजा हिंकारोऽवयोः प्रस्तावो गाव उद्गीथोऽश्वाः प्रतिहारः पुरुषो निधनमेता रेवत्यः पशुषु प्रोताः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ajā hiṃkāro'vayaḥ prastāvo gāva udgītho'śvāḥ pratihāraḥ puruṣo nidhanametā revatyaḥ paśuṣu protāḥ || 1 ||
