verses.trans: Goats (Ajāḥ) are the syllable 'Hiṃ'; sheep (Avayaḥ) are the introductory praise (Prastāva); cattle and cows (Gāvaḥ) are the loud chant (Udgītha); horses (Aśvāḥ) are the response (Pratihāra); and the human caretaker (Puruṣaḥ) is the finale (Nidhana). These are the Revatī Sāmans as woven into domesticated animals.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that *Revatī* means 'wealthy,' 'abundant,' or 'rich in nourishing assets.' In an agrarian Vedic ecosystem, true prosperity was measured by livestock (*paśu*). The Upanishad organizes these animals into a liturgical hierarchy. Goats (*ajāḥ*), being agile, quick-footed, and the first to sound, represent *Hiṃkāra*. Sheep (*avayaḥ*), moving together in gentle herds, represent *Prastāva*. Cattle and cows (*gāvaḥ*), who provide the supreme life-giving dairy products for domestic health and sacred fire offerings, are crowned as the core *Udgītha*. Horses (*aśvāḥ*), providing power, defense, and long-range transport, act as the stable *Pratihāra*. Finally, the human being (*puruṣaḥ*), who houses, guards, feeds, and relies on these animals for survival, serves as the *Nidhana* where this collective lifecycle finds its ultimate purpose and shelter. This teaches the seeker to see our relationship with the animal kingdom not as a system of cruel exploitation, but as a reciprocal, musical symphony.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this biological group as a beautiful display of the Lord's provider energy (*vibhūti*). Animals are living extensions of divine wealth meant to support human life and righteous duties (*dharma*). Placing the cow at the center as the *Udgītha* highlights the pure, sacred nature of the bovine form. The human being as *Nidhana* acts as a reminder that humanity's true role is to be a protective, loving guardian of the creatures entrusted to them by the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the *Revatī-Sāman* is supervised by specialized wealth-granting deities acting under the sovereign rule of Lord Viṣṇu. The term *protam* signifies that the food chain and animal domestication are not human inventions or evolutionary accidents. They are bound together by an unchangeable divine law designed to keep human communities alive, safe, and focused on the cosmic layout established by the Supreme Person.

verses.dev: स य एवमेता रेवत्यः पशुषु प्रोता वेद पशुमान्भवति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या पशुन्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametā revatyaḥ paśuṣu protā veda paśumān bhavati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā paśunna nindettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows these Revatī Sāmans as woven into animals becomes rich in livestock; he attains a full, radiant lifespan, and grows great in children, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to speak ill of or abuse animals.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the ethical responsibility of the vow (*Vrata*) given here: *paśūn na nindet* (one must never criticize, insult, or curse animals). Humans often take their livestock for granted or vent their frustrations on them when animals behave unpredictably. Shankara clarifies that treating animals with harsh contempt or speaking ill of them is a direct assault on the *Revatī* melody; it shatters the non-dual awareness of the practitioner. The vow demands complete empathy, retraining the mind to treat all domestic animals as living partners in a cosmic song.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that *paśumān bhavati* signifies a heart that is rich in compassion and surrounded by the blessings of gentle creatures. Mistreating or yelling at animals is a direct insult to the indwelling Lord (*Antaryāmin*) who resides within them. Maintaining this vow of gentle non-injury (*ahiṃsā*) keeps the home auspicious and ensures the family remains spiritually stable and aligned with God's loving care.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, speaking ill of or neglecting animals insults the minor deities who protect livestock and serve under Viṣṇu's command. True prosperity can never blossom where animals are abused or unappreciated. Honoring this vow with discipline ensures a harmonious household, stable livestock lines, long life, and an excellent spiritual reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: लोम हिंकारस्त्वक्प्रस्तावो मांसमुद्गीथोऽस्थि प्रतिहारो मज्जा निधनमेतद्यज्ञाज्ञीयमङ्गेषु प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: loma hiṃkārastvakprastāvo māṃsamudgītho'sthi pratihāro majjā nidhanametadyajñājñīyamaṅgeṣu protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The bodily hair (Loma) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the skin (Tvak) is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the flesh (Māṃsa) is the loud chant (Udgītha); the bone (Asthi) is the response (Pratihāra); and the marrow (Majjā) is the finale (Nidhana). This is the Yajñāyajñīya Sāman as woven into the limbs and bodily organs.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that the *Yajñāyajñīya-Sāman* gets its name from its opening ritual verse (*Yajñā-yajñā vo agnaye...*). This melody is deeply associated with stabilization, structure, and internal sacrificial offering. The Upanishad directs the seeker to map this sacred song inward, onto the very layers of their own physical body (*aṅgeṣu*). The outermost, fine boundary of bodily hair (*loma*) acts as the *Hiṃkāra*. The protective casing of skin (*tvak*) serves as the *Prastāva*. The vital substance of muscular flesh (*māṃsa*), which holds the body's dynamic energy, is crowned as the central *Udgītha*. The sturdy skeletal framework of bones (*asthi*), which resists gravity and preserves the body's structure, acts as the *Pratihāra*. Finally, the deepest, hidden core inside the bone—the marrow (*majjā*)—serves as the *Nidhana*, the ultimate place of quietude and storage. By meditating this way, the seeker breaks free from viewing the body as a mere bag of gross matter, transforming it instead into a breathing, resonant instrument of a cosmic ritual.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this somatic alignment to show that the physical body is fundamentally a living temple (*dehasthala*) designed for the service of the indwelling Lord. The layering from hair to marrow mirrors the nested courts of a sanctuary. Placing the flesh at the center as the *Udgītha* highlights that our physical vitality must be spent in active service, while the marrow as *Nidhana* shows that the deepest core of human anatomy is meant to store the silent, nourishing peace of divine devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the *Yajñāyajñīya-Sāman* is supervised by internal bio-deities (*dehastha-devatās*) who direct metabolic and structural growth under the sovereign rule of Lord Viṣṇu. The term *protam* emphasizes that our physiology is not a product of random cellular mechanics. The body is tightly organized by an intelligent divine layout, reminding the soul that every tissue and bone must be kept pure and oriented toward its true spiritual duty (*dharma*).

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्यज्ञाज्ञीयमङ्गेषु प्रोतं वेदाङ्गी भवति नाङ्गेन विहूर्छति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या संवत्सरं मांसं नाश्नीयात्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadyajñājñīyamaṅgeṣu protaṃ vedaṅgī bhavati nāṅgena vihūrchati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā saṃvatsaraṃ māṃsaṃ nāśnīyāttadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Yajñāyajñīya Sāman as woven into the limbs becomes whole in body (Aṅgī), never suffers structural deformity or crippling breakdown of his limbs, enjoys a full and vibrant lifespan, and grows great in children, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be to abstain from eating meat for a full year, or never to eat it at all.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the exceptional somatic consequence and vow (*Vrata*) attached to this practice: *saṃvatsaraṃ māṃsaṃ nāśnīyāt* (one should not eat meat for a year, or ideally, drop it altogether). The reasoning is direct and non-dual: because flesh (*māṃsa*) has been specifically identified as the sacred *Udgītha* phase of the internal Yajñāyajñīya melody, eating the flesh of another living being would be an act of spiritual blindness—literally devouring the Udgītha. Keeping this vow preserves the practitioner's internal harmony, ensuring they become *aṅgī* (fully whole and integrated) and protecting them from physical or nervous collapse (*na vihūrchati*).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that abstaining from meat cleanses the mind (*sattva-śuddhi*) and aligns the seeker with the principle of universal compassion. Since the indwelling Lord resides within all bodies, consuming flesh harms the temple of God in another creature. Following this discipline builds a clean, resilient body that can easily sustain deep meditation and host the unclouded light of divine grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, eating meat disrupts the internal subtle channels (*nāḍīs*) and angers the deities who govern human physiology. Observing this vow of restraint directly honors the life-force managed by Viṣṇu. Following this discipline brings great physical stamina, family stability, a healthy lineage, and an unblemished spiritual reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: अग्निर्हिंकारो वायुः प्रस्ताव आदित्य उद्गीथो नक्षत्राणि प्रतिहारश्चन्द्रमा निधनमेतद्राजनं देवतासु प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: agnirhiṃkāro vāyuḥ prastāva āditya udgītho nakṣatrāṇi pratihāraścandramā nidhanametadrājanaṃ devatāsu protam || 1 ||
verses.trans: Fire (Agni) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; Wind (Vāyu) is the introductory praise (Prastāva); the Sun (Āditya) is the loud chant (Udgītha); the Stars (Nakṣatrāṇi) are the response (Pratihāra); and the Moon (Candramas) is the finale (Nidhana). This is the Rājana Sāman as woven into the deities and cosmic forces.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that *Rājana* etymologically means 'splendid,' 'shining,' or 'kingly' (*rāj*), making it perfectly suited for the radiant deities and cosmic forces (*devatās*). The text organizes these macrocosmic powers into a beautiful sequence. Fire (*agni*), the closest and most immediate terrestrial light, serves as *Hiṃkāra*. Wind (*vāyu*), the pervasive atmosphere that fans and moves life, represents the *Prastāva*. The Sun (*āditya*), standing as the brilliant, supreme king of day and life, is crowned as the central *Udgītha*. The constellation of Stars (*nakṣatrāṇi*), which stand like defensive sentinels or counter-points scattered across space, act as the *Pratihāra*. Finally, the Moon (*candramas*), which provides the soothing night sanctuary where the minds of creatures rest and rejuvenate, serves as the *Nidhana*. This meditation trains the seeker's eye to look at skyward phenomena not as empty matter, but as a vast celestial song.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this celestial mapping to show how the bright administrative powers of the universe reflect the splendor of the Supreme Lord. Placing the Sun at the center as the *Udgītha* highlights that all light ultimately traces back to the primary source of illumination, while the Moon as *Nidhana* reassures the devotee that the comforting, cool grace of God is the ultimate resting harbor for the travel-weary mind.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the *Rājana-Sāman* is directed by the cosmic administrators who manage planetary cycles under the absolute, independent rule of Lord Viṣṇu. The word *protam* indicates that the interactions of elements, light, and stars are tightly coordinated. They follow an unchangeable divine layout designed to hold the universe steady, proving that creation operates within a flawless blueprint engineered by the Sovereign Being.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतद्राजनं देवतासु प्रोतं वेद एतासामेव देवतानां सलोकतां सायुज्यं गच्छति सर्वमायुरेति ज्योग्जीवति महान्प्रजया पशुभिर्भवति महान्कीर्त्या ब्राह्मणान्न निन्देत्तद्व्रतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametadrājanaṃ devatāsu protaṃ veda etāsāmeva devatānāṃ salokatāṃ sāyujyaṃ gacchati sarvamāyureti jyogjīvati mahān prajayā paśubhirbhavati mahān kīrtyā brāhmaṇānna nindettadvratam || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this Rājana Sāman as woven into the deities attains residency in the same world (Salokatā) and intimate communion (Sāyujya) with these very cosmic forces; he enjoys a full and glorious lifespan, and grows great in progeny, cattle, and renown. His sacred vow should be never to speak ill of or disrespect the keepers of sacred knowledge (Brāhmaṇas).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the unique metaphysical reward (*Salokatā* and *Sāyujya*) and the strict behavioral vow (*Vrata*) given here: *brāhmaṇān na nindet* (one must never criticize or speak ill of the Brāhmaṇas). Shankara notes that in this context, Brāhmaṇas represent the direct earthly guardians of the Vedic chants, rituals, and the cosmic knowledge that reveals these deities. Criticizing those who preserve this liturgical framework indicates a deep inner rebellion against the Sāman layout itself. Keeping this vow preserves the seeker's non-dual alignment, leading their consciousness to seamlessly merge with the cosmic fields of light.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that *Sāyujya* represents a profound spiritual state where the soul shares intimately in the qualities of divine light, freed from material ignorance. Respecting the true keepers of wisdom means valuing the line of spiritual teachers (*Guru-paramparā*). Maintaining this vow keeps the seeker's mind receptive to higher scriptural instructions, ensuring that their daily life remains bright and full of devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, speaking ill of genuine scholars who dedicate their lives to chanting the glories of Viṣṇu brings severe spiritual setbacks and blocks the mind. True cosmic alignment requires honoring the earthly hierarchy that links humanity to the gods. Following this discipline ensures material stability, a healthy family lineage, long life, and an excellent, enduring reputation (*kīrti*).

verses.dev: त्रयी विद्या हिंकारस्त्रयो लोकाः स प्रस्तावोऽग्निर्वायुरादित्याः स उद्गीथो नक्षत्राणि वयांसि मरीचयः स प्रतिहारः सर्पा गन्धर्वाः पितरस्तन्निधनमेतत्साम सर्वस्मिन्प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: trayī vidyā hiṃkārastrayo lokāḥ sa prastāvo'gnirvāyu rādityāḥ sa udgītho nakṣatrāṇi vayāṃsi marīcayaḥ sa pratihāraḥ sarpā gandharvāḥ pitarastannidhanametatsāma sarvasminprotam || 1 ||
verses.trans: The triple Vedic knowledge (Ṛg, Yajus, Sāma) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the three worlds (Earth, Atmosphere, Heaven) are the introductory praise (Prastāva); Fire, Wind, and Sun are the loud chant (Udgītha); the Stars, Birds, and Light-rays are the response (Pratihāra); and the Serpents, Celestial Musicians, and Ancestors are the finale (Nidhana). This is the all-inclusive Sāman as woven into everything.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that after detailing ten specific, localized variations of the Sāman, the Upanishad now opens the door to the ultimate *Sarva-Sāma*—the meditation on the universe as a singular, complete chant. The text creates groups of three to bind all vertical and horizontal layers of reality into the five chant divisions. The foundational grid of revelation, the *Trayī Vidyā* (the three Vedas), acts as *Hiṃkāra*. The spatial fields where life unfolds, the *Trayo Lokāḥ* (the three worlds), are the *Prastāva*. The primary energetic forces that light and move those worlds—Fire, Wind, and Sun—are crowned as the majestic *Udgītha*. The secondary cosmic networks—Stars, Birds in the air, and Light-rays—serve as the balancing *Pratihāra*. Finally, the subtler, hidden layers of life—Serpents (*sarpāḥ*), Gandharvas, and the Ancestors (*pitaraḥ*)—act as the *Nidhana*. Shankara explains that this total vision removes all sense of division; the seeker no longer sees separate pieces of matter, but a single, harmonized web where *Sarvam* (everything) is the Sāman itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that this all-inclusive grouping is an expression of the *Viśvarūpa* or cosmic body of the Supreme Lord. The universe is not an accidental collection of elements, but a beautifully orchestrated divine hierarchy. By bringing scriptural knowledge, physical worlds, celestial controllers, and ancestral spirits into one single musical matrix, the text guides the soul to see that everything is owned, filled, and harmonized by the indwelling Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that this universal integration is administered by the primary life-force, Mukhyaprāṇa, working directly under the absolute sovereignty of Lord Viṣṇu. The phrase *sarvasmin protam* (woven into everything) serves as a reminder of the universe's total dependency. No layer of reality—from the highest Veda to the lowest creeping serpent—can move or exist outside the rigid cosmic layout established by the independent will of God.

verses.dev: स य एवमेतत्साम सर्वस्मिन्प्रोतं वेद सर्वं ह भवति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametatsāma sarvasminprotaṃ veda sarvaṃ ha bhavati || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this all-inclusive Sāman as woven into everything becomes everything (Sarvaṃ ha bhavati).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a definitive non-dual commentary on *sarvaṃ ha bhavati* (he becomes everything). This is the apex reward of the meditation. When a practitioner completely drops their narrow identity as a single body and instead views every sound, world, creature, and light-ray as the unfolding of one vast Sāman chant, their ego-boundaries collapse. To realize that the cosmic harmony is identical to your inner Self is to *become* everything. This is not a physical shape-shifting, but an absolute expansion of consciousness where nothing remains separate from you, marking the transition from ritual tracking to liberation (*Mukti*).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that 'becoming everything' signifies the soul attaining the state of *Sādharmya*—sharing fully in the cosmic perspective, unconditioned joy, and infinite awareness of the Supreme Lord. The devotee is completely freed from the tiny prison of selfishness, experiencing the entire universe as an extension of God's glory and a playground for his own liberated consciousness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that a finite individual soul (*jīva*) can never literally become the infinite God. In this context, *sarvaṃ bhavati* means that the liberated soul gains unhindered access to all realms of creation and enjoys perfect harmony with the entire universe through the grace of Viṣṇu. Their will is perfectly aligned with the divine layout, so they face no boundaries, obstructions, or sorrow anywhere in existence.

verses.dev: तदेष श्लोको यदेषां पञ्चधा पञ्चधा त्रैण्यानि न तेभ्यस्त्यात्परमन्यदस्ति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tadeṣa śloko yadeṣāṃ pañcadhā pañcadhā traiṇyāni na tebhyastyātparamanyadasti || 3 ||
verses.trans: On this point there is this verse: 'There are things that are grouped by fives in three ways, and nothing exists that is higher or different from them.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara unpacks this dense, cryptic summary verse. The phrase *pañcadhā pañcadhā traiṇyāni* refers to the three distinct ways the fivefold Sāman categories have been mapped across reality: first to the cosmic worlds (Earth, Atmosphere, etc.), second to the physiological elements (Breath, Speech, Senses), and third to the elemental triads (Fire, Wind, Sun). By saying *na tebhyaḥ param asti* (nothing exists higher than them), the text confirms that this system encompasses all manifest reality. For the meditator, this means the entire universe has been gathered, organized, and dissolved into the Sāman framework, leaving no room for a separate, distracting world outside of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that this verse establishes the flawless completeness of the Lord's design. The threefold groupings of five represent the structural building blocks of the cosmic matrix (*Prapañca*). By recognizing that nothing exists outside this divine architecture, the devotee's mind settles into a state of total, restful surrender, seeing every experience as an interaction within the organized body of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this closing verse asserts the total sovereignty of the divine layout. The triads and fivefold classifications prove that creation is meticulously calculated and bound by numbers and laws. Nothing can slip through the cracks or operate independently of the primary rule of Viṣṇu, ensuring that the seeker can completely trust the cosmic government managed by the Supreme Person.

verses.dev: यस्तं वेद स सर्वविद्भवति न मां प्रति दिशो बलिं हरन्तीति सर्वमस्मीत्युपासीत तद्व्रतम् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yastaṃ veda sa sarvavidbhavati na māṃ prati diśo baliṃ harantīti sarvamasmītyupāseeta tadvratam || 4 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this becomes the knower of everything (Sarvavit). All the cardinal directions bring tribute and offerings to him. His sacred vow should be to meditate with the awareness: 'I am everything' (Sarvam asmīti). That is the vow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the final, supreme behavior vow (*Vrata*) of this section: *sarvam asmīti upāsīta* (one must meditate knowing 'I am all'). This is no longer a localized rule about not spitting or complaining; it is an absolute demand on identity. The sage must walk through the world with the unshakeable awareness that whatever they see, hear, or touch is an extension of their own universal being. The text notes that *diśo baliṃ haranti*—all directions bring tribute to him. Because he no longer fights the world as an outsider, the elements naturally serve, support, and honor him. He is *Sarvavit*, the knower of everything, because by knowing the underlying Sāman, he knows the heart of all things.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that when a devotee reaches this state of complete union, the whole world honors them because they see the Lord shining purely through them. The phrase 'I am everything' is understood from the perspective of the indwelling soul: 'Because the Lord is everything, and the Lord is my innermost Self, I am structurally unified with all creation.' This vow eliminates all pride, fear, and sense of lack, bathing the devotee in endless divine prosperity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the title *Sarvavit* (all-knowing) is granted to the devotee by the grace of Lord Viṣṇu, who reveals the inner mechanics of creation to his closest servants. The directions bringing tribute means that the planetary and directional deities (*Dikpālas*) cooperate with and protect the enlightened soul. The vow 'I am all' is practiced as an act of total surrender: 'I am completely filled, possessed, and sustained by the All-Pervading Lord, and my independent ego is nothing.' This keeps the seeker in a state of supreme humility and power, concluding the meditation chapter on a note of absolute victory.

verses.dev: विनर्दि साम्नोऽग्नेरुद्गीथस्तदनिरुक्तं प्रजापतेर्निरुक्तं सोमस्य मृदु लक्ष्णं वायोस्तीक्ष्णं क्रौञ्चं वृहस्पतेरपध्वान्तं यमस्य तानि सर्वाण्येवोपसेवेतार्णेयमेव तु यमस्य ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: vinardi sāmno'gnerudgīthastadaniruktaṃ prajāpaterniruktaṃ somasya mṛdu lakṣṇaṃ vāyostīkṣṇaṃ krauñcaṃ bṛhaspaterapadhvāntaṃ yamasya tāni sarvāṇyevopasevetāgneyameva tu yamasya || 1 ||
verses.trans: Of the musical modulations of the Sāman, the roaring tone (Vinardi) belongs to Agni; the undefined or unexpressed tone (Anirukta) belongs to Prajāpati; the defined or clear tone (Nirukta) belongs to Soma; the soft and smooth tone (Mṛdu-lakṣṇam) belongs to Vāyu; the sharp and piercing tone (Tīkṣṇam-krauñcam) belongs to Bṛhaspati; and the broken or dull tone (Apadhvāntam) belongs to Yama. A chanter should employ all these varieties, but avoid the tone belonging to Yama.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this section shifts from cosmic meditations to the precise acoustic discipline (*svara*) necessary for vocal projection, as phonetic purity directly impacts psychological stabilization. The text details six vocal qualities. The bellowing, deep-chested tone (*vinardi*) matches Agni's crackling fire. The deep, unuttered tone emerging from the navel (*anirukta*) mirrors Prajāpati's unmanifest potential. The clear, structured articulation (*nirukta*) aligns with Soma's clarity. The smooth, flowing whisper (*mṛdu*) reflects Vāyu's wind. The high, piercing tone resembling a crane's cry (*tīkṣṇam-krauñcam*) belongs to Bṛhaspati, the master of intellect. Finally, the raspy, dull sound (*apadhvāntam*), like a cracked bronze vessel, belongs to Yama, the god of death. Shankara emphasizes that while a master chanter understands all tones, they must strictly avoid Yama's flat, deadened tone (*varjayet*) in their ritual work, as it drains life-force and fractures the non-dual vitality of the breath.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this vocal taxonomy to demonstrate that sound modulation is a sacred pathway for tuning the individual soul's nervous system. Each phonetic quality is a direct attribute of the Lord's administrative powers (*vibhūtis*). Avoiding the tone of Yama is a liturgical instruction meant to protect the practitioner from descending into existential sorrow or depression, ensuring that vocal worship remains an uplifting, life-affirming dedication to the supreme indweller.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya notes that these acoustic qualities are supervised by specialized acoustic deities (*Svara-devatās*) acting under the supreme command of Lord Viṣṇu. Sound is an immutable physical layout. The prohibition against Yama's tone indicates that certain acoustic frequencies carry destructive kinetic energy. By choosing tones associated with Agni, Soma, and Bṛhaspati, the practitioner invokes purifying cosmic forces that stabilize the subtle physical vehicle for steady spiritual practice.

verses.dev: अमृतत्वं देवेभ्य आगायानीति प्रजया पशुभ्यो मनुष्येभ्यस्तृणमुदकमश्वेभ्यः स्वर्गं लोकं यजमानायान्नमात्मन आगायानीत्येतानि मनसा ध्यायन्नप्रमत्तः स्तुवीत ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: amṛtatvaṃ devebhya āgāyānīti prajayā paśubhyo manuṣyebhyastṛṇamudakamaśvebhyaḥ svargaṃ lokaṃ yajamānāyānnamātmana āgāyānītyetāni manasā dhyāyannapramattaḥ stuvīta || 2 ||
verses.trans: 'Let me sing for immortality for the deities; let me sing for progeny and cattle for humans; let me sing for grass and water for the livestock; let me sing for the heavenly world for the sacrificer; and let me sing for nourishment for myself.' Reflecting upon all these intentions in his mind, the chanter should sing the praises with absolute alertness.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the psychological frame of the chanter during execution. Vocalizing is never a thoughtless mechanical act; it requires absolute presence of mind (*apramattaḥ*). The chanter must intentionally direct the sound to benefit every layer of the cosmic network: invoking *amṛtatvam* (deathlessness) for the cosmic principles, peace for humanity, primary sustenance for the animal kingdom, higher spiritual planes for the seeker who sponsored the work, and basic energetic nourishment for themselves. In the non-dual view, this ensures that the ego does not hoard the spiritual fruit of the chant. The sound becomes a total altruistic offering that dissolves the separation between the individual singer and the global field.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that this verse reveals the true spirit of selfless service (*kaiṅkarya*). The chanter acts as an unselfish medium through whom divine grace flows to all living things. By meditating on the welfare of deities, humans, animals, and the soul together, the practice transforms a simple vocal exercise into a deep meditation on universal interconnectedness, fulfilling the Lord's desire for global welfare.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this verse defines the strict intentional blueprint required for successful worship. Every note must be explicitly dedicated to its proper recipient under the overarching rule of Viṣṇu. Lack of concentration (*pramāda*) ruins the sacrifice. Singing with a disciplined mind fixes the soul in its rightful place within the divine cosmic economy, bringing stability to both the material environment and the inner mind.

verses.dev: सर्वे स्वरा इन्द्रस्यात्मानः सर्व ऊष्माणः प्रजापतेरात्मानः सर्वे स्पर्शा मृत्योरात्मानस्तं यदि स्वरेषूपालभेतेन्द्रं शरणं प्रपन्नोऽभूवं स त्वा प्रतिवक्ष्यतीति स्वेनमेनं ब्रूयात् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sarve svarā indrasyātmānaḥ sarva ūṣmāṇaḥ prajāpaterātmānaḥ sarve sparśā mṛtyorātmānastaṃ yadi svareṣūpālabhetendraṃ śaraṇaṃ prapanno'bhūvaṃ sa tvā prativakṣyatīti svenamenaṃ brūyāt || 3 ||
verses.trans: All vowels (Svaras) are the embodiments of Indra; all sibilants (Ūṣmāṇas) are the embodiments of Prajāpati; all stop-consonants (Sparśas) are the embodiments of Death (Mṛtyu). If someone should criticize a chanter for his pronunciation of vowels, the chanter should say: 'I have taken refuge in Indra; he will answer you.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara details the specific phonetic assignment: vowels (*svaras*) represent Indra (the power of cosmic strength and flash illumination); sibilants like śa, ṣa, sa, ha (*ūṣmāṇas*) represent Prajāpati (the vital heat of generation); and stop-consonants from ka to ma (*sparśas*) represent Mṛtyu (the hard, solid boundaries of matter and death). This verse establishes an unshakeable spiritual confidence based on this alignment. If a critic finds fault with the chanter's vowels, the chanter does not react with defensive anger or insecurity. They rest knowing that because their vowels are dedicated entirely to Indra, any insult to the sound is an insult to Indra himself, who will naturally handle the obstruction.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that the alphabet itself is structured as the body of the Lord. Vowels carry the free flow of spirit, sibilants carry the warmth of life-breath, and consonants provide the firm boundaries. Trusting this sacred anatomy relieves the devotee from the fear of human criticism, allowing them to chant with absolute faith and deep peace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this verse reinforces the reality of the sound-controllers (*Varṇa-devatās*). Phonetics is a precise science under divine law. When a chanter performs their duty carefully and rests their mind in the supreme deity overseeing that specific sound, they are shielded from worldly doubts, and the underlying spiritual power of the liturgy remains completely undamaged.

verses.dev: अथ यद्येनमूंष्मसूपालभेत प्रजापतिं शरणं प्रपन्नोऽभूवं स त्वा प्रतिपेक्ष्यतीति स्वेनमेनं ब्रूयादथ यद्येनं स्पर्शेषूपालभेत मृत्युं शरणं प्रपन्नोऽभूवं स त्वा प्रतिधक्ष्यतीति स्वेनमेनं ब्रूयात् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadyenamūṣmasūpālabheta prajāpatiṃ śaraṇaṃ prapanno'bhūvaṃ sa tvā pratipekṣyatīti svenamenaṃ brūyādatha yadyenaṃ sparṣeṣūpālabheta mṛtyuṃ śaraṇaṃ prapanno'bhūvaṃ sa tvā pratidhakṣyatīti svenamenaṃ brūyāt || 4 ||
verses.trans: And if someone should criticize his sibilants, he should say: 'I have taken refuge in Prajāpati; he will crush you.' And if someone should criticize his consonants, he should say: 'I have taken refuge in Death; he will burn you up.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the remaining phonetic scenarios to close this defensive shield. If the sibilants (*ūṣmāṇas*) are mocked, the chanter invokes Prajāpati, the lord of vitality, who will shatter (*pratipekṣyati*) the critic's ignorance. If the hard consonants (*sparśas*) are criticized, the chanter invokes Mṛtyu (Death) itself, stating that Death will burn away (*pratidhakṣyati*) the critic's arrogance. Non-dually, this shows that the enlightened chanter does not engage in petty scholarly debates. By identifying their voice with the primal cosmic forces, the chanter's speech becomes completely unassailable, resting securely within the absolute power of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this severe language serves to warn against treating sacred sound with casual, arrogant human analysis. Sound is a manifestation of divine energy. When a chanter has surrendered their tongue to God, the cosmic laws themselves step in to defend the purity of the offering, keeping the devotee safe from external distractions.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this final verse demonstrates the absolute protection guaranteed to those who align with the cosmic layout engineered by Viṣṇu. When you know which specific deity commands a sound and you take refuge there, your performance is backed by the entire weight of the universe, and all negative criticisms are naturally neutralized by the sovereign laws of time and death.

verses.dev: त्रयो धर्मस्कन्धा यज्ञोऽध्ययनं दानमिति प्रथमस्तप एव द्वितीयो ब्रह्मचार्याचार्यकुलवासी तृतीयोऽत्यन्तमात्मानमाचार्यकुलेऽवसादयन्सर्व एते पुण्यलोका भवन्ति ब्रह्मसंस्थोऽमृतत्वमेति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: trayo dharmaskandhā yajño'dhyayanaṃ dānamiti prathamastapa eva dvitīyo brahmacāryācāryakulavāsī tṛtīyo'tyantamātmānamācāryakule'vasādayansarva ete puṇyalokā bhavanti brahmasaṃstho'mṛtatvameti || 1 ||
verses.trans: There are three branches of duty (Dharma-skandhas). Sacrifice, study, and charity form the first. Austerity or asceticism (Tapas) is the second. Dwelling as a lifelong student in the house of the teacher, completely mortifying the body, is the third. All of these attain meritorious worlds, but the one who stands firmly grounded in Brahman (Brahmasaṃstha) attains immortality (Amṛtatvam).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara builds a foundational pillar of Advaita philosophy upon this verse, using it to define the clear boundary between ritual actions (*Karma*) and absolute Knowledge (*Jñāna*). He explains that the *dharmaskandhas* represent the structured lifestyle stages (*Āśramas*). The first branch covers the householder (*Gṛhastha*), focusing on sacrifice, study, and giving. The second is the forest-hermit (*Vānaprastha*), defined by deep introspection and austerity (*Tapas*). The third is the lifelong celibate student (*Naiṣṭhika Brahmacārī*). Shankara strongly emphasizes that while all three lifestyles generate immense structural merit (*puṇyalokāḥ*), their rewards are strictly bound by time and space. In stark contrast, the phrase *Brahmasaṃsthaḥ amṛtatvam eti* introduces an entirely different category. A *Brahmasaṃstha* is someone whose mind has completely ceased to wander among material objects, remaining permanently anchored, settled, and dissolved in the non-dual reality of Brahman. Shankara argues that this state belongs exclusively to the formal renunciate (*Sannyāsī*), who has dropped all social duties to merge entirely into pure Consciousness, thereby claiming true, unchangeable immortality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja structuralizes this passage differently, arguing that the state of resting firmly in Brahman (*Brahmasaṃsthā*) is not restricted to a single external stage of life like formal renunciation. Instead, it represents an internal quality of deep devotion that can—and should—be perfected within any rightful stage of life. Whether one is a householder practicing charity or a student practicing self-discipline, if every single action is consciously surrendered to the Supreme Lord as an act of loving worship, that person is truly a *Brahmasaṃstha*. Immortality is not achieved by merely dropping social roles, but by recognizing that the soul is eternally dependent on, and filled by, the indwelling Godhead.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the classification of duties proves that the cosmic layout established by Lord Viṣṇu requires distinct human paths to maintain social and spiritual balance. No one should foolishly abandon their designated *Dharma*. However, performing duties mechanically only grants temporary, celestial rewards (*puṇyalokāḥ*). Real liberation and immortality are granted exclusively to the *Brahmasaṃstha*—the soul who possesses clear, unshakeable theological knowledge of Viṣṇu's absolute independence and who remains completely anchored in that truth while executing their worldly duties.

verses.dev: प्रजापतिर्लोकानभ्यतपत्तेषां अभितप्तानां रसान्प्रावृहदग्निं पृथिव्या वायुमन्तरिक्षादादित्यं दिवः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: prajāpatirlokānabhyatapatteṣāṃ abhitaptānāṃ rasānprāvṛhadagniṃ pṛthivyā vāyumantarīkṣādādityaṃ divaḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: Prajāpati brooded over the worlds. From them, thus brooded over, he extracted their essential essences: Fire (Agni) from the Earth, Wind (Vāyu) from the atmosphere, and the Sun (Āditya) from Heaven.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that this narrative shows the orderly distillation of cosmic layers, demonstrating how gross matter leads back to subtle energy. Prajāpati's 'brooding' (*Abhitaptānāṃ*) is not emotional anxiety, but the intense focus of cosmic intelligence. By distilling the planetary fields, he extracts their radiant cores. This teaches the seeker that beneath the solid surface of everyday objects lies a vibrant, energetic matrix that can be accessed through disciplined meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views this distillation as a demonstration of the Lord's flawless blueprint. Creation is organized like a nested series of rooms. The Supreme Lord, operating through Prajāpati, highlights Fire, Wind, and Sun as the vital controllers of their respective spheres, showing the devotee that every layer of physical space is carefully managed by a divine representative.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this verse confirms that the material universe operates under a strict, governed hierarchy. Material elements do not exist independently; they are supervised by specific elemental deities who answer directly to the sovereign command of Viṣṇu, who guides the entire evolutionary process from behind the scenes.

verses.dev: स एतास्तिस्रो देवता अभ्यतपत्तासां अभितप्तानां रसान्प्रावृहदग्नेर्ऋचो वायोर्यजूंषि आदित्यात्सामानि ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa etāstisro devatā abhyatapattāsāṃ abhitaptānāṃ rasānprāvṛhadagnerṛco vāyoryajūṃṣi ādityātsāmāni || 3 ||
verses.trans: He brooded over those three deities. From them, thus brooded over, he extracted their essences: the Ṛg verses from Fire, the Yajus formulas from Wind, and the Sāman chants from the Sun.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks this deeper distillation into the realm of sacred sound and knowledge. Cosmic light-forces are revealed to be identical to internal spiritual vibrations. The physical fire contains the seed of articulation (*Ṛg*); the invisible movement of the wind contains the dynamic action-formulas (*Yajus*); and the brilliant majesty of the sun contains the ultimate harmony (*Sāma*). This non-dual connection shows that scriptural revelation is not an arbitrary human invention, but an intrinsic, sonic property of the cosmos itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the extraction of the three Vedas from the primary deities confirms the sacred nature of scriptural sound. The Vedas are the breath of the Lord. By anchoring the chants to the Sun, the text reminds the seeker that the ultimate purpose of all liturgy is to illuminate the dark corners of the mind and guide the soul back to the supreme source of light.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this step demonstrates the divine origin and structural purity of the Vedas. The sacred sound-vibrations are managed by the highest administrative gods under Viṣṇu's authority. This systematic extraction ensures that the liturgical layout remains completely uncorrupted, serving as a reliable map for humanity's spiritual return.

verses.dev: स एतां त्रयीं विद्यामभ्यतपत्तस्या अभितप्ताया रसान्प्रावृहद्भूरिति ऋग्भ्यो भुव इत्यजुर्भ्यः स्वरिति सामभ्यः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa etāṃ trayīṃ vidyāmabhyatapattasyā abhitaptāyā rasānprāvṛhadbhūriti ṛgbhyo bhuva ityajurbhyaḥ svariti sāmabhyaḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: He brooded over this triple knowledge. From it, thus brooded over, he extracted its essential essences: the syllable 'Bhūḥ' from the Ṛg verses, the syllable 'Bhuvaḥ' from the Yajus formulas, and the syllable 'Svaḥ' from the Sāman chants.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara details the final distillation of all text into the three great mystical utterances (*Mahāvyāhṛtis*): *Bhūḥ*, *Bhuvaḥ*, and *Svaḥ*. Thousands of verses are condensed into three simple seed-sounds. This is a critical lesson in non-dual concentration: it shows the mind how to fold vast, complex outer information back into simple, powerful interior anchors, preparing the consciousness for silent meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school highlights that the *Vyāhṛtis* contain the concentrated potency of all scriptural wisdom. They are not mere words, but sonic seeds representing the entire body of the Lord. Chanting them with deep devotion invokes the protective presence of the Almighty across all three spheres of existence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that these three utterances are directly supervised by the primary forms of the Lord. They hold the structural keys to the cosmic layout. Mastering these sounds under the guidance of a qualified teacher connects the seeker directly to the divine laws that govern the physical, atmospheric, and celestial planes.

verses.dev: तद्भूरेवर्चोऽभ्यौहद्भुव इति यजूंष्यभ्यौहत्स्वरिति सामान्यभ्यौहत् ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tadbhūrevarco'bhyauhaddhuva iti yajūṃṣyabhyauhatsvariti sāmānyabhyauhat || 5 ||
verses.trans: He condensed the Ṛg verses into 'Bhūḥ'; he condensed the Yajus formulas into 'Bhuvaḥ'; and he condensed the Sāman chants into 'Svaḥ'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara explains that this explicit reinforcement locks the distillation process in reverse, creating an absolute sonic bridge. The entire material universe and all scriptural knowledge are now firmly anchored to these three syllables. They act as a spiritual doorway, allowing the meditator to dissolve the diversity of the world into a unified point of focus.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that this compression reveals how the Lord makes vast cosmic truths accessible to the individual soul. One does not need to memorize endless texts; by resting mindfully in these concentrated sacred seeds, the devotee aligns perfectly with the entire divine order.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this verse underscores the precise, mathematical design of the liturgy. The compression is a deliberate functional layout engineered by the Supreme Person, providing a streamlined, highly effective path for the soul to focus its intent without getting lost in endless textual variations.

verses.dev: तद्यथा शङ्कुना सर्वाणि पर्णानि संतृण्णान्येवमोङ्कारेण सर्वा वाक्संतृण्णोङ्कार एवेदं सर्वमोङ्कार एवेदं सर्वम् ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: tadyathā śaṅkunā sarvāṇi parṇāni saṃtṛṇṇānyevamoṅkāreṇa sarvā vāksaṃtṛṇṇoṅkāra evedaṃ sarvamoṅkāra evedaṃ sarvam || 6 ||
verses.trans: Just as all leaves are held together by a rib or stalk, even so is all speech held together by the syllable Om (Oṃkāra). Verily, the syllable Om is all this; yea, the syllable Om is all this.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a magnificent concluding commentary on this verse, using the vivid analogy of a leaf's veins held together by a central stalk (*śaṅkunā*). He explains that *Oṃkāra* (Om) is the absolute primordial matrix of all sound and existence. If you trace any word or sound back to its root, it emerges from the throat and mouth as 'A', rolls over the tongue as 'U', and seals at the lips as 'M'. Therefore, all speech (*sarvā vāk*), and by extension all objects named by speech, are woven around Om. By stating *oṅkāra evedaṃ sarvam* (Om is indeed everything), the Upanishad delivers its final non-dual punch: the entire universe is nothing but a manifestation of Om. When the seeker meditates on Om, they are not repeating a random chant; they are resting directly in the pure, undivided reality of Brahman, completing the journey from ritual action to absolute liberation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views Om as the primary, unshakeable name of the Supreme Lord (*Praṇava*). Just as a central stalk sustains a leaf, the power and presence of God holds all created worlds together, preventing them from falling into non-existence. Repeating Om is an act of total surrender to the indwelling Master who coordinates every breath and atom in creation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that Om is the supreme acoustic signature of Lord Viṣṇu, containing within its three structural sounds the primary operations of creation, maintenance, and dissolution. It is the core anchor of the entire cosmic layout. Every prayer, sacrifice, and action must be marked by Om to show total submission to the independent will of the Sovereign Ruler, who remains the ultimate source and protector of everything.

verses.dev: ब्रह्मवादिनो वदन्ति यत्साम्नां हृदयानि तानि देवेष्विाथ यत्साम्नां हृदयं तदस्मिन्प्रतिष्ठितमिति कथमत्र प्रतितिष्ठेत्कथमुत्तरस्मिन्प्रतितिष्ठेदिति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: brahmavādino vadanti yatsāmnāṃ hṛdayāni tāni deveṣviātha yatsāmnāṃ hṛdayaṃ tadasminpratiṣṭhitamiti kathamatra pratiṣṭhetkathamuttarasminpratiṣṭhediti || 1 ||
verses.trans: The knowers of Brahman (Brahmavādins) ask: 'The hearts of the Sāman chants reside in the gods; but where does the heart of the Sāman itself rest when it is offered here? How should the chanter be established in this world, and how should he be established in the higher world?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that the question posed here is the pivot of ritual responsibility. If the Sāman chants are structurally linked to the cosmic gods, how does the fallible human ritualist, standing in the mundane world, maintain an unbroken connection to that higher reality? The question seeks the 'anchor' of the sacrificial act: what grounds the human performance so that it reaches the Divine?
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this is a query about the mediation of sacrifice. The ritual is a bridge. To be established (*pratiṣṭhita*) is to ensure that one's offerings are correctly received and that the fruit of the action is realized, both here in the physical realm and later in the divine realm.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the question highlights the dependency of human action. The ritualist cannot sustain the Sāman on their own; they need a solid foundation—a divine support system—to ensure their sacrifice achieves its goal without suffering the defects of human imperfection.

verses.dev: अथ यत्प्रार्थितं तद्धुत्वा यद्धुत्वा तदूर्ध्वमथ यत्प्रार्थितं तद्धुत्वा यद्धुत्वा तदूर्ध्वं यद्विज्ञानं तद्धुत्वा यद्विज्ञानं तदूर्ध्वमित्यग्निं च पृथिवीं च चक्षुश्चेति वदन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: atha yatprārthitaṃ taddhutvā yaddhutvā tadūrdhvamatha yatprārthitaṃ taddhutvā yaddhutvā tadūrdhvaṃ yadvijñānaṃ taddhutvā yadvijñānaṃ tadūrdhvamityagniṃ ca pṛthivīṃ ca cakṣuśceti vadanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: Having offered into the morning light, one offers into the fire, the earth, and the eye—for they say that the dawn offering rises to these. Having offered into the midday light, one offers into the wind, the atmosphere, and the life-breath (Prāṇa). Having offered into the evening light, one offers into the sun, the heaven, and the ear.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara interprets this as a highly sophisticated method of 'offering into the Self.' The ritualist does not merely drop ghee into fire; they identify the ritual act with the cosmic elements. The morning offering is linked to the Earth-Fire-Eye triad; the midday to the Atmosphere-Wind-Breath triad; the evening to the Heaven-Sun-Ear triad. This total synchronization of sense-organs with cosmic elements ensures that the ritualist is never disconnected from the Absolute.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school views these rectifications as an integration of the ritualist's daily life into the body of the Lord. By anchoring the offerings to the senses and the elements, the householder ensures that every moment of the day is a continuous sacrifice (*Yajña*), keeping the soul perpetually in touch with the Divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya emphasizes that these specific offerings serve as *Prāyaścitta* (atonement/rectification). If any error is made during the day, these formulas at the dawn, noon, and twilight junctures bridge the gap. They essentially align the ritualist's errors with the corrective power of the deities, ensuring the sacrifice remains acceptable to Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: स य एवं वेद स ब्रह्मविद्भवति तदेतद्ब्रह्म प्राह प्रजापतिः प्रजापतये मनुर्मनवे प्रजाः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evaṃ veda sa brahmavidbhavati tadetadbrahma prāha prajāpatiḥ prajāpataye manurmanave prajāḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this becomes a knower of Brahman (Brahmavit). This sacred knowledge, Prajāpati proclaimed to Manu, Manu to his descendants, and so the lineage continues.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara concludes that the one who understands these rituals not as hollow actions but as cosmic integrations is a *Brahmavit*. The ritual becomes a path to Brahman because the ritualist sees through the symbols to the underlying reality. The lineage of teaching (*Paramparā*) is mentioned to show the authority and timelessness of this knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja notes that the preservation of this knowledge through Manu emphasizes the tradition's role in guiding humanity. It is not just information; it is a transformative legacy that enables every generation to live a life anchored in devotion and ritual purity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya observes that the descent of this knowledge from Prajāpati to Manu establishes the *Āgama* (scriptural) validity of these ritual rectifications. It confirms that these are not human inventions but divinely revealed truths provided for the soul's success in the material world.

verses.dev: असौ वा आदित्यो देवमधु तस्य द्यौरेव तिरश्चीनवंशोऽन्तरिक्षमपूपो मरीचयः पुत्राः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: asau vā ādityo devamadhu tasya dyaureva tiraścīnavaṃśo'ntarikṣamapūpo marīcayaḥ putrāḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Verily, yonder Sun is the honey of the gods. The heavenly realm (Dyaus) is the cross-beam that holds the hive; the intermediate space (Antarikṣa) is the honeycomb; and the light-rays (Marīcayaḥ) are the bees that produce the honey.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this is a highly symbolic meditation (*upāsanā*) designed to elevate the seeker’s perspective from seeing the sun as a mere ball of burning gas to seeing it as a divine storehouse of nectar (*madhu*). The sun represents the concentration of all cosmic merit. The rays are the 'bees' because they gather the nectar of human actions (sacrifices and prayers) and bring them back to the solar center. By meditating on the sun as a 'honeycomb' of divine rewards, the seeker prepares the mind to eventually see past the sun to the Source of light itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights the majesty of the Sun as the *Vibhūti* (glory) of the Lord. The gods 'gather' the nectar of our ritual offerings to the Sun, which acts as the divine administrator. This illustrates the beautiful, reciprocal relationship between humanity's righteous actions and the cosmic order managed by the Supreme.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the Sun is not only the honey but the primary administrator of all ritual outcomes (*Phala-pradātā*) under the Lord's command. This meditation serves to align the mind with the celestial hierarchy, recognizing that every drop of 'nectar'—every positive result of our lives—is being collected and organized by solar deities who operate directly under Viṣṇu's will.

verses.dev: तस्य यानि पूर्वानीतराणि रश्मयस्ता एव अस्य प्राच्यो मधुनाड्यस्तद्यदृच एव मधुकृत ऋग्वेद एव पुष्पं ता आपोऽमृतं दुहन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tasya yāni pūrvānītarāṇi raśmayastā eva asya prācyo madhunāḍyastadyadṛca eva madhukṛta ṛgveda eva puṣpaṃ tā āpo'mṛtaṃ duhanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: The rays of the sun that extend to the East are its eastern honey-cells. The Ṛg verses are the bees; the Ṛgveda is the flower; and those waters (the rays) produce the nectar of immortality (Amṛta).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara clarifies that the Ṛgveda is called the 'flower' because it provides the essential knowledge and praise that the 'bees' (rays/meditators) convert into the honey of immortality. This verse teaches that the Vedas are the source of spiritual sustenance. The nectar produced is not physical honey, but the *Amṛta*—the deathless realization of Brahman—which the seeker gathers by performing Vedic duties with enlightened awareness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school emphasizes that the Vedas are the perfect 'blossom' of divine wisdom. By 'extracting' the meaning of these verses, the seeker gains access to the divine grace that sustains the universe. The honey is the result of applying Vedic knowledge to one's life, creating a continuous supply of spiritual nourishment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the rays specifically directed to the East are the channels for wisdom and new beginnings. By connecting the Ṛgveda to these eastern rays, the text shows that the path of knowledge is the primary way to gather divine grace. The nectar produced is the knowledge of Viṣṇu, which is the only true source of immortality.

verses.dev: ता वा एता दक्षिणान्या रश्मयस्त एव अस्य दक्षिण मधुकृतो यजुर्वेद एव पुष्पं ता आपोऽमृतं दुहन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tā vā etā dakṣiṇānyā raśmayastā eva asya dakṣiṇa madhukṛto yajurveda eva puṣpaṃ tā āpo'mṛtaṃ duhanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, those rays of the sun that extend to the South are its southern honey-cells. The Yajus formulas are the bees; the Yajurveda is the flower; and those waters (the rays) produce the nectar of immortality (Amṛta).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes the systematic nature of this meditation. Just as the eastern rays are associated with the Ṛgveda (Praise), the southern rays are associated with the Yajurveda (Action/Ritual). In the symbolic language of the Upanishad, the South is the direction of the Pitṛs (ancestors) and ritual duty. Therefore, the Yajurveda, which governs the precise performance of sacrifice, acts as the flower. The nectar produced here is the spiritual refinement resulting from disciplined, karma-based service, which eventually leads the seeker toward the higher realization of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this verse anchors ritual action (*Karma*) in the grace of the Sun. By performing the Yajus-based sacrifices with the Sun as the witness and receptacle, the householder’s actions are purified and elevated. The southern direction represents the domain of maintenance and ancestral continuity, sustained by the divine light.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that the southern rays specifically channel the energy required for the maintenance of the world. The Yajurveda provides the rules for sacrifice, which keeps the cosmic engine running. The 'nectar' produced here is the divine order (*Ṛta*) that allows the soul to perform its duties in harmony with the Lord's administrative plan.

verses.dev: ता वा एताः प्रतीच्या रश्मयस्त एव अस्य प्रतीच्यौ मधुनाड्यः सामवेद एव मधुकृतः सामवेद एव पुष्पं ता आपोऽमृतं दुहन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tā vā etāḥ pratīcyā raśmayastā eva asya pratīcyau madhunāḍyaḥ sāmaveda eva madhukṛtaḥ sāmaveda eva puṣpaṃ tā āpo'mṛtaṃ duhanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: Those rays of the sun that extend to the West are its western honey-cells. The Sāman melodies are the bees; the Sāmaveda is the flower; and those waters (the rays) produce the nectar of immortality (Amṛta).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara observes that the West is the direction of setting—the transition toward the inward, meditative state. Here, the Sāmaveda (the Veda of music and ecstasy) acts as the flower. This signifies that as the day ends and the Sun moves West, the seeker should move from external ritual (East/South) to internal harmony and deep, meditative absorption (West). The nectar gathered here is the bliss of realization.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights that the Sāman chants are the most direct expressions of joy and praise. When the soul meditates upon the Sun through the Sāman rays, they gather the 'honey' of direct experience—the ecstatic realization of the Lord's beauty, which prepares the heart for complete surrender.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the western rays represent the culmination of spiritual practice. The Sāman melodies harmonize the soul's relationship with Viṣṇu. Gathering this 'honey' leads to the final, peaceful state of the devotee, where their understanding of the divine glory is fully mature.

verses.dev: ता वा एता उदीच्या रश्मयस्त एव अस्य उदञ्चो मधुनाड्योऽथर्वान्गिरस एव मधुकृत इतिहासापुराणमेव पुष्पं ता आपोऽमृतं दुहन्ति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tā vā etā udīcyā raśmayastā eva asya udañco madhunāḍyo'tharvāṅgirasa eva madhukṛta itihāsapurānameva puṣpaṃ tā āpo'mṛtaṃ duhanti || 3 ||
verses.trans: Those rays of the sun that extend to the North are its northern honey-cells. The Atharvan and Aṅgirasa (Atharvaveda) are the bees; the Itihāsa-Purāṇas (epics and histories) are the flower; and those waters produce the nectar of immortality (Amṛta).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the North is the direction of illumination and transcendence. The inclusion of Atharvaveda and Itihāsa-Purāṇas shows that even the practical, historical, and mythological aspects of the tradition contain the essence of the Divine. All aspects of human story and tradition are 'bees' that can extract the nectar of truth if viewed through the lens of the Sun's light.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja emphasizes that history (*Itihāsa*) and tradition (*Purāṇa*) are essential for the devotee, as they provide the narrative evidence of the Lord's *Avatāras* and mercy. These stories are the 'blossoms' that teach the soul how to love and serve God effectively in the world.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva adds that the Atharvaveda and the historical traditions contain the deeper mysteries and specific techniques for overcoming the obstacles of material life. By meditating on these as northern rays, the devotee gains the clarity needed to navigate the final stages of the path toward the Lord.

verses.dev: अथ वा एत ऊर्ध्वा रश्मयस्त एव अस्योर्ध्वा मधुनाड्यो गुह्या आदेशा एव मधुकृतो ब्रह्मैव पुष्पं ता आपोऽमृतं दुहन्ति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha vā eta ūrdhvā raśmayastā eva asyordhvā madhunāḍyo guhyā ādeśā eva madhukṛta brahmaiva puṣpaṃ tā āpo'mṛtaṃ duhanti || 4 ||
verses.trans: Now, those rays of the sun that extend Upward are its upper honey-cells. The hidden, secret teachings (Guhyā-ādeśāḥ) are the bees; Brahman itself is the flower; and those waters produce the nectar of immortality (Amṛta).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara reaches the climax of this meditation: the zenith. Here, the 'bee' is not a Veda, but the *Guhyā-ādeśāḥ*—the secret, direct instructions of the Upanishads—and the 'flower' is Brahman itself. This signifies that the highest meditation transcends all scriptural texts and arrives at the non-dual realization of the Infinite. The nectar is pure, unadulterated Mokṣa.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the upward rays signify the soul's final ascent toward Vaikuṇṭha. The 'hidden instructions' are the teachings of the Guru that guide the soul to look beyond the material rays to the Supreme Lord, who is the true source of all light and grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the upward direction represents the state of liberation (*Mukti*). Brahman is the flower of all flowers, the source of all nectar. To meditate on the upward rays is to focus the mind entirely on the Supreme Lord, seeking the final, eternal joy that only the realization of Viṣṇu can grant.

verses.dev: ता एता रश्मयः संतान्त्यन्ते ते देवा एतदेवामृतं पुंस्त्वेनाभिसंविशन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tā etā raśmayaḥ saṃtānyante te devā etadevāmṛtaṃ puṃstvenābhisaṃviśanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Those rays (which have gathered the nectar) spread out and merge again into that same Honey; the gods, by drinking this nectar, enter into that immortality (the nature of the Sun/Brahman).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that the rays are not independent entities but extensions of the solar center. When they return, the illusion of separation dissolves. Similarly, the 'gods' and the 'nectar' are unified. This signifies that the practitioner who understands the interconnectedness of all rituals and knowledge realizes that the goal (Brahman) and the path (meditation) are one.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that the rays merging back into the honey represents the soul's return to the source of divine grace. The 'drinking of the nectar' is the soul’s participation in the infinite bliss of the Supreme Lord, becoming established in His presence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that this merging is not total identity, but a state of perfect harmony and union (*Sāyujya*) where the soul, having fulfilled its duty, experiences the nectar of divine presence without ever again suffering from material limitations.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवममृतं वेद स एतामेव देवतां प्रति पद्यते रश्मिभिरेव ऊर्ध्व आक्रम्यते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etadevamamṛtaṃ veda sa etāmeva devatāṃ prati padyate raśmibhireva ūrdhva ākramyate || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this nectar in this way attains that very deity (the Sun/Brahman); and having ascended via the rays, he reaches the ultimate height.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara emphasizes that the 'ascent' is not a physical travel through space, but a cognitive leap (*Jñāna-mārga*). By knowing that the Sun/Brahman is the self of all, the seeker transcends the cycle of time. The 'rays' are the clarity of insight that lifts the consciousness above the dualities of rising and setting, light and dark.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja describes the ascent as the journey of the liberated soul (*Arcirādi-mārga*) along the path of light, guided by the Lord to the supreme abode. The knowledge of the nectar is the essential requirement for this final journey.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya clarifies that the seeker, by the grace of Viṣṇu and the deity of the Sun, is granted the ability to ascend to the highest level of heaven, where they can forever enjoy the vision of the Lord's glory.

verses.dev: स वा एष न कदाचनास्तमेति न चोदेति । यदेतन्मधु तद्वेदैतद्ब्रह्म । तत्रैते देवाः सर्वायुर्भवन्ति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa vā eṣa na kadācanāstameti na codeti | yadetanmadhu tadvedaitadbrahma | tatraite devāḥ sarvāyurbhavanti || 3 ||
verses.trans: Verily, this Sun neither sets nor rises. It is always light. Knowing this Honey (nectar), one knows Brahman. There, the gods enjoy an eternal lifespan.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers the profound core of the *Madhu-Vidyā*: the Sun of pure Consciousness never rises or sets. Rising and setting are perceptions of the conditioned mind bound by time. For the one who knows the 'Honey' as Brahman, time itself is negated, and the light of the Self shines eternally without shadow.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja explains that this verse describes the state of the eternal realm (*Vaikuṇṭha*), where the light of the Lord is constant and unbroken. Those who attain this state—the gods and the liberated souls—dwell in an eternal present, participating in the Lord's endless glory.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this truth applies to the Supreme Lord, Viṣṇu, who is the Sun of all suns. He is beyond time, and those who reach Him, through the meditation on His glory, partake in His eternal life, forever liberated from the cycle of birth and death.

verses.dev: अथ येऽस्योदञ्चो रश्मयस्ता एवास्योदीच्यो मधुनाड्योऽथर्वाङ्गिरस एव मधुकृत इतिहासपुराणं पुष्पं ता अमृता आपः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha ye 'syodañco raśmayas tā evāsyodīcyo madhunāḍyo 'tharvāṅgirasa eva madhukṛta itihāsapurāṇaṃ puṣpaṃ tā amṛtā āpaḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, those rays of the sun toward the north are its northern honey-cells. The Atharva-Aṅgiras hymns are the bees; the Itihāsa-Purāṇa (legend and ancient lore) is the flower; the immortal waters are the nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara continues the allegory: just as bees extract honey from flowers, the Atharva hymns extracted the luminous essence from sacred narrative. All scriptural activity culminates in the one radiant essence appearing in the sun.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Itihāsa-Purāṇa, too, is the Lord's word; its essence drawn out by meditation becomes nectar — the joy of those who serve Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva holds the Purāṇas to be a valid means of knowing Viṣṇu; their essence, like honey, is the bliss the Lord grants through right understanding.

verses.dev: ते वा एतेऽथर्वाङ्गिरस एतदितिहासपुराणमभ्यतपंस्तस्याभितप्तस्य यशस्तेज इन्द्रियं वीर्यमन्नाद्यं रसोऽजायत ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: te vā ete 'tharvāṅgirasa etad itihāsapurāṇam abhyatapaṃs tasyābhitaptasya yaśas teja indriyaṃ vīryam annādyaṃ raso 'jāyata || 2 ||
verses.trans: Those Atharva-Aṅgiras hymns brooded over the Itihāsa-Purāṇa; from it, thus brooded over, arose its essence — fame, splendour, vigour of the senses, virility, and food fit for eating.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The 'brooding' (tapas) is the ritual and meditative use of the texts, which releases their inner power as tangible excellence in the knower.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Through devout study the Lord causes the text's hidden glories to flow forth as blessings for the worshipper.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The fruits — fame, splendour, strength — are apportioned by Viṣṇu according to the devotion with which His word is studied.

verses.dev: तद्व्यक्षरत्तदादित्यमभितोऽश्रयत्तद्वा एतद्यदेतदादित्यस्य परं कृष्णं रूपम् ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tad vyakṣarat tad ādityam abhito 'śrayat tad vā etad yad etad ādityasya paraṃ kṛṣṇaṃ rūpam || 3 ||
verses.trans: That essence flowed forth and settled around the sun. That, verily, is what appears as the extremely dark form of the sun.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Each class of texts contributes a colour to the sun's appearance; the deep dark hue is the essence of the Atharva lore. The colours are superimposed forms, not the sun's true nature — pointing to the formless reality behind all appearances.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The hues of the solar orb are glories of the Lord's body, each fed by a stream of His own revealed word.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The colours show the graded manifestation of the Lord's energies in the solar deity, who is ever distinct from and dependent on Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: अथ येऽस्योर्ध्वा रश्मयस्ता एवास्योर्ध्वा मधुनाड्यो गुह्या एवादेशा मधुकृतो ब्रह्मैव पुष्पं ता अमृता आपः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha ye 'syordhvā raśmayas tā evāsyordhvā madhunāḍyo guhyā evādeśā madhukṛto brahmaiva puṣpaṃ tā amṛtā āpaḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, those rays of the sun that go upward are its upper honey-cells. The secret teachings (the Upaniṣads) are the bees; Brahman itself (the praṇava, OM) is the flower; the immortal waters are the nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Here the allegory reaches its summit: the hidden instructions — the Upaniṣads — draw their honey from Brahman itself, signified by OM. Śaṅkara notes that the highest essence comes not from ritual texts but from the knowledge portion of the Veda.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The 'secret commands' are the esoteric meditations on the Lord; their flower is the praṇava, His chief name. Their honey is the supreme bliss of union with Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Upaniṣads, rightly interpreted, reveal Viṣṇu as the sole independent reality; their essence is the highest of all nectars because their subject is the Highest.

verses.dev: ते वा एते गुह्या आदेशा एतद्ब्रह्माभ्यतपंस्तस्याभितप्तस्य यशस्तेज इन्द्रियं वीर्यमन्नाद्यं रसोऽजायत ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: te vā ete guhyā ādeśā etad brahmābhyatapaṃs tasyābhitaptasya yaśas teja indriyaṃ vīryam annādyaṃ raso 'jāyata || 2 ||
verses.trans: Those secret teachings brooded over Brahman (OM); from it, thus brooded over, arose its essence — fame, splendour, vigour of the senses, virility, and food fit for eating.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Meditation on OM as taught in the secret doctrine releases the supreme essence; the listed fruits are its visible portion, while its invisible portion is liberation itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Contemplation of the Lord through His praṇava-name yields both worldly excellence and the supreme end — His own abode.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: OM names Viṣṇu directly; meditation on it draws forth His grace, the source of every excellence.

verses.dev: तद्व्यक्षरत्तदादित्यमभितोऽश्रयत्तद्वा एतद्यदेतदादित्यस्य मध्ये क्षोभत इव ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tad vyakṣarat tad ādityam abhito 'śrayat tad vā etad yad etad ādityasya madhye kṣobhata iva || 3 ||
verses.trans: That essence flowed forth and settled around the sun. That, verily, is what appears to tremble, as it were, in the middle of the sun.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The shimmering at the sun's heart is the subtlest essence — that of the Upaniṣadic knowledge. The word 'iva' (as it were) warns that all such appearances are provisional; the reality is the unmoving Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The trembling radiance at the centre is the most intimate glory of the Lord's solar form, perceptible to advanced meditators.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The central radiance is the special presence of Viṣṇu within the solar orb, distinct from the deity Sūrya who serves Him.

verses.dev: ते वा एते रसानां रसा वेदा हि रसास्तेषामेते रसास्तानि वा एतान्यमृतानाममृतानि वेदा ह्यमृतास्तेषामेतान्यमृतानि ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: te vā ete rasānāṃ rasā vedā hi rasās teṣām ete rasās tāni vā etāny amṛtānām amṛtāni vedā hy amṛtās teṣām etāny amṛtāni || 4 ||
verses.trans: These (essences) are the essences of essences, for the Vedas are essences and these are their essences. These are the nectars of nectars, for the Vedas are nectars and these are their nectars.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The Vedas are the essence of the world; their own essence — the luminous forms in the sun — is therefore essence doubled. Śaṅkara sees here a ladder: world → Veda → essence → the One that the essences finally indicate.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord's word is the world's sweetness, and the fruit of meditating on it is sweetness redoubled — the foretaste of eternal service to Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The graded language ('essence of essences') confirms Madhva's hierarchy of realities, at whose summit stands Viṣṇu, the nectar of all nectars.

verses.dev: तद्यत्प्रथमममृतं तद्वसव उपजीवन्त्यग्निना मुखेन न वै देवा अश्नन्ति न पिबन्त्येतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tad yat prathamam amṛtaṃ tad vasava upajīvanty agninā mukhena na vai devā aśnanti na pibanty etad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: On that first nectar the Vasus live, with Agni as their mouth (chief). Truly, the gods neither eat nor drink; they are satisfied merely by seeing that nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains that the gods' 'seeing' is uninterrupted contemplation; their satisfaction without eating shows that fullness comes from knowledge, not consumption — a pointer toward the self-sufficiency of the knower of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The gods feast through vision because divine enjoyment is consciousness-borne; Agni as 'mouth' means he is their leader in enjoying the Lord's radiance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes the gods' superiority to humans: their nourishment is contemplative. Yet even they depend wholly on Viṣṇu, whose essence the nectar is.

verses.dev: त एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशन्त्येतस्माद्रूपादुद्यन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: ta etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśanty etasmād rūpād udyanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: They enter into that very form (the red form of the sun), and from that form they rise forth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The gods merge into and emerge from the solar colour they live upon — periodic rest and activity, mirroring the cosmic rhythm of dissolution and manifestation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Entering and rising from the form expresses the gods' intimate participation in the Lord's solar glory.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Even the gods' rest and rising are governed by the Lord within the sun; they are never independent.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवममृतं वेद वसूनामेवैको भूत्वाग्निनैव मुखेनैतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यति स एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशत्येतस्माद्रूपादुदेति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etad evam amṛtaṃ veda vasūnām evaiko bhūtvāgninaiva mukhenaitad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyati sa etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśaty etasmād rūpād udeti || 3 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this nectar thus becomes one of the Vasus themselves; with Agni as his mouth he is satisfied by seeing that nectar; he enters that very form and rises forth from that form.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Meditation conforms the meditator to its object: the knower attains the rank of the Vasus. For Śaṅkara such fruits, while glorious, remain within saṃsāra — stepping-stones toward the knowledge that liberates.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The worshipper, by the Lord's grace, is granted communion with the Vasus and shares their mode of blissful vision.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The qualified soul attains a station among the Vasus appropriate to its intrinsic grade — Madhva's doctrine of svarūpa-tāratamya (innate gradation).

verses.dev: स यावदादित्यः पुरस्तादुदेता पश्चादस्तमेता वसूनामेव तावदाधिपत्यं स्वाराज्यं पर्येता ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa yāvad ādityaḥ purastād udetā paścād astam etā vasūnām eva tāvad ādhipatyaṃ svārājyaṃ paryetā || 4 ||
verses.trans: As long as the sun rises in the east and sets in the west, so long will he enjoy the sovereignty and self-rule of the Vasus.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The fruit lasts a cosmic age — vast, yet bounded by time. Śaṅkara's implicit lesson: every meditational fruit short of Self-knowledge has a horizon.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: 'Svārājya' is rulership free of obstruction, conferred by the Lord for the duration of the solar order.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Sovereignty is real but derivative; the Vasus and their new companion rule only by Viṣṇu's delegation.

verses.dev: अथ यद्द्वितीयममृतं तद्रुद्रा उपजीवन्तीन्द्रेण मुखेन न वै देवा अश्नन्ति न पिबन्त्येतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad dvitīyam amṛtaṃ tad rudrā upajīvantīndreṇa mukhena na vai devā aśnanti na pibanty etad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, on that second nectar the Rudras live, with Indra as their mouth. Truly, the gods neither eat nor drink; they are satisfied merely by seeing that nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The second colour (the white form, essence of the Yajurveda) sustains the Rudras; the pattern of contemplative satisfaction repeats at each level of divine life.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Each class of gods enjoys its appointed stream of the Lord's glory, with its own leader as 'mouth'.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The ordered assignments — Rudras to the second nectar, Indra as chief — reflect the immutable hierarchy established by Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: त एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशन्त्येतस्माद्रूपादुद्यन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: ta etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśanty etasmād rūpād udyanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: They enter into that very form, and from that form they rise forth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: As with the Vasus, the Rudras' rest and emergence trace the rhythm of manifestation inherent in all conditioned existence.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Their entering and rising is ceaseless communion with the Lord's white radiance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Their movements are timed and willed by the indwelling Lord.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवममृतं वेद रुद्राणामेवैको भूत्वेन्द्रेणैव मुखेनैतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यति स एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशत्येतस्माद्रूपादुदेति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etad evam amṛtaṃ veda rudrāṇām evaiko bhūtvendreṇaiva mukhenaitad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyati sa etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśaty etasmād rūpād udeti || 3 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this nectar thus becomes one of the Rudras themselves; with Indra as his mouth he is satisfied by seeing that nectar; he enters that very form and rises forth from that form.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Knowledge assimilates the knower to the known: meditation on the second nectar wins Rudra-hood for a cosmic span.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord grants the meditator fellowship with the Rudras and participation in their vision.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Entry into the Rudra-host is according to the soul's fitness, by the Lord's appointment.

verses.dev: स यावदादित्यः पुरस्तादुदेता पश्चादस्तमेता द्विस्तावद्दक्षिणत उदेतोत्तरतोऽस्तमेता रुद्राणामेव तावदाधिपत्यं स्वाराज्यं पर्येता ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa yāvad ādityaḥ purastād udetā paścād astam etā dvis tāvad dakṣiṇata udetottarato 'stam etā rudrāṇām eva tāvad ādhipatyaṃ svārājyaṃ paryetā || 4 ||
verses.trans: Twice as long as the sun rises in the east and sets in the west will it rise in the south and set in the north; and just so long will he enjoy the sovereignty and self-rule of the Rudras.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The text envisions successive cosmic epochs in which the sun's course shifts; each higher meditation yields a duration double the last — yet all remain finite.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The doubling expresses ascending grades of divine enjoyment granted to deeper meditation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Longer sovereignty for higher hosts confirms gradation among the gods themselves.

verses.dev: अथ यत्तृतीयममृतं तदादित्या उपजीवन्ति वरुणेन मुखेन न वै देवा अश्नन्ति न पिबन्त्येतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha yat tṛtīyam amṛtaṃ tad ādityā upajīvanti varuṇena mukhena na vai devā aśnanti na pibanty etad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, on that third nectar the Ādityas live, with Varuṇa as their mouth. Truly, the gods neither eat nor drink; they are satisfied merely by seeing that nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The third (dark) form, essence of the Sāmaveda, sustains the Ādityas led by Varuṇa — the pattern ascends through subtler essences.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Ādityas drink the Lord's Sāma-glory through contemplative sight, Varuṇa first among them.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Each god receives according to rank; all receive from Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: त एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशन्त्येतस्माद्रूपादुद्यन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: ta etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśanty etasmād rūpād udyanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: They enter into that very form, and from that form they rise forth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The recurring refrain underscores the universality of the rhythm of absorption and emergence.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Continuous communion with the Lord's dark-radiant form is their life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Their repose and rising follow the Lord's ordinance.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवममृतं वेदादित्यानामेवैको भूत्वा वरुणेनैव मुखेनैतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यति स एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशत्येतस्माद्रूपादुदेति ॥ ३ ॥
