verses.iast: sa ya etad evam amṛtaṃ vedādityānām evaiko bhūtvā varuṇenaiva mukhenaitad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyati sa etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśaty etasmād rūpād udeti || 3 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this nectar thus becomes one of the Ādityas themselves; with Varuṇa as his mouth he is satisfied by seeing that nectar; he enters that very form and rises forth from that form.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The meditator gains Āditya-hood — a higher station, a longer span, but still within the wheel of time.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Fellowship with the Ādityas is the Lord's gift to this meditation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Attainment matches the soul's eternal grade, fixed by Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: स यावदादित्यो दक्षिणत उदेतोत्तरतोऽस्तमेता द्विस्तावत्पश्चादुदेता पुरस्तादस्तमेतादित्यानामेव तावदाधिपत्यं स्वाराज्यं पर्येता ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa yāvad ādityo dakṣiṇata udetottarato 'stam etā dvis tāvat paścād udetā purastād astam etādityānām eva tāvad ādhipatyaṃ svārājyaṃ paryetā || 4 ||
verses.trans: Twice as long as the sun rises in the south and sets in the north will it rise in the west and set in the east; and just so long will he enjoy the sovereignty and self-rule of the Ādityas.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The epochs lengthen as the meditations deepen — a doubling ladder of finite glories.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Higher meditations win longer enjoyment of the Lord's delegated rule.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Duration of sovereignty is precisely meted by the Lord.

verses.dev: अथ यच्चतुर्थममृतं तन्मरुत उपजीवन्ति सोमेन मुखेन न वै देवा अश्नन्ति न पिबन्त्येतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha yac caturtham amṛtaṃ tan maruta upajīvanti somena mukhena na vai devā aśnanti na pibanty etad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, on that fourth nectar the Maruts live, with Soma as their mouth. Truly, the gods neither eat nor drink; they are satisfied merely by seeing that nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The fourth (deep-dark) form, essence of the Atharva lore, sustains the storm-gods led by Soma.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Maruts taste the Lord's glory distilled from sacred narrative, with Soma at their head.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The assignment of hosts to nectars is the Lord's eternal arrangement.

verses.dev: त एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशन्त्येतस्माद्रूपादुद्यन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: ta etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśanty etasmād rūpād udyanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: They enter into that very form, and from that form they rise forth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Absorption and emergence: the universal pulse of manifested life.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Their dwelling is the Lord's dark-lustrous radiance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: All their states are Viṣṇu-directed.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवममृतं वेद मरुतामेवैको भूत्वा सोमेनैव मुखेनैतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यति स एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशत्येतस्माद्रूपादुदेति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etad evam amṛtaṃ veda marutām evaiko bhūtvā somenaiva mukhenaitad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyati sa etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśaty etasmād rūpād udeti || 3 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this nectar thus becomes one of the Maruts themselves; with Soma as his mouth he is satisfied by seeing that nectar; he enters that very form and rises forth from that form.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Marut-hood is the fruit; the ladder of finite attainments continues to lengthen.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord admits the meditator to the Maruts' company and joy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Station among the Maruts is granted per the soul's innate worth.

verses.dev: स यावदादित्यः पश्चादुदेता पुरस्तादस्तमेता द्विस्तावदुत्तरत उदेता दक्षिणतोऽस्तमेता मरुतामेव तावदाधिपत्यं स्वाराज्यं पर्येता ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa yāvad ādityaḥ paścād udetā purastād astam etā dvis tāvad uttarata udetā dakṣiṇato 'stam etā marutām eva tāvad ādhipatyaṃ svārājyaṃ paryetā || 4 ||
verses.trans: Twice as long as the sun rises in the west and sets in the east will it rise in the north and set in the south; and just so long will he enjoy the sovereignty and self-rule of the Maruts.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Each reversal of the sun's course marks a higher epoch; the doubling continues.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Longer spans of rule reward subtler contemplations.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Time itself is apportioned by the Lord to each attainment.

verses.dev: अथ यत्पञ्चमममृतं तत्साध्या उपजीवन्ति ब्रह्मणा मुखेन न वै देवा अश्नन्ति न पिबन्त्येतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha yat pañcamam amṛtaṃ tat sādhyā upajīvanti brahmaṇā mukhena na vai devā aśnanti na pibanty etad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Now, on that fifth nectar the Sādhyas live, with Brahmā as their mouth. Truly, the gods neither eat nor drink; they are satisfied merely by seeing that nectar.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The fifth, central trembling essence — that of the Upaniṣads — nourishes the subtlest gods, the Sādhyas, with Brahmā (the praṇava or Hiraṇyagarbha) as their chief.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Sādhyas live on the innermost glory of the Lord, revealed by the secret teachings.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Even the loftiest hosts subsist on Viṣṇu's essence; 'Brahmā as mouth' marks the highest created intelligence serving the Independent One.

verses.dev: त एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशन्त्येतस्माद्रूपादुद्यन्ति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: ta etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśanty etasmād rūpād udyanti || 2 ||
verses.trans: They enter into that very form, and from that form they rise forth.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Even at the subtlest level the rhythm holds — until knowledge ends all entering and rising.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Their life is unbroken intimacy with the Lord's central radiance.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Their states, too, depend on the Lord within the sun.

verses.dev: स य एतदेवममृतं वेद साध्यानामेवैको भूत्वा ब्रह्मणैव मुखेनैतदेवामृतं दृष्ट्वा तृप्यति स एतदेव रूपमभिसंविशत्येतस्माद्रूपादुदेति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etad evam amṛtaṃ veda sādhyānām evaiko bhūtvā brahmaṇaiva mukhenaitad evāmṛtaṃ dṛṣṭvā tṛpyati sa etad eva rūpam abhisaṃviśaty etasmād rūpād udeti || 3 ||
verses.trans: He who knows this nectar thus becomes one of the Sādhyas themselves; with Brahmā as his mouth he is satisfied by seeing that nectar; he enters that very form and rises forth from that form.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The highest meditational fruit in this series — Sādhya-hood — borders on the path that leads beyond all stations.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The meditator joins the innermost circle of the Lord's attendants in the cosmic order.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The soul's highest possible cosmic station is reached, yet eternal distinction from Viṣṇu remains.

verses.dev: स यावदादित्य उत्तरत उदेता दक्षिणतोऽस्तमेता द्विस्तावदूर्ध्वमुदेतार्वागस्तमेता साध्यानामेव तावदाधिपत्यं स्वाराज्यं पर्येता ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa yāvad āditya uttarata udetā dakṣiṇato 'stam etā dvis tāvad ūrdhvam udetārvāg astam etā sādhyānām eva tāvad ādhipatyaṃ svārājyaṃ paryetā || 4 ||
verses.trans: Twice as long as the sun rises in the north and sets in the south will it rise overhead and set below; and just so long will he enjoy the sovereignty and self-rule of the Sādhyas.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The final cosmic epoch: the sun standing overhead. Beyond this lies only the state described next — where rising and setting cease altogether.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The longest span of delegated sovereignty, prelude to the eternal state beyond all solar motion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The last and longest finite reward before the text turns to the eternal.

verses.dev: अथ तत ऊर्ध्व उदेत्य नैवोदेता नास्तमेतैकल एव मध्ये स्थाता तदेष श्लोकः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: atha tata ūrdhva udetya naivodetā nāstam etaikala eva madhye sthātā tad eṣa ślokaḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: Then, having risen upward from there, it will neither rise nor set; it will stand alone in the middle. On this there is this verse:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara reads this as the state of the knower: for him the sun of consciousness neither rises nor sets — the changeless Brahman, ever standing 'alone in the middle', beyond all temporal alternation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The motionless sun symbolises the eternal, unobstructed experience of the Lord in the supreme abode, where time's alternations cease.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The ever-standing sun is the Lord's eternal splendour; for the released soul there is unbroken vision of Viṣṇu, without the rising and setting of worldly states.

verses.dev: न वै तत्र न निम्लोच नोदियाय कदाचन । देवास्तेनाहं सत्येन मा विराधिषि ब्रह्मणेति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: na vai tatra na nimloca nodiyāya kadācana | devās tenāhaṃ satyena mā virādhiṣi brahmaṇeti || 2 ||
verses.trans: 'There, verily, it never set, nor did it ever rise. O gods, by this truth, may I not fall from Brahman.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The śloka is the knower's asseveration: in the Reality there never was rising or setting at all — time itself is appearance. By this truth he prays never to fall from Brahman, i.e., never to lapse from knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The verse is the meditator's solemn oath of fidelity, invoking the changeless realm of the Lord as witness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: An oath by the changeless world of Viṣṇu: may the devotee never be severed from Him.

verses.dev: न ह वा अस्मा उदेति न निम्लोचति सकृद्दिवा हैवास्मै भवति य एतामेवं ब्रह्मोपनिषदं वेद ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: na ha vā asmā udeti na nimlocati sakṛd divā haivāsmai bhavati ya etām evaṃ brahmopaniṣadaṃ veda || 3 ||
verses.trans: For him who knows this Brahma-Upaniṣad (secret doctrine of the Veda) thus, the sun neither rises nor sets; for him it is day once and for all.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Perpetual day is the unbroken light of Self-knowledge: once ignorance is dispelled, there is no more alternation of knowledge and its loss. This is Śaṅkara's celebrated reading — liberation is not an event in time but the cessation of time's claim.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: For the liberated, the experience of the Lord is uninterrupted — a single everlasting day of communion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Endless day means unbroken, blissful awareness of Viṣṇu in mokṣa, never again shadowed by saṃsāra.

verses.dev: तद्धैतद्ब्रह्मा प्रजापतय उवाच प्रजापतिर्मनवे मनुः प्रजाभ्यस्तद्धैतदुद्दालकायारुणये ज्येष्ठाय पुत्राय पिता ब्रह्म प्रोवाच ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: tad dhaitad brahmā prajāpataya uvāca prajāpatir manave manuḥ prajābhyas tad dhaitad uddālakāyāruṇaye jyeṣṭhāya putrāya pitā brahma provāca || 4 ||
verses.trans: This Brahmā told to Prajāpati, Prajāpati to Manu, Manu to his descendants. And this Brahman the father declared to his eldest son Uddālaka Āruṇi.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The lineage of transmission (sampradāya) guarantees the teaching's authority; knowledge of Brahman lives through an unbroken chain of teacher and disciple.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The sacred succession shows that this vidyā is to be received from a qualified ācārya, not invented.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva prizes the guru-paramparā: truth descends from the Lord through Brahmā to the worthy.

verses.dev: इदं वाव तज्ज्येष्ठाय पुत्राय पिता ब्रह्म प्रब्रूयात्प्रणाय्याय वान्तेवासिने ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: idaṃ vāva taj jyeṣṭhāya putrāya pitā brahma prabrūyāt praṇāyyāya vāntevāsine || 5 ||
verses.trans: This very Brahman, then, a father may declare to his eldest son or to a worthy resident disciple —

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The restriction protects the teaching from unfit recipients; readiness (adhikāra) is essential for knowledge to bear fruit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The vidyā is a family treasure of the spirit, to be entrusted to the devoted and disciplined.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Only the qualified may receive the highest doctrine — fitness is itself God-given.

verses.dev: नान्यस्मै कस्मैचन यद्यप्यस्मा इमामद्भिः परिगृहीतां धनस्य पूर्णां दद्यादेतदेव ततो भूय इत्येतदेव ततो भूय इति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: nānyasmai kasmaicana yady apy asmā imām adbhiḥ parigṛhītāṃ dhanasya pūrṇāṃ dadyād etad eva tato bhūya ity etad eva tato bhūya iti || 6 ||
verses.trans: — and to no one else, even if one should offer him this whole sea-girt earth filled with wealth; for this teaching is greater than that — yes, this is greater than that.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: No finite wealth can equal the knowledge that ends all want. The repetition seals the chapter: Brahman-knowledge is beyond price.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord's vidyā outweighs the earth itself; it is given for love and fitness, never sold.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The supreme worth of God-knowledge over all riches is affirmed twice for certainty.

verses.dev: गायत्री वा इदं सर्वं भूतं यदिदं किञ्च वाग्वै गायत्री वाग्वा इदं सर्वं भूतं गायति च त्रायते च ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: gāyatrī vā idaṃ sarvaṃ bhūtaṃ yad idaṃ kiñca vāg vai gāyatrī vāg vā idaṃ sarvaṃ bhūtaṃ gāyati ca trāyate ca || 1 ||
verses.trans: The Gāyatrī, verily, is all this, whatever being exists. Speech indeed is Gāyatrī, for speech sings (gāyati) and protects (trāyate) all this that exists.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara derives 'Gāyatrī' from gāyati (sings, names) and trāyate (protects): speech names all beings and thereby protects them. Since nothing is apart from being named, the Gāyatrī stands for the whole world — and ultimately for the Brahman it expresses.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Gāyatrī, the mother of the Vedas, is the Lord's own utterance; through it He sustains and saves all creatures. Meditating on it is meditating on Him as expressed in sacred speech.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva holds that the Gāyatrī signifies Viṣṇu, whose names sung in speech protect the singer. The identification of world and Gāyatrī means all depends on Him whom the metre names.

verses.dev: या वै सा गायत्रीयं वाव सा येयं पृथिव्यस्यां हीदं सर्वं भूतं प्रतिष्ठितमेतामेव नातिशीयते ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: yā vai sā gāyatrīyaṃ vāva sā yeyaṃ pṛthivy asyāṃ hīdaṃ sarvaṃ bhūtaṃ pratiṣṭhitam etām eva nātiśīyate || 2 ||
verses.trans: That which is the Gāyatrī is verily this earth, for on this earth all beings are established, and they do not go beyond it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The metre is equated with the earth as universal support — a meditative identification leading the mind from the gross support to the ultimate support, Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Earth supports beings as the Lord's body supports all; the Gāyatrī teaches His all-supporting nature.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The earth's supporting power is delegated by Viṣṇu, whom the Gāyatrī ultimately denotes.

verses.dev: या वै सा पृथिवीयं वाव सा यदिदमस्मिन्पुरुषे शरीरमस्मिन्हीमे प्राणाः प्रतिष्ठिता एतदेव नातिशीयन्ते ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: yā vai sā pṛthivīyaṃ vāva sā yad idam asmin puruṣe śarīram asmin hīme prāṇāḥ pratiṣṭhitā etad eva nātiśīyante || 3 ||
verses.trans: That which is this earth is verily this body in man, for in it these vital breaths are established, and they do not go beyond it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The contemplation moves inward: earth → body. The macrocosm is mapped onto the microcosm, preparing the discovery of the innermost ground.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The body, like the earth, is the Lord's instrument of support for the prāṇas that serve the soul.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The body's sustaining power, too, is Viṣṇu's gift, exercised through His presence.

verses.dev: यद्वै तत्पुरुषे शरीरमिदं वाव तद्यदिदमस्मिन्नन्तः पुरुषे हृदयमस्मिन्हीमे प्राणाः प्रतिष्ठिता एतदेव नातिशीयन्ते ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yad vai tat puruṣe śarīram idaṃ vāva tad yad idam asminn antaḥ puruṣe hṛdayam asmin hīme prāṇāḥ pratiṣṭhitā etad eva nātiśīyante || 4 ||
verses.trans: That which is the body in man is verily this heart within man, for in it these vital breaths are established, and they do not go beyond it.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Body → heart: the seat of life and of meditation. The series funnels attention to the cave of the heart where Brahman is to be sought.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The heart is the Lord's chosen throne within the person; the prāṇas wait on Him there.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The heart is the special seat of the indwelling Lord (hṛdaya-stha Hari), on whom the breaths depend.

verses.dev: सैषा चतुष्पदा षड्विधा गायत्री तदेतदृचाभ्यनूक्तम् ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: saiṣā catuṣpadā ṣaḍvidhā gāyatrī tad etad ṛcābhyanūktam || 5 ||
verses.trans: This Gāyatrī is four-footed and sixfold. This very thing is declared in a Ṛg verse:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Four feet: speech, beings, earth, body-heart series; sixfold: speech, beings, earth, body, heart, prāṇas. The structured metre mirrors the structured cosmos.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The fourfold-sixfold structure displays the ordered amplitude of the Lord's manifestation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The enumeration shows scripture's precision; each 'foot' rests on the Lord.

verses.dev: तावानस्य महिमा ततो ज्यायांश्च पूरुषः । पादोऽस्य सर्वा भूतानि त्रिपादस्यामृतं दिवीति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: tāvān asya mahimā tato jyāyāṃś ca pūruṣaḥ | pādo 'sya sarvā bhūtāni tripād asyāmṛtaṃ divīti || 6 ||
verses.trans: 'Such is His greatness; greater than that is the Puruṣa. One foot of Him is all beings; His three feet are the immortal in heaven.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Quoting the Puruṣa-sūkta: the entire universe is but a quarter of the Reality; three quarters remain transcendent and immortal. Brahman infinitely exceeds its manifestation — the heart of Advaita's teaching on the world as a fraction-like appearance.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja's school cherishes this verse: the cosmos is a mere quarter-manifestation of the Lord's glory, while His essential, immortal splendour transcends it — He is both immanent and supremely transcendent.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads it as declaring Viṣṇu's immeasurable transcendence: creation expresses a fragment of His power, while His fullness abides eternally beyond, untouched by change.

verses.dev: यद्वै तद्ब्रह्मेतीदं वाव तद्योयं बहिर्धा पुरुषादाकाशो यो वै स बहिर्धा पुरुषादाकाशः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: yad vai tad brahmetīdaṃ vāva tad yo 'yaṃ bahirdhā puruṣād ākāśo yo vai sa bahirdhā puruṣād ākāśaḥ || 7 ||
verses.trans: What is called Brahman — that is the same as this space outside a person. And that space which is outside a person —

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Space, the subtlest and most pervasive element, is taken as Brahman's symbol. The teaching will now equate outer, inner, and heart-space, dissolving the apparent walls between them.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The all-pervading space images the Lord's pervasion; outer space is His cosmic presence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Space is Brahman's emblem because Viṣṇu pervades all; yet the symbol is not the Lord Himself.

verses.dev: अयं वाव स योऽयमन्तः पुरुष आकाशो यो वै सोऽन्तः पुरुष आकाशः ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: ayaṃ vāva sa yo 'yam antaḥ puruṣa ākāśo yo vai so 'ntaḥ puruṣa ākāśaḥ || 8 ||
verses.trans: — that is the same as this space within a person. And that space which is within a person —

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Outer space and inner space are one; the apparent division by the body is no real division — a direct pointer to non-duality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord who fills the cosmos equally fills the person; His pervasion knows no outside or inside.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The same Lord present everywhere is present within — pervasion without identity of soul and God.

verses.dev: अयं वाव स योऽयमन्तर्हृदय आकाशस्तदेतत्पूर्णमप्रवर्ति पूर्णमप्रवर्तिनीं श्रियं लभते य एवं वेद ॥ ९ ॥
verses.iast: ayaṃ vāva sa yo 'yam antar hṛdaya ākāśas tad etat pūrṇam apravarti pūrṇam apravartinīṃ śriyaṃ labhate ya evaṃ veda || 9 ||
verses.trans: — that is the same as this space within the heart. That is the Full, the Non-moving. He who knows thus obtains full and unfailing prosperity.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The space in the heart is the Full (pūrṇa) and Unchanging (apravarti) — Brahman itself, immediately present within. He who knows this fullness lacks nothing; outer prosperity merely shadows the inner attainment.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Within the heart-space dwells the Lord, complete and immutable; knowing Him there, the devotee receives unfailing spiritual wealth — His grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The 'Full and Unmoving' is Viṣṇu enthroned in the heart; the knower receives from Him imperishable felicity.

verses.dev: तस्य ह वा एतस्य हृदयस्य पञ्च देवसुषयः स योऽस्य प्राङ्सुषिः स प्राणस्तच्चक्षुः स आदित्यस्तदेतत्तेजोऽन्नाद्यमित्युपासीत तेजस्व्यन्नादो भवति य एवं वेद ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tasya ha vā etasya hṛdayasya pañca devasuṣayaḥ sa yo 'sya prāṅ suṣiḥ sa prāṇas tac cakṣuḥ sa ādityas tad etat tejo 'nnādyam ity upāsīta tejasvy annādo bhavati ya evaṃ veda || 1 ||
verses.trans: Of this heart there are five openings for the gods. Its eastern opening is the prāṇa; that is the eye; that is the sun. One should meditate on it as splendour and as food. He who knows thus becomes splendid and an eater of food.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The heart's 'openings' are channels through which the one Life appears as breath, sense, and deity. Each triad (breath–organ–god) is a single power seen at three levels — preparation for seeing the one Self behind all powers.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The five doors are stations of the Lord's deputies guarding His inner court; meditating on each as His glory yields its fruit.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Each opening is presided over by a god under Viṣṇu's command; worship of the hierarchy as His servants brings splendour.

verses.dev: अथ योऽस्य दक्षिणः सुषिः स व्यानस्तच्छ्रोत्रं स चन्द्रमास्तदेतच्छ्रीश्च यशश्चेत्युपासीत श्रीमान्यशस्वी भवति य एवं वेद ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: atha yo 'sya dakṣiṇaḥ suṣiḥ sa vyānas tac chrotraṃ sa candramās tad etac chrīś ca yaśaś cety upāsīta śrīmān yaśasvī bhavati ya evaṃ veda || 2 ||
verses.trans: Its southern opening is the vyāna; that is the ear; that is the moon. One should meditate on it as prosperity and fame. He who knows thus becomes prosperous and famous.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Vyāna, pervading breath, links hearing and the moon; the meditation transforms physiological fact into contemplative symbol.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The moon-door dispenses the Lord's gifts of wealth and renown to the meditator.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Candra serves Viṣṇu at the southern door; his gifts flow by the Lord's leave.

verses.dev: अथ योऽस्य प्रत्यङ्सुषिः सोऽपानः सा वाक्सोऽग्निस्तदेतद्ब्रह्मवर्चसमन्नाद्यमित्युपासीत ब्रह्मवर्चस्यन्नादो भवति य एवं वेद ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha yo 'sya pratyaṅ suṣiḥ so 'pānaḥ sā vāk so 'gnis tad etad brahmavarcasam annādyam ity upāsīta brahmavarcasy annādo bhavati ya evaṃ veda || 3 ||
verses.trans: Its western opening is the apāna; that is speech; that is fire. One should meditate on it as the lustre of sacred knowledge and as food. He who knows thus becomes lustrous with sacred knowledge and an eater of food.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Apāna, speech, and fire form the western triad; brahmavarcasa — the radiance born of Vedic learning — is its meditative fruit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Agni at the western door bestows the glow of sacred study on the Lord's worshipper.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Fire, the Lord's priestly servant, grants Vedic lustre to those who honour the order.

verses.dev: अथ योऽस्योदङ्सुषिः स समानस्तन्मनः स पर्जन्यस्तदेतत्कीर्तिश्च व्युष्टिश्चेत्युपासीत कीर्तिमान्व्युष्टिमान्भवति य एवं वेद ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha yo 'syodaṅ suṣiḥ sa samānas tan manaḥ sa parjanyas tad etat kīrtiś ca vyuṣṭiś cety upāsīta kīrtimān vyuṣṭimān bhavati ya evaṃ veda || 4 ||
verses.trans: Its northern opening is the samāna; that is the mind; that is the rain-god Parjanya. One should meditate on it as renown and beauty. He who knows thus becomes renowned and beautiful.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Samāna, the equalising breath, pairs with mind and rain — both gatherers and distributors; renown and radiance are their fruit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Parjanya pours the Lord's favours of fame and grace through the northern door.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The rain-god, Viṣṇu's officer, rewards meditation with renown.

verses.dev: अथ योऽस्योर्ध्वः सुषिः स उदानः स वायुः स आकाशस्तदेतदोजश्च महश्चेत्युपासीतौजस्वी महस्वान्भवति य एवं वेद ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yo 'syordhvaḥ suṣiḥ sa udānaḥ sa vāyuḥ sa ākāśas tad etad ojaś ca mahaś cety upāsītaujasvī mahasvān bhavati ya evaṃ veda || 5 ||
verses.trans: Its upper opening is the udāna; that is the wind; that is space. One should meditate on it as strength and greatness. He who knows thus becomes strong and great.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Udāna, the ascending breath that carries the soul upward at death, pairs with wind and space; its fruits are vigour and greatness — and its direction is the path of ascent.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The upward door, kept by Vāyu, opens toward the Lord's realm; meditation on it strengthens the soul for the final journey.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Vāyu — in Madhva's school the highest of souls and Viṣṇu's foremost servant — keeps the upper door; his favour gives strength and carries the soul aloft.

verses.dev: ते वा एते पञ्च ब्रह्मपुरुषाः स्वर्गस्य लोकस्य द्वारपाः स य एतानेवं पञ्च ब्रह्मपुरुषान्स्वर्गस्य लोकस्य द्वारपान्वेदास्य कुले वीरो जायते प्रतिपद्यते स्वर्गं लोकं य एतानेवं पञ्च ब्रह्मपुरुषान्स्वर्गस्य लोकस्य द्वारपान्वेद ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: te vā ete pañca brahmapuruṣāḥ svargasya lokasya dvārapāḥ sa ya etān evaṃ pañca brahmapuruṣān svargasya lokasya dvārapān vedāsya kule vīro jāyate pratipadyate svargaṃ lokaṃ ya etān evaṃ pañca brahmapuruṣān svargasya lokasya dvārapān veda || 6 ||
verses.trans: These, verily, are the five persons of Brahman, the door-keepers of the heavenly world. He who knows these five persons of Brahman as the door-keepers of the heavenly world — in his family a hero is born, and he himself reaches the heavenly world.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The 'heavenly world' here is the Brahman within the heart, whose doors the five powers guard. Knowing the keepers, one passes through them to the Light they serve.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The five divine persons guard the way to the Lord's presence in the heart; the knower is admitted to His world.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The door-keepers admit only those who know their stations under the Lord; such a knower attains the world of light.

verses.dev: अथ यदतः परो दिवो ज्योतिर्दीप्यते विश्वतः पृष्ठेषु सर्वतः पृष्ठेष्वनुत्तमेषूत्तमेषु लोकेष्विदं वाव तद्यदिदमस्मिन्नन्तः पुरुषे ज्योतिः ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad ataḥ paro divo jyotir dīpyate viśvataḥ pṛṣṭheṣu sarvataḥ pṛṣṭheṣv anuttameṣūttameṣu lokeṣv idaṃ vāva tad yad idam asminn antaḥ puruṣe jyotiḥ || 7 ||
verses.trans: Now, that light which shines above this heaven, above all, in the highest worlds beyond which there are none higher — that is the same as this light which is here within the person.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara's key text: the supreme Light beyond heaven and the light within the person are one and the same. The transcendent Brahman is the immanent Self — the identity at the core of Advaita.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The supreme Light is the Lord in His transcendent splendour, and the very same Lord shines within the heart as the inner controller — identity of the indweller, not of soul and God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads: the Light supreme above and the Light within are the one Lord Viṣṇu, present in both places; the soul that perceives Him within sees the same God who reigns beyond.

verses.dev: तस्यैषा दृष्टिर्यत्रैतदस्मिञ्शरीरे संस्पर्शेनोष्णिमानं विजानाति तस्यैषा श्रुतिर्यत्रैतत्कर्णावपिगृह्य निनदमिव नदथुरिवाग्नेरिव ज्वलत उपशृणोति तदेतद्दृष्टं च श्रुतं चेत्युपासीत चक्षुष्यः श्रुतो भवति य एवं वेद य एवं वेद ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: tasyaiṣā dṛṣṭir yatraitad asmiñ śarīre saṃsparśenoṣṇimānaṃ vijānāti tasyaiṣā śrutir yatraitat karṇāv apigṛhya ninadam iva nadathur ivāgner iva jvalata upaśṛṇoti tad etad dṛṣṭaṃ ca śrutaṃ cety upāsīta cakṣuṣyaḥ śruto bhavati ya evaṃ veda ya evaṃ veda || 8 ||
verses.trans: Of this light, the seeing is this: when one perceives warmth in this body by touch. Its hearing is this: when, closing the ears, one hears something like a hum, like the rumble of a blazing fire. One should meditate on it as the seen and the heard. He who knows thus becomes one beautiful to see and widely heard of — he who knows thus.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The inner Light gives direct, bodily evidence: the warmth of life and the inner sound. Śaṅkara values these as proofs that the Self is not remote but immediately experienced; the repetition closes the vidyā.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord within graciously offers tangible tokens — warmth and inner resonance — so the devotee may fix the mind on His presence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Bodily warmth and the inner hum are signs of the indwelling Lord's activity, perceivable evidence of His presence for the attentive devotee.

verses.dev: सर्वं खल्विदं ब्रह्म तज्जलानिति शान्त उपासीत । अथ खलु क्रतुमयः पुरुषो यथाक्रतुरस्मिँल्लोके पुरुषो भवति तथेतः प्रेत्य भवति स क्रतुं कुर्वीत ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: sarvaṃ khalv idaṃ brahma tajjalān iti śānta upāsīta | atha khalu kratumayaḥ puruṣo yathākratur asmiṁl loke puruṣo bhavati tathetaḥ pretya bhavati sa kratuṃ kurvīta || 1 ||
verses.trans: All this, verily, is Brahman, for from It all arises (ja), into It all dissolves (la), and in It all breathes (an) — thus tranquil, one should meditate. Now, a person consists of will (kratu); as is a person's will in this world, so does he become on departing hence. Let him therefore form his will.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara's reading of 'tajjalān': the world is born from, dissolves into, and lives in Brahman — hence nothing exists apart from It. 'Sarvaṃ khalv idaṃ brahma' is for him a direct statement of non-duality: the universe has no being independent of Brahman, as pots have none apart from clay. Tranquillity (śānta) is the prerequisite, for only a stilled mind can entertain the identity. The doctrine of kratu teaches that meditation shapes destiny — until knowledge dawns and destiny itself is transcended.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja treats this text as central: all this is Brahman because all — souls and matter — constitutes His body, arising from, sustained by, and dissolving into Him. The identity is the identity of body and embodied, not the erasure of distinction. The injunction to 'form one's will' commands śaraṇāgati-infused meditation: as one resolves on the Lord here, so one attains Him hereafter.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets: all this is 'Brahman' in the sense that all depends utterly on Viṣṇu for origin, maintenance, and dissolution — not that all is identical with Him. The tranquil meditator contemplates the Lord's universal causality. The kratu doctrine affirms that the soul's fixed devotion to the Lord here determines its attainment hereafter.

verses.dev: मनोमयः प्राणशरीरो भारूपः सत्यसंकल्प आकाशात्मा सर्वकर्मा सर्वकामः सर्वगन्धः सर्वरसः सर्वमिदमभ्यात्तोऽवाक्यनादरः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: manomayaḥ prāṇaśarīro bhārūpaḥ satyasaṃkalpa ākāśātmā sarvakarmā sarvakāmaḥ sarvagandhaḥ sarvarasaḥ sarvam idam abhyātto 'vāky anādaraḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: He who consists of mind, whose body is the vital breath, whose form is light, whose resolve is truth, whose self is space; who contains all actions, all desires, all odours, all tastes; who embraces all this, who speaks not, who is without eagerness —

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The attributes describe Brahman as grasped through the mind in the heart: luminous, of unfailing will, subtle as space. 'Embracing all, yet silent and unconcerned' — Brahman pervades everything while remaining actionless witness. Śaṅkara takes these qualities as aids for meditation (upāsanā), provisional pointers to the attributeless Reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: These are the Lord's auspicious attributes (kalyāṇa-guṇas), each literally true: His resolve is never frustrated (satyasaṃkalpa), His form is luminous, He pervades like space, He contains all. Rāmānuja insists they are real qualities of the personal Brahman, the object of loving meditation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads each epithet as a real, infinite perfection of Viṣṇu — His true will, His luminous body of consciousness-bliss, His containment of all things as their inner ruler. The silence and non-eagerness signify His utter self-sufficiency.

verses.dev: एष म आत्मान्तर्हृदयेऽणीयान्व्रीहेर्वा यवाद्वा सर्षपाद्वा श्यामाकाद्वा श्यामाकतण्डुलाद्वैष म आत्मान्तर्हृदये ज्यायान्पृथिव्या ज्यायानन्तरिक्षाज्ज्यायान्दिवो ज्यायानेभ्यो लोकेभ्यः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: eṣa ma ātmāntar hṛdaye 'ṇīyān vrīher vā yavād vā sarṣapād vā śyāmākād vā śyāmākataṇḍulād vaiṣa ma ātmāntar hṛdaye jyāyān pṛthivyā jyāyān antarikṣāj jyāyān divo jyāyān ebhyo lokebhyaḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: This is my Self within the heart, smaller than a grain of rice, than a barley-corn, than a mustard seed, than a millet grain, than the kernel of a millet grain. This is my Self within the heart, greater than the earth, greater than the mid-region, greater than heaven, greater than all these worlds.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The paradox of smaller-than-small and greater-than-great shatters spatial thinking: the Self has no size, being the infinite ground of all sizes. 'My Self' (ma ātmā) is decisive for Śaṅkara — the Brahman meditated upon is not another, but one's own innermost Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord, subtle enough to dwell in the heart's core, is simultaneously vaster than all worlds — His omnipresence and accessibility together. 'My Self' means 'the Self of my self', the inner controller of the soul.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva: the indwelling Viṣṇu is atomic in His presence within the heart yet infinite in His cosmic majesty — both literally true of the Lord, who is distinct from the finite soul He inhabits.

verses.dev: सर्वकर्मा सर्वकामः सर्वगन्धः सर्वरसः सर्वमिदमभ्यात्तोऽवाक्यनादर एष म आत्मान्तर्हृदय एतद्ब्रह्मैतमितः प्रेत्याभिसंभवितास्मीति यस्य स्यादद्धा न विचिकित्सास्तीति ह स्माह शाण्डिल्यः शाण्डिल्यः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sarvakarmā sarvakāmaḥ sarvagandhaḥ sarvarasaḥ sarvam idam abhyātto 'vāky anādara eṣa ma ātmāntar hṛdaya etad brahmaitam itaḥ pretyābhisaṃbhavitāsmīti yasya syād addhā na vicikitsāstīti ha smāha śāṇḍilyaḥ śāṇḍilyaḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: Containing all actions, all desires, all odours, all tastes, embracing all this, speechless, without eagerness — this is my Self within the heart; this is Brahman. Into Him I shall enter on departing hence. He who has this faith has indeed no doubt. Thus spoke Śāṇḍilya — yes, Śāṇḍilya.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The vidyā closes with certainty: 'I shall become Him' — for Śaṅkara, the meditator's conviction ripens into the knowledge that he already is That. Freedom from doubt (na vicikitsā) is the mark of mature realisation. The sage's name is repeated to honour the teaching's authority.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The devotee's assurance — 'departing hence, I shall attain Him' — is the fruit of unwavering faith in the indwelling Lord; he reaches Brahman and shares His bliss without losing individuality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva underscores 'abhisaṃbhavitāsmi' as attainment, not merger: the soul reaches Viṣṇu's presence and abides with Him eternally. Firm faith free of doubt is itself the Lord's gift to the elect.

verses.dev: अन्तरिक्षोदरः कोशो भूमिबुध्नो न जीर्यति दिशो ह्यस्य स्रक्तयो द्यौरस्योत्तरं बिलं स एष कोशो वसुधानस्तस्मिन्विश्वमिदं श्रितम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: antarikṣodaraḥ kośo bhūmibudhno na jīryati diśo hy asya sraktayo dyaur asyottaraṃ bilaṃ sa eṣa kośo vasudhānas tasmin viśvam idaṃ śritam || 1 ||
verses.trans: There is a chest whose belly is the mid-region and whose bottom is the earth; it never decays. Its corners are the quarters of the sky, and its upper opening is heaven. This chest is the treasury; within it rests this whole universe.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The cosmos is imaged as one undecaying receptacle — a meditation that gathers the scattered world into a single whole, pointing toward the one substratum holding all.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The cosmic chest is the Lord's treasury, His body containing all wealth of beings; nothing exists outside His containment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The undecaying container reflects the Lord's sustaining power; the universe rests in Him though He is untouched by its changes.

verses.dev: तस्य प्राची दिग्जुहूर्नाम सहमाना नाम दक्षिणा राज्ञी नाम प्रतीची सुभूता नामोदीची तासां वायुर्वत्सः स य एतमेवं वायुं दिशां वत्सं वेद न पुत्ररोदं रोदिति सोऽहमेतमेवं वायुं दिशां वत्सं वेद मा पुत्ररोदं रुदम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tasya prācī dig juhūr nāma sahamānā nāma dakṣiṇā rājñī nāma pratīcī subhūtā nāmodīcī tāsāṃ vāyur vatsaḥ sa ya etam evaṃ vāyuṃ diśāṃ vatsaṃ veda na putrarodaṃ roditi so 'ham etam evaṃ vāyuṃ diśāṃ vatsaṃ veda mā putrarodaṃ rudam || 2 ||
verses.trans: Its eastern quarter is named Juhū (the offering ladle), the southern Sahamānā (the enduring), the western Rājñī (the queen), the northern Subhūtā (the prosperous). Their calf is the wind. He who knows the wind thus as the calf of the quarters does not weep for a son. 'I know the wind thus as the calf of the quarters — may I not weep for a son.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The vidyā is taught as a protective meditation for a father: knowing the wind — the cosmic life that never perishes — one is shielded from the grief of losing what lives by it.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The named quarters are the Lord's powers; the wind, His breath, is their child. The father entrusts his son to the Lord's all-sustaining life and is freed from fear.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Vāyu, dearest of the Lord's servants, is the life of the directions; invoking him under Viṣṇu's authority protects one's lineage.

verses.dev: अरिष्टं कोशं प्रपद्येऽमुनामुनामुना प्राणं प्रपद्येऽमुनामुनामुना भूः प्रपद्येऽमुनामुनामुना भुवः प्रपद्येऽमुनामुनामुना स्वः प्रपद्येऽमुनामुनामुना ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: ariṣṭaṃ kośaṃ prapadye 'munāmunāmunā prāṇaṃ prapadye 'munāmunāmunā bhūḥ prapadye 'munāmunāmunā bhuvaḥ prapadye 'munāmunāmunā svaḥ prapadye 'munāmunāmunā || 3 ||
verses.trans: 'I take refuge in the imperishable chest with this one, this one, this one (naming the son). I take refuge in the prāṇa with this one, this one, this one. I take refuge in Bhūḥ... in Bhuvaḥ... in Svaḥ with this one, this one, this one.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The thrice-repeated 'with this one' places the son under each cosmic shelter by name; refuge (prapatti) in the undecaying whole secures what is fragile.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: A model of śaraṇāgati: the father performs formal surrender of his child to the Lord's cosmic forms — chest, breath, and the three worlds.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Refuge is sought in the Lord through His manifestations; the repetition of the son's name commits him to divine protection.

verses.dev: स यदवोचं प्राणं प्रपद्य इति प्राणो वा इदं सर्वं भूतं यदिदं किञ्च तमेव तत्प्रापत्सि ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa yad avocaṃ prāṇaṃ prapadya iti prāṇo vā idaṃ sarvaṃ bhūtaṃ yad idaṃ kiñca tam eva tat prāpatsi || 4 ||
verses.trans: When I said 'I take refuge in the prāṇa', it is because the prāṇa is all this, whatever exists — so it was in That alone that I took refuge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Prāṇa here stands for the universal Life — Brahman as the breath of all; refuge in it is refuge in the All.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The universal prāṇa is the Lord as inner sustainer of every creature; surrender to Him is total shelter.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: All beings live by the Lord's breath; refuge in prāṇa is refuge in Viṣṇu through His chief instrument.

verses.dev: अथ यदवोचं भूः प्रपद्य इति पृथिवीं प्रपद्येऽन्तरिक्षं प्रपद्ये दिवं प्रपद्य इत्येव तदवोचम् ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad avocaṃ bhūḥ prapadya iti pṛthivīṃ prapadye 'ntarikṣaṃ prapadye divaṃ prapadya ity eva tad avocam || 5 ||
verses.trans: And when I said 'I take refuge in Bhūḥ', what I said was: I take refuge in the earth, I take refuge in the mid-region, I take refuge in heaven.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The vyāhṛti 'Bhūḥ' compresses the three worlds; the mantra's power lies in its condensed truth.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The sacred utterance unfolds into the Lord's threefold cosmic domain, all of it shelter.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Each vyāhṛti names a sphere of the Lord's governance in which protection is sought.

verses.dev: अथ यदवोचं भुवः प्रपद्य इत्यग्निं प्रपद्ये वायुं प्रपद्य आदित्यं प्रपद्य इत्येव तदवोचम् ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad avocaṃ bhuvaḥ prapadya ity agniṃ prapadye vāyuṃ prapadya ādityaṃ prapadya ity eva tad avocam || 6 ||
verses.trans: And when I said 'I take refuge in Bhuvaḥ', what I said was: I take refuge in fire, I take refuge in the wind, I take refuge in the sun.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The second utterance gathers the luminaries — the world's powers of light and life — as forms of the one refuge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Fire, wind, and sun are the Lord's radiant officers; refuge in them is refuge in their Master.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The gods named serve Viṣṇu; shelter sought through them rests finally in Him.

verses.dev: अथ यदवोचं स्वः प्रपद्य इत्यृग्वेदं प्रपद्ये यजुर्वेदं प्रपद्ये सामवेदं प्रपद्य इत्येव तदवोचं तदवोचम् ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad avocaṃ svaḥ prapadya ity ṛgvedaṃ prapadye yajurvedaṃ prapadye sāmavedaṃ prapadya ity eva tad avocaṃ tad avocam || 7 ||
verses.trans: And when I said 'I take refuge in Svaḥ', what I said was: I take refuge in the Ṛgveda, I take refuge in the Yajurveda, I take refuge in the Sāmaveda. That is what I said — that is what I said.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The final refuge is the Veda itself — the breath of the Real in the form of knowledge. The repetition closes the protective vidyā.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The three Vedas are the Lord's word; to shelter in them is to rest in His revealed grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The eternal Vedas, whose import is Viṣṇu, are the surest refuge; the doubling affirms the rite's completion.

verses.dev: पुरुषो वाव यज्ञस्तस्य यानि चतुर्विंशति वर्षाणि तत्प्रातःसवनं चतुर्विंशत्यक्षरा गायत्री गायत्रं प्रातःसवनं तदस्य वसवोऽन्वायत्ताः प्राणा वाव वसव एते हीदं सर्वं वासयन्ति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: puruṣo vāva yajñas tasya yāni caturviṃśati varṣāṇi tat prātaḥsavanaṃ caturviṃśatyakṣarā gāyatrī gāyatraṃ prātaḥsavanaṃ tad asya vasavo 'nvāyattāḥ prāṇā vāva vasava ete hīdaṃ sarvaṃ vāsayanti || 1 ||
verses.trans: A person, verily, is a sacrifice. His first twenty-four years are the morning pressing, for the Gāyatrī has twenty-four syllables and the morning pressing is performed with Gāyatrī. With this the Vasus are connected; the vital breaths are indeed the Vasus, for they make all this dwell (vāsayanti).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains this vidyā as the interiorisation of ritual: one who cannot perform outer sacrifices may see his very lifespan as the sacrifice. The etymology — prāṇas as Vasus because they 'house' all — turns physiology into liturgy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Life itself, offered in awareness, becomes worship of the Lord; the breaths that sustain the body are His Vasus, ministering within.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The body's span is the Lord's appointed rite; the prāṇas serve as gods under Viṣṇu, sustaining the sacrifice of living.

verses.dev: तं चेदेतस्मिन्वयसि किञ्चिदुपतपेत्स ब्रूयात्प्राणा वसव इदं मे प्रातःसवनं माध्यंदिनं सवनमनुसंतनुतेति माहं प्राणानां वसूनां मध्ये यज्ञो विलोप्सीयेत्युद्धैव तत एत्यगदो ह भवति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ ced etasmin vayasi kiñcid upatapet sa brūyāt prāṇā vasava idaṃ me prātaḥsavanaṃ mādhyaṃdinaṃ savanam anusaṃtanuteti māhaṃ prāṇānāṃ vasūnāṃ madhye yajño vilopsīyety uddhaiva tata ety agado ha bhavati || 2 ||
verses.trans: If any sickness afflict him in that period of life, let him say: 'O breaths, O Vasus, extend this my morning pressing into the midday pressing; let me, the sacrifice, not be cut off in the midst of the breaths, the Vasus.' Then he rises from it and becomes free of illness.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The prayer applies sacrificial continuity to life: as a rite must not be interrupted, neither should the life-sacrifice. The conviction itself, says Śaṅkara, has healing power for the meditator.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The sick man petitions the Lord's ministers to preserve the offering of his life — humble dependence that draws divine protection.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Health is restored by the gods at the Lord's will when the sacrifice of life is reverently commended to them.

verses.dev: अथ यानि चतुश्चत्वारिंशद्वर्षाणि तन्माध्यंदिनं सवनं चतुश्चत्वारिंशदक्षरा त्रिष्टुप्त्रैष्टुभं माध्यंदिनं सवनं तदस्य रुद्रा अन्वायत्ताः प्राणा वाव रुद्रा एते हीदं सर्वं रोदयन्ति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha yāni catuścatvāriṃśad varṣāṇi tan mādhyaṃdinaṃ savanaṃ catuścatvāriṃśadakṣarā triṣṭup traiṣṭubhaṃ mādhyaṃdinaṃ savanaṃ tad asya rudrā anvāyattāḥ prāṇā vāva rudrā ete hīdaṃ sarvaṃ rodayanti || 3 ||
verses.trans: The next forty-four years are the midday pressing, for the Triṣṭubh has forty-four syllables and the midday pressing is performed with Triṣṭubh. With this the Rudras are connected; the vital breaths are indeed the Rudras, for they make all this weep (rodayanti).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Middle age, with its burdens, belongs to the Rudras — the breaths as causes of weeping when they depart or afflict. The etymology again welds body to cosmos.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Rudra-breaths chasten as the Lord's discipline; even life's severities are within His sacrificial order.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Rudras' power to cause weeping is exercised under Viṣṇu's justice; the wise see order, not chance, in affliction.

verses.dev: तं चेदेतस्मिन्वयसि किञ्चिदुपतपेत्स ब्रूयात्प्राणा रुद्रा इदं मे माध्यंदिनं सवनं तृतीयसवनमनुसंतनुतेति माहं प्राणानां रुद्राणां मध्ये यज्ञो विलोप्सीयेत्युद्धैव तत एत्यगदो ह भवति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ ced etasmin vayasi kiñcid upatapet sa brūyāt prāṇā rudrā idaṃ me mādhyaṃdinaṃ savanaṃ tṛtīyasavanam anusaṃtanuteti māhaṃ prāṇānāṃ rudrāṇāṃ madhye yajño vilopsīyety uddhaiva tata ety agado ha bhavati || 4 ||
verses.trans: If any sickness afflict him in that period of life, let him say: 'O breaths, O Rudras, extend this my midday pressing into the third pressing; let me, the sacrifice, not be cut off in the midst of the breaths, the Rudras.' Then he rises from it and becomes free of illness.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The same healing resolve, now addressed to the Rudra-breaths: continuity of the life-rite is the theme.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Petition to the Lord's stern ministers turns severity into protection.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The gods extend life when the Lord, hearing the dedicated soul, so ordains.

verses.dev: अथ यान्यष्टाचत्वारिंशद्वर्षाणि तत्तृतीयसवनमष्टाचत्वारिंशदक्षरा जगती जागतं तृतीयसवनं तदस्यादित्या अन्वायत्ताः प्राणा वावादित्या एते हीदं सर्वमाददते ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yāny aṣṭācatvāriṃśad varṣāṇi tat tṛtīyasavanam aṣṭācatvāriṃśadakṣarā jagatī jāgataṃ tṛtīyasavanaṃ tad asyādityā anvāyattāḥ prāṇā vāvādityā ete hīdaṃ sarvam ādadate || 5 ||
verses.trans: The next forty-eight years are the third pressing, for the Jagatī has forty-eight syllables and the third pressing is performed with Jagatī. With this the Ādityas are connected; the vital breaths are indeed the Ādityas, for they take all this to themselves (ādadate).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Old age belongs to the Ādityas — the breaths as gatherers, drawing in the powers they once spread out. The life-sacrifice totals 116 years across its three pressings.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: In life's evening the Lord's Āditya-ministers gather the soul's faculties homeward.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Ādityas reclaim what was lent; all faculties return to the Lord's keeping.

verses.dev: तं चेदेतस्मिन्वयसि किञ्चिदुपतपेत्स ब्रूयात्प्राणा अदित्या इदं मे तृतीयसवनमायुरनुसंतनुतेति माहं प्राणानामादित्यानां मध्ये यज्ञो विलोप्सीयेत्युद्धैव तत एत्यगदो हैव भवति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ ced etasmin vayasi kiñcid upatapet sa brūyāt prāṇā adityā idaṃ me tṛtīyasavanam āyur anusaṃtanuteti māhaṃ prāṇānām ādityānāṃ madhye yajño vilopsīyety uddhaiva tata ety agado haiva bhavati || 6 ||
verses.trans: If any sickness afflict him in that period of life, let him say: 'O breaths, O Ādityas, extend this my third pressing to a full lifespan; let me, the sacrifice, not be cut off in the midst of the breaths, the Ādityas.' Then he rises from it and becomes free of illness indeed.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Even in the last pressing the rite may be prolonged to its full measure; the meditation claims the whole span ordained for the sacrifice.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The aged devotee asks the Lord's gatherers for the rite's completion, and grace grants health.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Length of days is the Lord's to give; the prayerful sacrifice receives its full term.

verses.dev: एतद्ध स्म वै तद्विद्वानाह महिदास ऐतरेयः स किं म एतदुपतपसि योऽहमनेन न प्रेष्यामीति स ह षोडशं वर्षशतमजीवत्प्र ह षोडशं वर्षशतं जीवति य एवं वेद ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: etad dha sma vai tad vidvān āha mahidāsa aitareyaḥ sa kiṃ ma etad upatapasi yo 'ham anena na preṣyāmīti sa ha ṣoḍaśaṃ varṣaśatam ajīvat pra ha ṣoḍaśaṃ varṣaśataṃ jīvati ya evaṃ veda || 7 ||
verses.trans: Knowing this, Mahidāsa Aitareya said (to his disease): 'Why do you afflict me thus, since I shall not die by this?' He lived a hundred and sixteen years. He who knows thus also lives on to a hundred and sixteen years.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The sage's fearless address to disease shows knowledge as mastery: he who knows life's sacrificial measure cannot be cut down before its completion.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Mahidāsa's confidence rests on the Lord who guards the sacrifice committed to Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The knower's longevity is the Lord's reward for completing the rite of life in devotion.

verses.dev: स यदशिशिषति यत्पिपासति यन्न रमते ता अस्य दीक्षाः ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: sa yad aśiśiṣati yat pipāsati yan na ramate tā asya dīkṣāḥ || 1 ||
verses.trans: When a man hungers, thirsts, and abstains from pleasure — these are his initiatory rites (dīkṣā).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Life's austerities are themselves the sacrificial consecration: hunger, thirst, and restraint purify as dīkṣā does. The whole of experience is reread as ritual.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Privations borne in remembrance of the Lord become consecration; nothing in the devotee's life falls outside worship.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Sufferings accepted as the Lord's discipline initiate the soul into the sacrifice of living.

verses.dev: अथ यदश्नाति यत्पिबति यद्रमते तदुपसदैरेति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad aśnāti yat pibati yad ramate tad upasadair eti || 2 ||
verses.trans: And when he eats, drinks, and enjoys pleasure — then he keeps the upasad rites (days of milk-fasting and worship).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Even enjoyment, taken in measure, parallels the gentle upasad observances — the rite embraces both privation and delight.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Lawful enjoyment received as the Lord's prasāda is itself a mode of His service.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Pleasures used within dharma are part of the sacrifice, not its violation.

verses.dev: अथ यद्धसति यज्जक्षति यन्मैथुनं चरति स्तुतशस्त्रैरेव तदेति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha yad dhasati yaj jakṣati yan maithunaṃ carati stutaśastrair eva tad eti || 3 ||
verses.trans: And when he laughs, feasts, and unites with his wife — then he joins in the chants and recitations (stuta and śastra).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The sacrifice's joyous hymnody corresponds to life's gladness; nothing human is excluded from the inner rite.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Household joys, hallowed by remembrance, are the music of the life-sacrifice.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Even mirth has its ritual analogue when life is lived under the Lord's ordinance.

verses.dev: अथ यत्तपो दानमार्जवमहिंसा सत्यवचनमिति ता अस्य दक्षिणाः ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: atha yat tapo dānam ārjavam ahiṃsā satyavacanam iti tā asya dakṣiṇāḥ || 4 ||
verses.trans: Austerity, charity, uprightness, non-violence, and truthfulness — these are his gifts to the priests (dakṣiṇā).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The five virtues are the fees of the life-sacrifice — what the sacrificer must actually give. Śaṅkara notes that ethical substance replaces external payment in this inner liturgy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Virtues offered to the Lord are the true dakṣiṇā; conduct is the currency of devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva stresses these five as obligatory offerings whose acceptance lies with the Lord alone.

verses.dev: तस्मादाहुः सोष्यत्यसोष्टेति पुनरुत्पादनमेवास्य तन्मरणमेवावभृथः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tasmād āhuḥ soṣyaty asoṣṭeti punar utpādanam evāsya tan maraṇam evāvabhṛthaḥ || 5 ||
verses.trans: Therefore they say 'he will press' and 'he has pressed' (of the soma) — that is his rebirth (in the rite); and death itself is the final purificatory bath (avabhṛtha).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Birth is the rite's commencement, death its closing ablution — life rounded as one complete sacrifice. For the knower, death is not terror but the rite's consummation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Death as avabhṛtha washes the soul for entry into the Lord's presence; the life-offering ends in purification, not loss.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The final bath signifies the soul's cleansing passage to its Lord-appointed destiny.

verses.dev: तद्धैतद्घोर आङ्गिरसः कृष्णाय देवकीपुत्रायोक्त्वोवाचापिपास एव स बभूव सोऽन्तवेलायामेतत्त्रयं प्रतिपद्येताक्षितमस्यच्युतमसि प्राणसंशितमसीति तत्रैतौ द्वौ श्लोकौ भवतः ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: tad dhaitad ghora āṅgirasaḥ kṛṣṇāya devakīputrāyoktvovācāpipāsa eva sa babhūva so 'ntavelāyām etat trayaṃ pratipadyetākṣitam asy acyutam asi prāṇasaṃśitam asīti tatraitau dvau ślokau bhavataḥ || 6 ||
verses.trans: Ghora Āṅgirasa, having taught this to Kṛṣṇa the son of Devakī, said — and he became free of thirst (for anything further) — 'At the final hour one should take refuge in these three: You are the Indestructible, You are the Unfallen, You are the very essence of life.' On this there are these two verses:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes the dying meditation: three affirmations of the imperishable Reality. The pupil 'thirsted no more' — the teaching extinguished all further craving. The mention of Kṛṣṇa Devakīputra has been treasured by tradition as linking this vidyā to the Lord of the Gītā.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The threefold refuge — Imperishable, Unfallen (Acyuta), Life of life — names the Lord Himself; the school delights that Kṛṣṇa, Devakī's son, here receives what He would later teach as the Gītā's eternal message.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva's tradition reads the three names as Viṣṇu's own: Akṣita, Acyuta, Prāṇasaṃśita. The final-hour remembrance of these names is the seed of the Gītā's teaching on remembering the Lord at death.

verses.dev: आदित्प्रत्नस्य रेतसः । उद्वयं तमसस्परि ज्योतिः पश्यन्त उत्तरं स्वः पश्यन्त उत्तरं देवं देवत्रा सूर्यमगन्म ज्योतिरुत्तममिति ज्योतिरुत्तममिति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: ād it pratnasya retasaḥ | ud vayaṃ tamasas pari jyotiḥ paśyanta uttaraṃ svaḥ paśyanta uttaraṃ devaṃ devatrā sūryam aganma jyotir uttamam iti jyotir uttamam iti || 7 ||
verses.trans: '(They behold) the light of the ancient seed. Beholding the higher light beyond darkness, beholding the higher heaven, we have reached the Sun, god among gods, the highest light — yes, the highest light.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The two Ṛg citations celebrate the passage from darkness to the supreme Light — for Śaṅkara, from ignorance to the Self's self-luminosity. The repetition seals the vidyā of life-as-sacrifice.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The 'highest light' is the supreme Person beyond the darkness of matter; the verse sings the soul's arrival at His radiant abode.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Reaching 'the god among gods, the highest light' is attaining Viṣṇu, supreme over all deities — the goal of the dying devotee's remembrance.

verses.dev: मनो ब्रह्मेत्युपासीतेत्यध्यात्ममथाधिदैवतमाकाशो ब्रह्मेत्युभयमादिष्टं भवत्यध्यात्मं चाधिदैवतं च ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: mano brahmety upāsītety adhyātmam athādhidaivatam ākāśo brahmety ubhayam ādiṣṭaṃ bhavaty adhyātmaṃ cādhidaivataṃ ca || 1 ||
verses.trans: One should meditate on the mind as Brahman — this with reference to the self. And with reference to the gods: space is Brahman. Thus both are taught — that relating to the self and that relating to the gods.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Mind within and space without are the subtlest representatives of Brahman on either plane; meditating on both trains the seeker to find the One in the inner and the outer alike.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Mind and space are the Lord's most pervasive instruments; honouring Him in both, the worshipper learns His double presence — within the soul and throughout the cosmos.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Mind and space symbolise Viṣṇu's inner and outer rule; the symbols are exalted, but the Lord remains distinct from and master of both.

verses.dev: तदेतच्चतुष्पाद्ब्रह्म वाक्पादः प्राणः पादश्चक्षुः पादः श्रोत्रं पाद इत्यध्यात्ममथाधिदैवतमग्निः पादो वायुः पाद आदित्यः पादो दिशः पाद इत्युभयमेवादिष्टं भवत्यध्यात्मं चैवाधिदैवतं च ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tad etac catuṣpād brahma vāk pādaḥ prāṇaḥ pādaś cakṣuḥ pādaḥ śrotraṃ pāda ity adhyātmam athādhidaivatam agniḥ pādo vāyuḥ pāda ādityaḥ pādo diśaḥ pāda ity ubhayam evādiṣṭaṃ bhavaty adhyātmaṃ caivādhidaivataṃ ca || 2 ||
verses.trans: This Brahman has four feet: speech is one foot, breath is one foot, the eye is one foot, the ear is one foot — this with reference to the self. With reference to the gods: fire is one foot, wind is one foot, the sun is one foot, the quarters are one foot. Thus both are taught — that relating to the self and that relating to the gods.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The mind-Brahman walks on four feet — the faculties within, the deities without; each inner power has its cosmic counterpart, all limbs of the one Reality.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The four faculties and four deities are the Lord's coordinated servants, His 'feet' by which He moves in the person and the world.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The pairing of organs and gods shows the Lord's administration: each sense is ruled by its presiding deity, each deity by Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: वागेव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः सोऽग्निना ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: vāg eva brahmaṇaś caturthaḥ pādaḥ so 'gninā jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda || 3 ||
verses.trans: Speech is one of the four feet of Brahman. With the light of fire it shines and warms. He who knows thus shines and warms with renown, with fame, and with the lustre of sacred knowledge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Speech kindled by fire images the word kindled by insight; the knower radiates what he contemplates.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Agni empowers the devotee's speech; meditation returns as eloquence and holy lustre.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Fire's gift of shining speech comes through the Lord's appointment.

verses.dev: प्राण एव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः स वायुना ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: prāṇa eva brahmaṇaś caturthaḥ pādaḥ sa vāyunā jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda || 4 ||
verses.trans: Breath is one of the four feet of Brahman. With the light of the wind it shines and warms. He who knows thus shines and warms with renown, with fame, and with the lustre of sacred knowledge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Breath and wind, one life within and without — the meditation knits the person into the cosmos.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Vāyu invigorates the worshipper's life-breath with divine vitality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Vāyu, the Lord's prime minister, lends his light to the knower's breath.

verses.dev: चक्षुरेव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः स आदित्येन ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: cakṣur eva brahmaṇaś caturthaḥ pādaḥ sa ādityena jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda || 5 ||
verses.trans: The eye is one of the four feet of Brahman. With the light of the sun it shines and warms. He who knows thus shines and warms with renown, with fame, and with the lustre of sacred knowledge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Sight and sun: the seer within and the illuminator without are functions of one light.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The sun lends the eye its power as the Lord's gift of vision.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Sūrya serves the Lord by lighting the creature's eye.

verses.dev: श्रोत्रमेव ब्रह्मणश्चतुर्थः पादः स दिग्भिर्ज्योतिषा भाति च तपति च भाति च तपति च कीर्त्या यशसा ब्रह्मवर्चसेन य एवं वेद ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: śrotram eva brahmaṇaś caturthaḥ pādaḥ sa digbhir jyotiṣā bhāti ca tapati ca bhāti ca tapati ca kīrtyā yaśasā brahmavarcasena ya evaṃ veda || 6 ||
verses.trans: The ear is one of the four feet of Brahman. With the light of the quarters it shines and warms. He who knows thus shines and warms with renown, with fame, and with the lustre of sacred knowledge.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Hearing and the encompassing quarters complete the four feet; the meditation ends with the knower radiant on every side.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The quarters carry sound to the devotee's ear by the Lord's pervading order.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The directional deities serve hearing under Viṣṇu's rule; the fourfold meditation honours His whole administration.

verses.dev: आदित्यो ब्रह्मेत्यादेशस्तस्योपव्याख्यानमसदेवेदमग्र आसीत् । तत्सदासीत्तत्समभवत्तदाण्डं निरवर्तत तत्संवत्सरस्य मात्रामशयत तन्निरभिद्यत ते आण्डकपाले रजतं च सुवर्णं चाभवताम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: ādityo brahmety ādeśas tasyopavyākhyānam asad evedam agra āsīt | tat sad āsīt tat samabhavat tad āṇḍaṃ niravartata tat saṃvatsarasya mātrām aśayata tan nirabhidyata te āṇḍakapāle rajataṃ ca suvarṇaṃ cābhavatām || 1 ||
verses.trans: 'The sun is Brahman' — this is the teaching. Its explanation: in the beginning this was non-existent (unmanifest). It became existent; it grew; it turned into an egg. It lay for the period of a year; then it split open. The two halves of the eggshell became silver and gold.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara is careful: 'asat' means not absolute non-being but the unmanifest state of name-and-form; from the One the cosmic egg evolves. The sun, first-born of the egg, is honoured as Brahman's most visible symbol.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The 'non-existent' is Brahman with souls and matter in their subtle, undifferentiated state; creation is the Lord's body unfolding from subtlety into the cosmic egg.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva denies any literal non-being: the world's seed-state pre-exists under Viṣṇu's will, who alone causes the egg's formation and bursting.

verses.dev: तद्यद्रजतं सेयं पृथिवी यत्सुवर्णं सा द्यौर्यज्जरायु ते पर्वता यदुल्बं समेघो नीहारो या धमनयस्ता नद्यो यद्वास्तेयमुदकं स समुद्रः ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: tad yad rajataṃ seyaṃ pṛthivī yat suvarṇaṃ sā dyaur yaj jarāyu te parvatā yad ulbaṃ sameghо nīhāro yā dhamanayas tā nadyo yad vāsteyam udakaṃ sa samudraḥ || 2 ||
verses.trans: What was silver is this earth; what was gold is the sky; what was the outer membrane is the mountains; what was the inner membrane is cloud and mist; what were the veins are the rivers; what was the fluid within is the ocean.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The egg's anatomy becomes the world's geography — a vision of the cosmos as one living birth, preparing the mind to see unity beneath multiplicity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Every feature of the world is a transformed limb of the Lord's primal manifestation; nothing is alien to His body.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The detailed correspondence exhibits the Lord's artistry; each part is His deliberate fashioning, not chance.

verses.dev: अथ यत्तदजायत सोऽसावादित्यस्तं जायमानं घोषा उलूलवोऽनूदतिष्ठन्त्सर्वाणि च भूतानि सर्वे च कामास्तस्मात्तस्योदयं प्रति प्रत्यायनं प्रति घोषा उलूलवोऽनूत्तिष्ठन्ति सर्वाणि च भूतानि सर्वे च कामाः ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: atha yat tad ajāyata so 'sāv ādityas taṃ jāyamānaṃ ghoṣā ulūlavo 'nūdatiṣṭhant sarvāṇi ca bhūtāni sarve ca kāmās tasmāt tasyodayaṃ prati pratyāyanaṃ prati ghoṣā ulūlavo 'nūttiṣṭhanti sarvāṇi ca bhūtāni sarve ca kāmāḥ || 3 ||
verses.trans: And what was born of it is yonder sun. As it was being born, shouts of jubilation rose up after it, and all beings and all desires. Therefore at its rising and at its every return, shouts of jubilation rise up, and all beings and all desires.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The sun's birth is greeted by the world's joy — for light is life and the visible face of the Real; every dawn repeats the first creation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Creation rejoices at the Lord's luminous emergence; daily sunrise renews the world's thanksgiving to Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The jubilation honours the Lord present in the sun; beings and their fulfilments depend on his rising.

verses.dev: स य एतमेवं विद्वानादित्यं ब्रह्मेत्युपास्तेऽभ्याशो ह यदेनं साधवो घोषा आ च गच्छेयुरुप च निम्रेडेरन्निम्रेडेरन् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya etam evaṃ vidvān ādityaṃ brahmety upāste 'bhyāśo ha yad enaṃ sādhavo ghoṣā ā ca gaccheyur upa ca nimreḍerann nimreḍeran || 4 ||
verses.trans: He who, knowing thus, meditates on the sun as Brahman — pleasant sounds will quickly come to him and delight him — yes, delight him.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The fruit fits the meditation: he who reveres the world-gladdening light is himself surrounded by gladness. The repetition closes the chapter's final vidyā.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord in the sun rewards His meditator with joy on every side.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Viṣṇu, worshipped through His solar presence, grants the devotee a life attended by auspicious delight.

verses.dev: जानश्रुतिर्ह पौत्रायणः श्रद्धादेयो बहुदायी बहुपाक्य आस स ह सर्वत आवसथान्मापयांचक्रे सर्वत एव मेऽन्नमत्स्यन्तीति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: jānaśrutir ha pautrāyaṇaḥ śraddhādeyo bahudāyī bahupākya āsa sa ha sarvata āvasathān māpayāṃ cakre sarvata eva me 'nnam atsyantīti || 1 ||
verses.trans: Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa was a giver with faith, a giver of much, a keeper of many kitchens. He had rest-houses built everywhere, thinking: 'Everywhere people will eat my food.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara presents the generous king whose charity, though noble, still carries the subtle pride of 'my food'. The story will humble him toward knowledge, which charity alone cannot buy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The king's open-handedness is genuine dharma, the seed-bed of grace; yet the narrative shows that the Lord leads even the virtuous further — from giving to knowing.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that meritorious works prepare but do not replace knowledge of the Lord; Jānaśruti's puṇya earns him the encounter that will instruct him.

verses.dev: अथ हंसा निशायामतिपेतुस्तद्धैवं हंसो हंसमभ्युवाद हो होऽयि भल्लाक्ष भल्लाक्ष जानश्रुतेः पौत्रायणस्य समं दिवा ज्योतिराततं तन्मा प्रसाङ्क्षीस्तत्त्वा मा प्रधाक्षीरिति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: atha haṃsā niśāyām atipetus tad dhaivaṃ haṃso haṃsam abhyuvāda ho ho 'yi bhallākṣa bhallākṣa jānaśruteḥ pautrāyaṇasya samaṃ divā jyotir ātataṃ tan mā prasāṅkṣīs tat tvā mā pradhākṣīr iti || 2 ||
verses.trans: Now one night some swans flew past, and one swan said to another: 'Ho there, short-sighted one! The light of Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa has spread like the sky. Do not touch it, lest it burn you!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The swans — sages or gods in bird form, says Śaṅkara — speak so the king may overhear. His 'light' is the lustre of merit; the dialogue is staged by providence to provoke his aspiration.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord employs even birds as messengers of grace; praise of the king's light is the bait that will draw him to a true knower.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Divine beings in swan form execute the Lord's plan, honouring merit while preparing its transcendence.

verses.dev: तमु ह परः प्रत्युवाच कम्वर एनमेतत्सन्तं सयुग्वानमिव रैक्वमात्थेति यो नु कथं सयुग्वा रैक्व इति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tam u ha paraḥ pratyuvāca kam vara enam etat santaṃ sayugvānam iva raikvam āttheti yo nu kathaṃ sayugvā raikva iti || 3 ||
verses.trans: The other replied: 'Come now — who is this fellow of whom you speak as if he were Raikva of the cart?' 'And what sort of man is this Raikva of the cart?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The swan's retort dethrones royal merit before the silent knower: all the king's glory does not compare with Raikva. Knowledge outshines works.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: True greatness lies hidden with the Lord's knower, not in public charity; the swan redirects the king's gaze.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The hierarchy of worth is divinely proclaimed: the knower of the Lord ranks above the wealthiest giver.

verses.dev: यथा कृतायविजितायाधरेयाः संयन्त्येवमेनं सर्वं तदभिसमैति यत्किञ्च प्रजाः साधु कुर्वन्ति यस्तद्वेद यत्स वेद स मयैतदुक्त इति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yathā kṛtāya vijitāyādhareyāḥ saṃyanty evam enaṃ sarvaṃ tad abhisamaiti yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti yas tad veda yat sa veda sa mayaitad ukta iti || 4 ||
verses.trans: 'As the lower throws of the dice all pass to the winner of the kṛta (the highest throw), so whatever good people do, all of it accrues to him. He who knows what Raikva knows — it is of him I speak.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara's celebrated image: as all lesser dice-throws are contained in the highest, all merits are subsumed in the knower of Brahman. Knowledge gathers and surpasses every good work.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: All virtue ripens toward and is credited to the one who knows the Lord; the knower is the focal point of the world's goodness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord assigns the fruit of scattered merits to His knower; knowledge of Him is the kṛta-throw that wins all.

verses.dev: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायण उपशुश्राव स ह संजिहान एव क्षत्तारमुवाचाङ्गारे ह सयुग्वानमिव रैक्वमात्थेति यो नु कथं सयुग्वा रैक्व इति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tad u ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇa upaśuśrāva sa ha saṃjihāna eva kṣattāram uvācāṅgāre ha sayugvānam iva raikvam āttheti yo nu kathaṃ sayugvā raikva iti || 5 ||
verses.trans: Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa overheard this. Rising at once, he said to his attendant: 'Friend, you speak of me as if I were Raikva of the cart!' — 'And what sort of man is this Raikva of the cart?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The king, stung awake, repeats the swan's words — the first sign of the humility that qualifies one for knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Grace works: the king cannot rest until he finds the man greater than himself.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Hearing of a superior, the king seeks him — the right response to revealed hierarchy.

verses.dev: यथा कृतायविजितायाधरेयाः संयन्त्येवमेनं सर्वं तदभिसमैति यत्किञ्च प्रजाः साधु कुर्वन्ति यस्तद्वेद यत्स वेद स मयैतदुक्त इति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: yathā kṛtāya vijitāyādhareyāḥ saṃyanty evam enaṃ sarvaṃ tad abhisamaiti yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti yas tad veda yat sa veda sa mayaitad ukta iti || 6 ||
verses.trans: (The attendant repeated:) 'As the lower throws of the dice all pass to the winner of the kṛta, so whatever good people do, all of it accrues to him. He who knows what Raikva knows — it is of him I speak.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The teaching is repeated verbatim — scripture's way of fixing the dice-image in the seeker's mind.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Repetition confirms the doctrine: the knower absorbs all merit.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The restatement certifies the truth the king must act upon.

verses.dev: स ह क्षत्तान्विष्य नाविदमिति प्रत्येयाय तं होवाच यत्रारे ब्राह्मणस्यान्वेषणा तदेनमर्च्छेति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: sa ha kṣattānviṣya nāvidam iti pratyeyāya taṃ hovāca yatrāre brāhmaṇasyānveṣaṇā tad enam arccheti || 7 ||
verses.trans: The attendant searched and returned saying, 'I did not find him.' The king said: 'Where one searches for a knower of Brahman — there seek him (in solitude, in the wilds).'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Knowers of Brahman are not found in palaces but where the world does not look — the king himself supplies the clue, showing his ripening discernment.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord's men dwell hidden; the seeker must look with reverence, away from pomp.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The true knower shuns display; he is sought where worldly eyes do not go.

verses.dev: सोऽधस्ताच्छकटस्य पामानं कषमाणमुपोपविवेश तं हाभ्युवाद त्वं नु भगवः सयुग्वा रैक्व इत्यहं ह्यरा३ इति ह प्रतिजज्ञे स ह क्षत्ताविदमिति प्रत्येयाय ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: so 'dhastāc chakaṭasya pāmānaṃ kaṣamāṇam upopaviveśa taṃ hābhyuvāda tvaṃ nu bhagavaḥ sayugvā raikva ity ahaṃ hy arā3 iti ha pratijajñe sa ha kṣattāvidam iti pratyeyāya || 8 ||
verses.trans: He found him beneath a cart, scratching his itch, and sat down near him, saying: 'Are you, revered sir, Raikva of the cart?' — 'Indeed I am!' he acknowledged. The attendant returned, saying: 'I have found him.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The greatest knower appears as the least of men — itching under a cart. Śaṅkara: outward state reveals nothing of inner attainment; the world's judgments are inverted in the realm of knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord hides His jewels in plain clay; reverence ('bhagavaḥ') is rightly offered despite appearances.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Raikva's lowly seat conceals exalted knowledge — rank before God owes nothing to circumstance.

verses.dev: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायणः षट्शतानि गवां निष्कमश्वतरीरथं तदादाय प्रतिचक्रमे तं हाभ्युवाद ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tad u ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇaḥ ṣaṭśatāni gavāṃ niṣkam aśvatarīrathaṃ tad ādāya praticakrame taṃ hābhyuvāda || 1 ||
verses.trans: Then Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa took six hundred cows, a gold necklace, and a chariot drawn by mules, and went back to him, saying:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The king brings wealth to buy what wealth cannot buy — the first offering will be refused, teaching that knowledge is not merchandise.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Gifts express the disciple's earnestness, yet the teacher waits for the offering of self.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Material gifts alone cannot win the vidyā; the teacher tests the seeker's totality.

verses.dev: रैक्वेमानि षट्शतानि गवामयं निष्कोऽयमश्वतरीरथोऽनु म एतां भगवो देवतां शाधि यां देवतामुपास्स इति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: raikvemāni ṣaṭśatāni gavām ayaṃ niṣko 'yam aśvatarīratho 'nu ma etāṃ bhagavo devatāṃ śādhi yāṃ devatām upāssa iti || 2 ||
verses.trans: 'Raikva, here are six hundred cows, this necklace, this mule-chariot. Now teach me, revered sir, the deity you worship.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The request is right, but the approach is mercantile; the teacher's rebuff will purify it.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: He asks for the upāsya-devatā — the proper question; the manner must still ripen into surrender.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The seeker asks for the deity of meditation; teaching awaits fuller humility.

verses.dev: तमु ह परः प्रत्युवाचाह हारेत्वा शूद्र तवैव सह गोभिरस्त्विति तदु ह पुनरेव जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायणः सहस्रं गवां निष्कमश्वतरीरथं दुहितरं तदादाय प्रतिचक्रमे ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tam u ha paraḥ pratyuvācāha hāretvā śūdra tavaiva saha gobhir astv iti tad u ha punar eva jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇaḥ sahasraṃ gavāṃ niṣkam aśvatarīrathaṃ duhitaraṃ tad ādāya praticakrame || 3 ||
verses.trans: But the other replied: 'Ah, take them away, śūdra! Keep the necklace and chariot together with the cows.' Then Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa came back again with a thousand cows, the necklace, the mule-chariot, and his daughter.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains 'śūdra' not literally but as 'one melting with grief (śuc)' or one approaching knowledge like a śūdra — with goods instead of qualification. The rebuke burns away the king's transactional spirit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The harsh word tests and humbles; the king's return with his dearest — his daughter — shows surrender deepening into totality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the rebuke as gauging the seeker's perseverance; true desire for the Lord's knowledge survives insult.

verses.dev: तं हाभ्युवाद रैक्वेदं सहस्रं गवामयं निष्कोऽयमश्वतरीरथ इयं जायेयं ग्रामो यस्मिन्नास्सेऽन्वेव मा भगवः शाधीति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ hābhyuvāda raikvedaṃ sahasraṃ gavām ayaṃ niṣko 'yam aśvatarīratha iyaṃ jāyeyaṃ grāmo yasminn āsse 'nv eva mā bhagavaḥ śādhīti || 4 ||
verses.trans: He said to him: 'Raikva, here are a thousand cows, this necklace, this mule-chariot, this maiden for a wife, and this village in which you sit. Now, revered sir, teach me!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Now everything is laid down — wealth, kin, dominion. When nothing is withheld, the disciple is ready; the gift of all signifies the gift of self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Complete surrender (ātma-nikṣepa) at the teacher's feet is the gateway of grace; the Lord's knower now consents.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Total offering demonstrates fitness; the teacher, seeing it, will impart the saving doctrine.

verses.dev: तस्या ह मुखमुपोद्गृह्णन्नुवाचाजहारेमाः शूद्रानेनैव मुखेनालापयिष्यथा इति ते हैते रैक्वपर्णा नाम महावृषेषु यत्रास्मा उवास स तस्मै होवाच ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tasyā ha mukham upodgṛhṇann uvācājahāremāḥ śūdrānenaiva mukhenālāpayiṣyathā iti te haite raikvaparṇā nāma mahāvṛṣeṣu yatrāsmā uvāsa sa tasmai hovāca || 5 ||
verses.trans: Raising her face toward him, Raikva said: 'He has brought all these, śūdra; by this face alone you would have made me speak!' Those villages among the Mahāvṛṣas where he lived are called Raikvaparṇa. And he said to him:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The maiden's face — the emblem of the king's total relinquishment — unlocks the teaching. Śaṅkara: it is the completeness of surrender, not the items given, that 'makes the teacher speak'.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Acceptance of the bride seals the bond of teacher and disciple as kin; grace now flows freely.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The teacher accepts the offering as the Lord's arrangement and proceeds to instruct the now-qualified king.

verses.dev: वायुर्वाव संवर्गो यदा वा अग्निरुद्वायति वायुमेवाप्येति यदा सूर्योऽस्तमेति वायुमेवाप्येति यदा चन्द्रोऽस्तमेति वायुमेवाप्येति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: vāyur vāva saṃvargo yadā vā agnir udvāyati vāyum evāpyeti yadā sūryo 'stam eti vāyum evāpyeti yadā candro 'stam eti vāyum evāpyeti || 1 ||
verses.trans: The wind, verily, is the absorber (saṃvarga). When fire goes out, it passes into the wind; when the sun sets, it passes into the wind; when the moon sets, it passes into the wind.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Raikva's doctrine: the wind gathers the cosmic gods into itself — a meditation on the One into which the many resolve, foreshadowing the dissolution of all into Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Vāyu's absorbing power is the Lord's own gathering energy at work in the cosmos; meditation on it honours His sustaining-and-withdrawing play.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: For Madhva, Vāyu is the Lord's foremost agent: the gods' resolution into him at their setting displays his unique rank under Viṣṇu.

verses.dev: यदाप उच्छुष्यन्ति वायुमेवापियन्ति वायुर्ह्येवैतान्सर्वान्संवृङ्क्ते इत्यधिदैवतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: yadāpa ucchuṣyanti vāyum evāpiyanti vāyur hy evaitān sarvān saṃvṛṅkte ity adhidaivatam || 2 ||
